english_abstract:
Abstract

Background: Non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) is the most severe form of male factor infertility, affecting approximately 10–15% of azoospermic men. Microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) is the gold-standard surgical intervention, yet success rates vary between 30–60%, and no validated, comprehensive preoperative prediction tool exists. This study aimed to develop and validate a machine learning (ML) framework for predicting micro-TESE outcomes with emphasis on explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) and clinical translation.

Methods: A retrospective cohort of 2,413 NOA patients (2,450 screened; 37 excluded) who underwent micro-TESE at the Royan Institute, Tehran, Iran (2007–2022) was analyzed. Fifty-five raw variables were expanded to 73 features through a custom natural language processing pipeline that parsed free-text bilateral pathology reports into 16 quantitative histopathological indicators and 2 cohort metadata flags. Sixteen ML algorithms were benchmarked using 5×5 nested cross-validation with 50-iteration Bayesian hyperparameter search per model. RobustScaler standardization, k-nearest neighbor imputation with missingness indicators, and SMOTE were applied strictly within cross-validation folds. Performance was evaluated using AUC, calibration (Brier score), and decision curve analysis (DCA). TreeSHAP, LIME, and partial dependence plots provided multi-method explainability. The study adhered to TRIPOD+AI 2024 and PROBAST+AI 2025 standards.

Results: The sperm retrieval success rate was 36.7% (886/2,413). CatBoost was the champion model with AUC 0.8306 (95% CI: 0.823–0.845), accuracy 0.769, sensitivity 0.678, specificity 0.822, PPV 0.688, NPV 0.815, and F1-score 0.683. Global SHAP analysis identified luteinizing hormone (LH; mean |SHAP| = 0.317), age (0.270), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH; 0.250), and testosterone (0.212) as the top predictors. Cross-method concordance confirmed identical top-four rankings across SHAP, LIME, and PDP in 100% of 200 bootstrap replicates. DCA demonstrated positive net benefit across threshold probabilities 0.05–0.60, with the counseling window of 0.20–0.40 yielding 5–19 fewer unnecessary interventions per 100 patients. A clinical decision support system (CDSS) prototype incorporating 11 clinically governed features was designed.

Conclusion: This study presents the largest ML-based benchmark for micro-TESE prediction, demonstrating that a leakage-controlled CatBoost model achieves clinically useful discrimination (AUC 0.8306) with robust calibration and measurable clinical utility. Multi-method XAI confirmed that hormonal axes—particularly the gonadotropin–testosterone pathway—dominate predictive importance with biologically coherent explanations. The integrated framework establishes a reproducible paradigm for evidence-based preoperative counseling in male infertility.

Keywords: Male Infertility; Azoospermia; Machine Learning; Testicular Sperm Extraction; Explainable Artificial Intelligence
persian_abstract:
چکیده

زمینه و هدف: آزواسپرمی غیرانسدادی (NOA) شدیدترین شکل ناباروری مردانه است که تقریباً ۱۰ تا ۱۵ درصد مردان مبتلا به آزواسپرمی و حدود ۱ درصد کل مردان جهان را تحت تأثیر قرار می‌دهد. استخراج اسپرم از بافت بیضه با میکرودیسکسیون (micro-TESE) روش استاندارد طلایی جراحی برای بازیابی اسپرم در این بیماران محسوب می‌شود؛ با این حال، میزان موفقیت این روش به‌طور قابل‌توجهی متغیر بوده (۳۰ تا ۶۰ درصد) و هیچ پارامتر بالینی، هورمونی یا هیستوپاتولوژیک منفردی نمی‌تواند نتیجه را به‌طور قابل اطمینانی پیش‌بینی کند. عدم وجود یک ابزار پیش‌بینی پیش از عمل جامع، معتبر و قابل تفسیر، پزشکان و بیماران را در هنگام تصمیم‌گیری برای انجام این جراحی تهاجمی بدون حمایت کافی تصمیم‌گیری باقی می‌گذارد. هدف این مطالعه توسعه و اعتبارسنجی یک چارچوب جامع یادگیری ماشین (ML) برای پیش‌بینی بازیابی موفق اسپرم در بیماران NOA تحت micro-TESE با تأکید بر هوش مصنوعی قابل تفسیر (XAI) و ترجمه بالینی بود.

روش‌ها: یک کوهورت گذشته‌نگر شامل ۲,۴۱۳ بیمار NOA (از ۲,۴۵۰ غربال‌شده؛ ۳۷ نفر به‌دلیل فقدان پیامد اولیه حذف شدند) که در پژوهشگاه رویان علوم تولید مثل زیست‌پزشکی، تهران، ایران (۲۰۰۷–۲۰۲۲) تحت micro-TESE قرار گرفته بودند، تحلیل شد. پنجاه‌وپنج متغیر بالینی خام از طریق یک خط لوله پردازش زبان طبیعی (NLP) اختصاصی که گزارش‌های پاتولوژی متنی دوطرفه را به ۱۶ شاخص کمّی هیستوپاتولوژیک و ۲ پرچم فراداده کوهورت تبدیل کرد، به یک ماتریس تحلیلی ۷۳ ویژگی‌ای گسترش یافت. شانزده الگوریتم ML شامل تقویت گرادیان، روش‌های ترکیبی، خطی، درخت‌محور، شبکه عصبی، مبتنی بر فاصله، هسته‌ای و احتمالی با استفاده از طرح اعتبارسنجی متقاطع تو‌در‌توی ۵×۵ با ۵۰ تکرار جستجوی بیزی فراپارامتر به ازای هر مدل مقایسه شدند. استانداردسازی RobustScaler، جایگذاری k-نزدیک‌ترین همسایه با شاخص‌های گمشدگی و روش SMOTE به‌طور صرف درون چین‌های اعتبارسنجی متقاطع اعمال شدند تا از نشت داده جلوگیری شود. عملکرد مدل با سطح زیر منحنی مشخصه عملکرد گیرنده (AUC)، کالیبراسیون (نمره بریر)، و تحلیل منحنی تصمیم (DCA) ارزیابی شد. سه روش مکمل XAI شامل TreeSHAP، LIME و نمودارهای وابستگی جزئی (PDP) برای تفسیرپذیری سراسری و محلی به‌کار گرفته شدند. این مطالعه از استانداردهای گزارش‌دهی TRIPOD+AI 2024 و PROBAST+AI 2025 پیروی کرد.

یافته‌ها: میزان کلی موفقیت بازیابی اسپرم ۳۶.۷ درصد (۸۸۶ از ۲,۴۱۳) بود. مدل CatBoost به‌عنوان بهترین مدل با AUC برابر ۰.۸۳۰۶ (فاصله اطمینان ۹۵ درصد: ۰.۸۲۳–۰.۸۴۵)، دقت ۰.۷۶۹، حساسیت ۰.۶۷۸، ویژگی ۰.۸۲۲، ارزش اخباری مثبت ۰.۶۸۸، ارزش اخباری منفی ۰.۸۱۵ و نمره F1 برابر ۰.۶۸۳ شناسایی شد. تحلیل SHAP سراسری هورمون لوتئینیزان (LH؛ میانگین |SHAP| = ۰.۳۱۷)، سن بیمار (۰.۲۷۰)، هورمون محرک فولیکول (FSH؛ ۰.۲۵۰) و تستوسترون (۰.۲۱۲) را به‌عنوان چهار پیش‌بین تأثیرگذار برتر شناسایی کرد. تحلیل هماهنگی بین‌روشی تأیید کرد که رتبه‌بندی چهار ویژگی برتر در ۱۰۰ درصد از ۲۰۰ تکرار بوت‌استرپ در هر سه روش SHAP، LIME و PDP یکسان بود. تحلیل منحنی تصمیم نشان داد که مدل در محدوده آستانه‌های احتمالی ۰.۰۵ تا ۰.۶۰ سود خالص مثبتی نسبت به راهبردهای درمان‌همه و درمان‌هیچ‌کس دارد و پنجره مشاوره بالینی ۰.۲۰ تا ۰.۴۰ تخمین زده شد که ۵ تا ۱۹ مداخله غیرضروری کمتر به ازای هر ۱۰۰ بیمار حاصل شود. یک نمونه اولیه سیستم پشتیبانی تصمیم بالینی (CDSS) شامل ۱۱ ویژگی بالینی با محدوده‌های مرجع، منطق اعتبارسنجی رنگ‌کد و توضیحات SHAP بیمارمحور طراحی شد.

نتیجه‌گیری: این مطالعه بزرگ‌ترین محک‌زنی مبتنی بر ML برای پیش‌بینی بازیابی اسپرم در micro-TESE تاکنون را ارائه می‌دهد و نشان می‌دهد که مدل CatBoost کنترل‌شده از نظر نشت داده، تمایز بالینی مفیدی (AUC = ۰.۸۳۰۶) با کالیبراسیون مستحکم و سودمندی بالینی قابل‌اندازه‌گیری به دست می‌آورد. XAI چندروشه تأیید کرد که محورهای هورمونی—به‌ویژه مسیر گنادوتروپین–تستوسترون—بر اهمیت پیش‌بینی غالب هستند و توضیحات منسجم زیست‌شناختی و قابل تفسیر بالینی فراهم می‌کنند. چارچوب یکپارچه—شامل روش‌شناسی دقیق ML، XAI شفاف و CDSS ترجمه‌ای—الگویی تکرارپذیر برای مشاوره پیش از عمل مبتنی بر شواهد در ناباروری مردانه ایجاد می‌کند.

کلیدواژه‌ها: ناباروری مردانه؛ آزواسپرمی؛ یادگیری ماشین؛ استخراج اسپرم از بیضه؛ هوش مصنوعی قابل تفسیر

chapter_1_introduction:
Chapter 1: Introduction

1.1 Problem Statement and Background

1.1.1 Male Infertility: A Global Health Burden

Infertility is a condition of the reproductive system defined by the failure to achieve a clinical pregnancy after twelve months or more of regular, unprotected sexual intercourse [1]. It affects an estimated 48.5 million couples worldwide and has been recognized by the World Health Organization (WHO) as a significant global public health concern [2]. Although historically the clinical and research focus has been disproportionately placed on female factors, contemporary evidence indicates that male factor infertility contributes to approximately 50% of all infertility cases, either as the sole etiology or as a contributing factor alongside female pathology [3]. Among men presenting for fertility evaluation, an estimated 7–10% of the male population of reproductive age is affected by some degree of subfertility or infertility [4]. The consequences extend far beyond the biomedical domain: male infertility is associated with profound psychosocial distress, relationship strain, diminished quality of life, and substantial economic burden through both direct medical costs and lost productivity [5].

The diagnostic evaluation of male infertility centers on the semen analysis, which remains the cornerstone laboratory investigation recommended by international guidelines including those of the WHO, the American Urological Association (AUA), and the European Association of Urology (EAU) [6]. Abnormal semen parameters—oligozoospermia (low sperm concentration), asthenozoospermia (poor motility), teratozoospermia (abnormal morphology), and their combinations—account for the majority of identifiable male factor diagnoses. However, the most severe end of the male infertility spectrum is azoospermia, defined as the complete absence of spermatozoa in the ejaculate on at least two separate semen analyses following centrifugation [7]. Azoospermia is identified in approximately 10–15% of infertile men and in roughly 1% of the general male population, making it a relatively common yet clinically devastating diagnosis [8].

Azoospermia is etiologically classified into two principal categories: obstructive azoospermia (OA) and non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA). Obstructive azoospermia results from mechanical blockage of the male reproductive tract—at the level of the epididymis, vas deferens, or ejaculatory ducts—in the setting of preserved spermatogenesis. OA is generally amenable to surgical reconstruction or straightforward sperm retrieval procedures, with high success rates [9]. In contrast, non-obstructive azoospermia arises from intrinsic testicular failure, reflecting a fundamental impairment of spermatogenesis at the testicular level. NOA is the more common subtype, accounting for approximately 60% of all azoospermia cases, and represents the most severe phenotype of male factor infertility [10]. The underlying pathophysiology is heterogeneous, encompassing a wide range of genetic, hormonal, developmental, toxic, and idiopathic etiologies. Chromosomal abnormalities—most notably Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY)—are identified in approximately 10–15% of NOA patients, while Y-chromosome microdeletions in the azoospermia factor (AZF) regions account for an additional 5–10% [11]. Hormonal derangements involving the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, including elevated gonadotropins (FSH and LH) with low testosterone, further characterize the endocrine milieu of testicular failure. Additional etiologies include cryptorchidism, varicocele, prior gonadotoxic chemotherapy or radiation, testicular torsion, and environmental or occupational exposures, though in a substantial proportion of cases (approximately 30–40%), no identifiable cause is established, and the diagnosis remains idiopathic [12].

The histopathological spectrum of NOA is likewise diverse. At one extreme, Sertoli cell-only syndrome (SCO) describes seminiferous tubules populated exclusively by Sertoli cells with complete absence of germ cells. Maturation arrest (MA)—which may occur at the spermatogonial, spermatocytic, round spermatid, or elongated spermatid stages—represents an intermediate phenotype in which germ cell development is initiated but arrested at a specific differentiation stage. Hypospermatogenesis refers to a generalized quantitative reduction in germ cell production across the seminiferous epithelium, while the presence of complete spermatogenesis with tubular heterogeneity (CSTH) in some tubules indicates that foci of active sperm production persist amidst otherwise impaired parenchyma [13]. Critically, testicular histopathology in NOA is frequently heterogeneous both within and between testes: a single patient may harbor tubules exhibiting SCO, MA, hypospermatogenesis, and CSTH in different regions of the same testis. This intratesticular heterogeneity is the biological foundation upon which surgical sperm retrieval is predicated—if even isolated foci of spermatogenesis persist, it may be technically possible to retrieve mature spermatozoa for use in assisted reproduction [14].

1.1.2 Microdissection Testicular Sperm Extraction: The Gold Standard

For men with NOA, microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) has emerged as the gold-standard surgical intervention for sperm retrieval. First described by Schlegel in 1999, micro-TESE employs an operating microscope to systematically examine the seminiferous tubules of the testis under high magnification, selectively extracting larger, more opaque tubules that are more likely to contain active spermatogenesis [15]. Compared with conventional testicular sperm extraction (c-TESE), which relies on random multi-site biopsies, micro-TESE offers several advantages: superior sperm retrieval rates, reduced tissue destruction, lower complication rates including postoperative hypogonadism, and the ability to identify focal islands of spermatogenesis that would be missed by blind sampling techniques [16].

Contemporary series report micro-TESE sperm retrieval rates (SRR) ranging from approximately 30% to 60%, with the wide variability attributable to differences in patient selection criteria, surgeon experience, institutional protocols, histopathological case mix, and the definition of successful retrieval [17]. In the largest series, SRR for specific histopathological subtypes vary considerably: patients with hypospermatogenesis achieve retrieval rates exceeding 70%, those with late maturation arrest approximately 50–60%, early maturation arrest 20–40%, and Sertoli cell-only syndrome approximately 20–30%, though even in the latter category, focal spermatogenesis sufficient for retrieval is identified in a nontrivial proportion of cases [18]. Following successful sperm retrieval, the extracted spermatozoa are used for intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI), which has revolutionized the treatment of severe male factor infertility and provided the only reliable pathway to biological fatherhood for men with NOA [19].

1.1.3 The Clinical Dilemma: An Invasive Procedure with Uncertain Outcome

Despite its transformative potential, micro-TESE presents a fundamental clinical dilemma. The procedure is invasive, requiring general anesthesia and surgical exploration of testicular tissue. It carries nontrivial risks including pain, hematoma formation, testicular atrophy, and transient or permanent decline in serum testosterone levels [20]. The financial burden is substantial, encompassing not only the surgical procedure itself but also the ancillary costs of preoperative evaluation, anesthesia, andrology laboratory processing, and subsequent ICSI cycles. The emotional toll on patients and their partners is considerable, as couples invest substantial hope, time, and resources in a procedure whose outcome cannot be reliably predicted in advance [21].

The core challenge is that micro-TESE outcomes are binary and high-stakes: either spermatozoa are successfully retrieved, enabling the possibility of biological fatherhood through ICSI, or the procedure fails to retrieve sperm, leaving the couple to consider alternative family-building options such as donor sperm or adoption. With an overall failure rate of approximately 40–65% depending on the cohort, a large proportion of men who undergo this invasive procedure do so without ultimately achieving sperm retrieval [22]. This reality underscores the critical unmet need for accurate, reliable, and clinically interpretable preoperative prediction tools that can stratify patients according to their likelihood of successful retrieval, thereby supporting informed shared decision-making between clinicians and patients before commitment to surgery.

1.2 Significance and Importance of the Study

1.2.1 The Multidimensional Burden on Patients

The decision to undergo micro-TESE is one of the most consequential in reproductive medicine from the patient's perspective. The physical burden includes surgical risks, recovery time, and potential long-term hormonal consequences. The emotional burden is amplified by the uncertainty of outcome—patients and their partners report high levels of anxiety, depression, and relationship stress during the period leading up to and following the procedure [23]. The financial burden is likewise substantial: in many healthcare systems, micro-TESE and subsequent ICSI cycles are not fully covered by insurance, and out-of-pocket costs can reach tens of thousands of dollars per attempt, with no guarantee of success [24]. For patients in whom the procedure fails, the accumulated physical, emotional, and financial investment represents a profound loss, compounded by the need to grieve the potential loss of biological parenthood.

An accurate preoperative prediction tool would fundamentally alter this calculus by providing patients and clinicians with individualized, evidence-based probability estimates of surgical success. Patients predicted to have a high likelihood of sperm retrieval could proceed with greater confidence and realistic expectations. Conversely, patients with very low predicted probabilities could be counseled about the realistic chances of success, enabling them to make fully informed decisions about whether to proceed with surgery or to consider alternative reproductive options earlier in their treatment pathway. Such a tool would reduce unnecessary surgical interventions, decrease the aggregate burden of failed procedures, and improve the allocation of finite surgical and laboratory resources [25].

1.2.2 Limitations of Current Clinical Predictors

Decades of clinical research have attempted to identify preoperative predictors of micro-TESE success from the standard diagnostic workup. The most extensively studied biomarkers include serum hormonal parameters—follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), luteinizing hormone (LH), total testosterone, inhibin B, anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), and estradiol (E2)—as well as derived hormonal ratios such as testosterone-to-LH and testosterone-to-FSH [26]. Clinical and demographic variables including patient age, body mass index (BMI), testicular volume (assessed by physical examination or ultrasonography), and genetic markers (karyotype, Y-chromosome microdeletion status) have also been investigated [27]. Additionally, histopathological findings from diagnostic testicular biopsy, when available, provide information about the predominant pattern of seminiferous tubule pathology [28].

Despite this extensive predictor inventory, no single biomarker or simple combination of clinical variables has achieved sufficient discrimination to serve as a reliable standalone predictor. FSH, the most widely studied individual hormone, demonstrates moderate correlation with spermatogenic status but has insufficient sensitivity and specificity for clinical decision-making when used in isolation—numerous studies have shown that even markedly elevated FSH levels do not preclude successful sperm retrieval, and conversely, normal FSH does not guarantee it [29]. Testicular volume, while correlated with the quantity of spermatogenic tissue, similarly lacks the discriminatory power necessary for individual-level prediction. Histopathological assessment from diagnostic biopsy, though informative, is limited by sampling error—particularly in the setting of the intratesticular heterogeneity that characterizes NOA—and is not always performed as part of the preoperative evaluation [30].

Traditional statistical approaches, principally multivariable logistic regression, have been employed to combine these predictors into composite models or nomograms. While these efforts have produced some useful prognostic tools, they are inherently limited by assumptions of linearity and additivity in the relationship between predictors and outcomes, difficulty in capturing complex nonlinear interactions among variables, and sensitivity to multicollinearity among correlated hormonal markers [31]. The result is a clinical landscape in which preoperative counseling relies heavily on clinical experience and general population-level statistics rather than individualized, data-driven risk estimates—a gap that machine learning methods are uniquely positioned to address.

1.2.3 The Research Gap

A systematic assessment of the existing literature—including the scoping review conducted by Jamalirad et al. (2025) [32] and the state-of-the-art review by Kandil et al. (2026) [33]—reveals that despite growing interest in machine learning for micro-TESE prediction, no validated, comprehensive, and leakage-controlled ML-based prediction tool exists that simultaneously addresses the following requirements: (a) a large, representative patient cohort sufficient to capture the heterogeneity of NOA phenotypes; (b) a comprehensive benchmark of multiple ML algorithm families with rigorous hyperparameter optimization; (c) strict data leakage prevention through nested cross-validation with all preprocessing steps (imputation, scaling, oversampling) applied within folds; (d) integration of structured features derived from free-text bilateral histopathology reports through natural language processing; (e) multi-method explainability using complementary XAI techniques; (f) formal model calibration assessment and decision curve analysis to quantify clinical utility; and (g) a translational clinical decision support system specification bridging the gap from model to bedside. The present study was designed to address each of these gaps simultaneously within a unified methodological framework.



The potential clinical impact of an accurate prediction tool can be quantified through decision curve analysis (DCA), a methodological framework that evaluates the net benefit of a prediction model across a range of clinically relevant threshold probabilities. Unlike traditional discrimination metrics such as AUC, which summarize overall model performance without reference to clinical consequences, DCA explicitly incorporates the relative harms and benefits of different decision thresholds, providing a direct measure of how many patients would benefit from model-guided decision-making compared with default strategies of treating all patients or treating none [25]. In the context of micro-TESE, DCA can estimate the number of unnecessary surgical interventions that could be avoided by using a prediction model to identify patients with very low retrieval probabilities, while simultaneously ensuring that patients with high retrieval likelihood are not inappropriately deterred from surgery. This framework bridges the gap between statistical model performance and clinical actionability, and is increasingly recognized as an essential component of clinical prediction model evaluation alongside discrimination and calibration assessments.

Furthermore, model calibration—the agreement between predicted probabilities and observed outcome frequencies—is a critical but frequently overlooked dimension of prediction model performance. A well-discriminating model that is poorly calibrated may systematically overestimate or underestimate risk, leading to inappropriate clinical decisions despite achieving a high AUC. Formal calibration assessment through reliability diagrams and the Brier score, complemented by post-hoc recalibration techniques such as Platt scaling, ensures that the predicted probabilities transmitted to clinicians and patients are trustworthy and actionable. The present study incorporates both DCA and formal calibration assessment as integral components of the evaluation framework, reflecting contemporary best practices in clinical prediction model research.

1.3 Artificial Intelligence in Reproductive Medicine

1.3.1 A Brief History of Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Medicine

The application of artificial intelligence and machine learning to clinical medicine has evolved substantially over the past four decades. Early expert systems of the 1970s and 1980s, such as MYCIN for antibiotic selection and INTERNIST-1 for general internal medicine diagnosis, demonstrated the feasibility of encoding medical knowledge into computational systems but were limited by brittle rule sets and the knowledge acquisition bottleneck [34]. The resurgence of neural networks in the 1990s and 2000s, coupled with the exponential growth of digitized medical data through electronic health records (EHR), medical imaging repositories, and genomic databases, created the foundation for a new era of data-driven clinical prediction [35].

The contemporary ML toolkit for clinical prediction encompasses a diverse array of algorithmic families. Linear models, including logistic regression with regularization variants (ridge, lasso, elastic net), remain foundational for their interpretability and well-characterized statistical properties. Tree-based methods—decision trees, random forests, and gradient boosting machines (GBM) including XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost—have demonstrated particular strength on tabular clinical data, offering native handling of mixed feature types, robustness to outliers, and the ability to capture nonlinear interactions without explicit specification [36]. Ensemble methods that combine multiple base learners through bagging, boosting, or stacking further improve predictive performance and stability. Neural network architectures, ranging from simple multilayer perceptrons (MLP) to deep learning models, provide flexible function approximation but typically require larger datasets and offer less transparent decision processes. Distance-based methods (k-nearest neighbors), kernel methods (support vector machines), and probabilistic classifiers (naive Bayes) round out the standard algorithmic repertoire [37].

In the broader context of clinical medicine, ML-based prediction models have achieved notable success across diverse applications: cardiovascular risk stratification, sepsis early warning systems, diabetic retinopathy screening, cancer prognosis, intensive care unit mortality prediction, and hospital readmission risk, among many others [38]. The US Food and Drug Administration has authorized hundreds of ML-based medical devices, and regulatory frameworks continue to evolve to accommodate the unique characteristics of learning algorithms. However, the translation of ML models from development to clinical deployment remains a persistent challenge, with the vast majority of published clinical ML models never achieving routine clinical use—a phenomenon termed the "last mile" problem in clinical AI [39].

1.3.2 Current Applications of Artificial Intelligence in Reproductive Medicine

The field of reproductive medicine has witnessed increasing integration of AI and ML technologies across multiple domains. In female reproductive medicine, ML models have been applied to predict outcomes of in vitro fertilization (IVF) and ICSI cycles, assess embryo quality and viability through automated morphokinetic analysis, optimize ovarian stimulation protocols, and predict ovarian reserve decline [40]. Computer-assisted sperm analysis (CASA) systems represent one of the earliest applications of automated image analysis in andrology, providing standardized assessment of sperm concentration, motility, and morphological parameters [41].

In the specific context of male infertility and azoospermia, ML applications have expanded in recent years. The prediction of sperm retrieval outcomes in surgical extraction procedures has been the subject of growing research interest, with studies exploring gradient-boosted trees [42], random forests [43], logistic regression nomograms [44], AutoML approaches [45], and multi-center nomogram development [46]. The diversity of approaches reflects both the promise of ML for this clinical problem and the lack of methodological consensus on optimal model development, validation, and reporting practices.

Beyond outcome prediction, AI has been applied to real-time intraoperative assistance during TESE procedures—notably, deep learning "co-pilot" systems for rare sperm identification in tissue samples [47]—as well as to semen quality assessment, varicocele management optimization, and the integration of multi-omics data for male infertility classification [48]. The scoping review by Jamalirad et al. comprehensively mapped the landscape of AI predictive models and advancements in micro-TESE for NOA, identifying fourteen relevant studies and highlighting critical gaps in sample size, validation rigor, explainability, and clinical translation [32].



Among the most instructive precedents in the NOA prediction literature, several studies merit detailed examination as they collectively define the methodological landscape against which the present work is positioned. Zeadna et al. (2020) published one of the earliest ML applications to sperm retrieval prediction, comparing gradient-boosted trees with logistic regression on 119 patients and reporting an AUC of 0.807 with high sensitivity (91%) but low specificity (51%) [42]. Notably, this study employed conventional TESE rather than micro-TESE, limiting its applicability to the microdissection context. Bachelot et al. (2023) extended the algorithmic comparison to eight ML models on 201 patients with temporal validation on 26 prospective cases, identifying inhibin B and varicocele history as the strongest predictors and achieving AUC 0.900 with Random Forest [43]. Kobayashi et al. (2023) applied AutoML to 450 patients, reporting AUC 0.725 and highlighting the testosterone-to-estradiol ratio as the most important feature [45]. Deng et al. (2023) and Liu et al. (2023) developed logistic regression nomograms on 200 and 217 patients respectively, with AUCs of 0.720 and 0.781, incorporating biomarkers including AMH, inhibin B, Klinefelter syndrome, and cryptorchidism history [44]. More recently, Ceyhan et al. (2025) constructed a multi-center nomogram from 3,093 patients using multivariate logistic regression, representing the largest sample in the literature but without ML algorithm comparison or XAI integration [46]. Cao et al. (2024) applied XGBoost with SHAP to predict clinical pregnancy following sperm retrieval and ICSI (AUC 0.825), while Xiao et al. (2025) developed a gradient-boosted decision tree nomogram achieving AUC 0.974 for differentiating NOA from obstructive azoospermia—a classification task distinct from but related to retrieval prediction. Review articles by Verratti et al. (2024), Ibañez Vazquez et al. (2026), and Kandil et al. (2025) have synthesized the evidence and unanimously called for larger cohorts, rigorous validation, multi-method explainability, and translational clinical tools [33].

A critical synthesis of these studies reveals four persistent methodological limitations that the present research was specifically designed to address. First, sample size constraints: only one study (Ceyhan et al.) exceeds 1,000 patients, and the largest pure-ML study (Kobayashi) included only 450 patients for training. Small cohorts magnify optimism bias, limit the diversity of clinical phenotypes captured by the model, and produce unstable feature importance rankings. Second, narrow algorithm benchmarking: most studies compare one to three models, providing no systematic evidence about which ML paradigm is optimal for this prediction task. Third, inconsistent leakage controls: the sequence of imputation, scaling, oversampling, and feature selection is not always performed strictly within cross-validation folds, which can produce inflated and unreproducible performance estimates. The present study discovered this challenge firsthand through a pipeline correction (v1 to v2) in which an IQR-clipping bug that introduced data leakage was identified and eliminated. Fourth, the absence of integrated clinical translation: few studies combine formal calibration assessment, decision curve analysis, multi-method XAI, and a CDSS specification within a single unified framework.

1.3.3 The Promise of Machine Learning for Clinical Prediction in Micro-TESE

Machine learning offers several theoretical and practical advantages over traditional statistical methods for predicting micro-TESE outcomes. First, ML algorithms can learn complex, nonlinear, and high-order interaction effects from data without requiring explicit a priori specification of the functional form, enabling the identification of predictive patterns that linear models may miss [49]. Second, ensemble methods and gradient boosting frameworks are particularly well-suited to the mixed-type, moderate-dimensional feature spaces that characterize clinical andrology data—combining continuous hormonal measurements, ordinal histopathological scores, categorical diagnostic codes, and binary flags within a single unified model. Third, advanced imputation strategies (such as k-nearest neighbor imputation) and oversampling techniques (such as SMOTE) can be integrated within cross-validation pipelines to handle the substantial missingness and class imbalance that are endemic to real-world clinical datasets [50]. Fourth, the nested cross-validation design provides a statistically rigorous framework for simultaneously optimizing hyperparameters and estimating generalization performance without the optimistic bias that afflicts simpler validation strategies [51].

However, the promise of ML must be tempered by an honest assessment of the challenges. Clinical datasets in reproductive medicine are often moderate in size, retrospectively collected, and subject to selection bias, missing data, and measurement variability. The risk of data leakage—wherein information from the test set inadvertently influences model training through preprocessing steps such as imputation, scaling, or feature selection performed on the entire dataset—is a pervasive and frequently underreported problem that can produce substantially inflated performance estimates [52]. The present study was explicitly designed to address this challenge through a leakage-controlled pipeline in which all preprocessing operations are performed strictly within cross-validation folds, and through transparent disclosure of a pipeline correction (v1 to v2) that eliminated a data leakage artifact identified during the research process.

1.3.4 Explainable Artificial Intelligence: A Critical Requirement for Clinical Adoption

A fundamental barrier to the clinical adoption of ML-based prediction models is the "black box" problem: many high-performing ML algorithms produce predictions without transparent reasoning, making it difficult for clinicians to understand why a particular prediction was made, whether the model's reasoning aligns with established medical knowledge, and whether the prediction can be trusted for individual patient decisions [53]. This opacity is particularly problematic in high-stakes clinical contexts such as micro-TESE counseling, where the consequences of the prediction directly influence an irreversible surgical decision.

Explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) encompasses a family of methods designed to make ML model predictions interpretable and transparent. The XAI landscape includes both model-agnostic methods—applicable to any ML algorithm regardless of its internal structure—and model-specific approaches that exploit the mathematical properties of particular algorithm families. Among the most widely adopted XAI methods in clinical research are SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), which provide theoretically grounded feature attribution values based on coalitional game theory [54]; Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME), which approximate complex model behavior locally with interpretable surrogate models [55]; and partial dependence plots (PDP), which visualize the marginal effect of individual features on the predicted outcome [56].

The use of multiple, complementary XAI methods—rather than relying on a single explainability technique—provides several important benefits. Cross-method concordance analysis can validate that feature importance rankings and directional effects are robust and not artifacts of a particular explanatory framework. Global explanations (whole-model feature importance) can be complemented by local explanations (individual patient-level reasoning) to provide both population-level insights and case-specific clinical narratives. When the rankings and directional effects are consistent across SHAP, LIME, and PDP, clinicians can have greater confidence that the model's reasoning reflects genuine data patterns rather than methodological artifacts [57].

In the context of this thesis, multi-method XAI serves a dual purpose. First, it provides clinical validation of the model by demonstrating that the features driving predictions are biologically plausible and consistent with established knowledge of reproductive endocrinology—specifically, that the gonadotropin–testosterone axis, patient age, and testicular volume parameters emerge as dominant predictors. Second, it enables the translation of opaque model outputs into clinically interpretable explanations that can be communicated to patients and their partners during preoperative counseling, supporting the shared decision-making paradigm that is central to contemporary patient-centered care [58].

1.4 Research Objectives

The overarching aim of this thesis is to develop, validate, and interpret a comprehensive machine learning framework for predicting successful sperm retrieval in non-obstructive azoospermia patients undergoing microdissection testicular sperm extraction, with emphasis on clinical explainability and translational utility.

1.4.1 Primary Objective

The primary objective is to develop and rigorously validate a comprehensive ML-based prediction framework for micro-TESE sperm retrieval outcomes using a large retrospective cohort of 2,413 NOA patients from the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine. This framework incorporates a custom NLP pipeline for parsing free-text bilateral pathology reports, a 16-model benchmark spanning all major ML paradigms, and a leakage-controlled nested cross-validation design adhering to TRIPOD+AI and PROBAST+AI reporting standards.

1.4.2 Secondary Objectives

The secondary objectives of this research are as follows:

First, to systematically compare 16 ML algorithms—including gradient boosting models (CatBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, Gradient Boosting), ensemble methods (Random Forest, Stacking Ensemble, Voting Ensemble), linear models (Logistic Regression, Ridge Classifier, Lasso, Elastic Net), a decision tree, a multilayer perceptron neural network, k-nearest neighbors, support vector machine with radial basis function kernel, and naive Bayes—in order to identify the optimal algorithmic approach for this clinical prediction task and to characterize the performance landscape across distinct ML paradigms.

Second, to identify the key clinical features driving model predictions through multi-method explainable AI analysis using TreeSHAP (global and local), LIME, and partial dependence plots, and to validate the stability and concordance of feature importance rankings across these complementary methods through bootstrap resampling.

Third, to assess model calibration through reliability diagrams, Brier scores, and Platt scaling analysis, and to quantify clinical utility through decision curve analysis, thereby establishing the threshold probability range over which the model provides positive net benefit compared with default clinical strategies.

Fourth, to design a translational clinical decision support system (CDSS) prototype that incorporates the validated ML model with clinically governed feature selection, reference ranges, color-coded validation logic, and patient-facing SHAP explanations, bridging the gap between model development and bedside clinical utility.

1.4.3 Tertiary Objective

The tertiary objective is to contribute to the evidence base through a systematic scoping review of AI predictive models and advancements in micro-TESE for NOA, published in Human Reproduction Open [32], which provides the contextual foundation for the empirical work and identifies the specific methodological gaps addressed by the present research.

1.5 Research Questions and Hypotheses

This thesis is organized around three central research questions, each associated with specific testable hypotheses:

Research Question 1: Can machine learning models predict successful sperm retrieval in NOA patients undergoing micro-TESE with clinically useful discrimination, as defined by an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) exceeding 0.75?

Hypothesis 1: A rigorously developed and leakage-controlled ML model trained on a comprehensive feature set—including clinical demographics, hormonal profiles, testicular measurements, genetic markers, and NLP-derived bilateral histopathological indicators—will achieve an AUC significantly exceeding 0.75, representing clinically useful discrimination for preoperative counseling. This hypothesis is grounded in the premise that the multidimensional clinical profile of NOA patients contains predictive information that, when captured by flexible nonlinear ML algorithms, exceeds the discriminatory capacity of individual biomarkers or simple linear combinations thereof.

Research Question 2: Which clinical and laboratory features are most predictive of micro-TESE sperm retrieval success, and are the feature importance rankings stable across multiple explainable AI methods?

Hypothesis 2: Hormonal parameters of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis—particularly gonadotropins (FSH, LH) and testosterone—will emerge as the dominant predictive features, consistent with their established biological role in reflecting spermatogenic reserve. Furthermore, the top-ranked features will demonstrate high concordance across SHAP, LIME, and PDP analyses, indicating that their importance is a genuine property of the data and the prediction task rather than an artifact of any single explainability method.

Research Question 3: Can explainable AI methods provide clinically interpretable explanations of model predictions that are suitable for integration into preoperative counseling and clinical decision support?

Hypothesis 3: Multi-method XAI analysis will produce explanations that are (a) biologically coherent, reflecting established pathophysiological mechanisms of spermatogenic failure; (b) clinically actionable, identifying modifiable or informative features that can guide counseling conversations; and (c) patient-comprehensible, expressible in visual and narrative formats suitable for shared decision-making. The integration of these explanations into a CDSS framework will demonstrate the feasibility of translating ML model outputs into bedside clinical tools.

1.6 Scope and Limitations

This study is conducted within a defined scope, and its limitations are transparently acknowledged in accordance with best practices for clinical prediction model research.

1.6.1 Study Design and Data Source

This is a single-center retrospective cohort study based on data from the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine, Tehran, Iran, covering the period 2007–2022. The retrospective design, while enabling analysis of a large cohort accumulated over fifteen years, introduces inherent limitations related to data quality, completeness, and potential selection bias. The single-center nature limits the generalizability of the findings to other institutional settings, patient populations, and surgical practices, and external validation in independent cohorts is an essential next step before clinical deployment.

1.6.2 Data Quality and Missingness

The dataset exhibits substantial missingness across several clinical variables, reflecting the real-world nature of the data source and evolving clinical protocols over the fifteen-year study period. Missingness ranges from 0% for core demographics (age, race) to over 80% for sonographic testicular measurements, with several variables (inhibin B, AMH, SHBG, TSH) exhibiting 100% missingness and consequently excluded from analysis. While k-nearest neighbor imputation with missingness indicators was employed within the cross-validation framework to handle incomplete data, the degree of missingness for certain potentially important biomarkers (e.g., inhibin B, which has been identified as a strong predictor in other studies) represents a limitation that may underestimate the full predictive potential of a complete feature set.

1.6.3 Outcome Definition

The primary outcome is defined as successful sperm retrieval (binary: yes/no) at the first micro-TESE procedure. This definition does not capture downstream outcomes including sperm quality parameters, ICSI fertilization rates, embryo development, clinical pregnancy, or live birth rates. While sperm retrieval is the immediate and most clinically relevant endpoint for the surgical intervention itself, a more comprehensive outcome hierarchy would provide greater insight into the ultimate reproductive utility of the prediction.

1.6.4 Methodological Boundaries

The study evaluates sixteen ML algorithms but does not explore deep learning architectures beyond the multilayer perceptron, as the tabular nature and moderate size of the dataset favor tree-based ensemble methods. Time-series modeling of longitudinal patient trajectories is not addressed, as the dataset structure is cross-sectional at the point of surgical intervention. The CDSS prototype is specified at the design level and has not yet undergone prospective clinical validation, usability testing, or formal clinical trial evaluation.

1.6.5 Ethical Considerations

The study was approved by the Institutional Review Board (IRB) as a retrospective analysis of de-identified clinical data, with a waiver of individual informed consent. All data were handled in accordance with institutional data governance policies and applicable privacy regulations.

1.7 Thesis Organization

This thesis is organized into five chapters, each addressing a distinct component of the research framework.

Chapter 1 (the present chapter) has provided the introduction, establishing the clinical context of non-obstructive azoospermia and micro-TESE, articulating the research gap, defining the objectives and research questions, and delineating the scope and limitations of the study.

Chapter 2 presents a comprehensive review of the literature, organized into several thematic sections. The first section reviews the epidemiology, pathophysiology, and clinical management of NOA, including the evolution of surgical sperm retrieval techniques. The second section examines the landscape of clinical and laboratory predictors of micro-TESE outcomes, including hormonal biomarkers, histopathological patterns, genetic markers, and imaging parameters. The third section provides a critical appraisal of existing machine learning and statistical prediction models for micro-TESE, synthesizing findings from the fourteen studies identified in the scoping review [32] and subsequent publications. The fourth section reviews the theoretical foundations and practical applications of explainable AI in clinical medicine, with emphasis on SHAP, LIME, and partial dependence methods. The fifth section addresses model calibration, decision curve analysis, and the principles of clinical utility assessment. The sixth section discusses clinical decision support systems in reproductive medicine and the translational pathway from model to bedside.

Chapter 3 details the methodology of the study, including: the study design and population; data collection procedures and feature engineering, with particular attention to the custom NLP pipeline for bilateral pathology report parsing; the 16-model ML pipeline with nested cross-validation, hyperparameter optimization, and leakage-prevention protocols; the multi-method XAI framework (TreeSHAP, LIME, PDP); calibration assessment and decision curve analysis; the CDSS design specification; and adherence to TRIPOD+AI 2024 and PROBAST+AI 2025 reporting standards. The transparent disclosure of the v1-to-v2 pipeline correction—in which a data leakage artifact caused by an IQR-clipping bug was identified and eliminated, resulting in a change of the champion model from LightGBM (AUC 0.7327) to CatBoost (AUC 0.8306)—is presented as a case study in research integrity and the importance of leakage-controlled validation.

Chapter 4 presents the results, organized into the following sections: descriptive statistics of the study cohort; the 16-model performance comparison with the v2 leaderboard; detailed operating characteristics of the CatBoost champion model; calibration analysis including Brier scores and reliability diagrams; decision curve analysis with net benefit quantification and interventions-saved estimates; global and local SHAP analysis with the complete 29-feature importance ranking; LIME local explanations; partial dependence plot patterns for key features; cross-method concordance analysis; and the CDSS feature mapping with reference ranges and scoring logic. Five representative local explanation cases are presented to illustrate the clinical narrative capability of the XAI framework.

Chapter 5 provides the discussion and conclusions, synthesizing the key findings in the context of the existing literature, discussing the biological coherence of the XAI results, addressing the clinical implications for preoperative counseling and shared decision-making, acknowledging limitations and potential biases, and outlining directions for future research including prospective validation, multi-center external validation, integration of emerging biomarkers (rete testis thickness, Raman spectroscopy), and formal clinical evaluation of the CDSS prototype. The chapter concludes with a summary of the contributions of this thesis to the fields of medical informatics and reproductive medicine.

CHAPTER 2: LITERATURE REVIEW

2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Non-Obstructive Azoospermia

2.1.1 Definition and Classification

Azoospermia, defined as the complete absence of spermatozoa in the ejaculate following centrifugation of at least two semen samples collected after appropriate abstinence intervals, affects approximately 1% of all men in the general population and 10–15% of men presenting to fertility clinics [1,2]. This condition is broadly classified into two pathophysiologically distinct categories: obstructive azoospermia (OA), in which spermatogenesis is normal but the transport of spermatozoa is impeded by anatomical or functional obstruction of the reproductive tract, and non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA), in which there is an intrinsic failure of spermatogenesis within the testes themselves [3]. NOA accounts for approximately 60% of all azoospermia cases and represents the most severe phenotype of male factor infertility, as it reflects fundamental defects in the biological machinery of sperm production rather than a mechanical impediment to sperm delivery [1,2].

The classification of NOA follows a conventional anatomical framework that distinguishes pre-testicular, testicular, and post-testicular causes [3,4]. Pre-testicular NOA encompasses conditions in which the testes are intrinsically capable of spermatogenesis but fail to receive the appropriate hormonal stimulation from the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis. This category includes hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, which may be congenital (as in Kallmann syndrome, characterized by anosmia and gonadotropin-releasing hormone deficiency) or acquired (as a consequence of pituitary adenomas, infiltrative diseases, exogenous androgen administration, or hypothalamic suppression from chronic illness or medications) [4,5]. Pre-testicular causes are of particular clinical significance because they are frequently amenable to medical therapy with gonadotropins, potentially rendering surgical sperm retrieval unnecessary.

Testicular NOA, which constitutes the largest and most clinically challenging subcategory, encompasses conditions in which the spermatogenic failure originates within the testicular parenchyma itself. The etiologies are diverse and include chromosomal abnormalities such as Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY), Y-chromosome microdeletions affecting the azoospermia factor (AZF) regions, single-gene mutations, cryptorchidism (particularly bilateral or late-corrected), prior testicular torsion, post-pubertal mumps orchitis, exposure to gonadotoxic agents (chemotherapy, radiation), varicocele, and a substantial proportion classified as idiopathic when no identifiable cause is found despite comprehensive evaluation [3,4,5]. The histopathological correlates of testicular NOA include Sertoli-cell-only syndrome (SCO), in which the seminiferous tubules are devoid of germ cells entirely; maturation arrest (MA), in which spermatogenesis initiates but halts at a specific stage of germ cell development; and hypospermatogenesis, in which all stages of spermatogenesis are present but at markedly reduced efficiency [6]. These histopathological patterns carry prognostic significance for sperm retrieval, as the probability of finding spermatozoa at micro-TESE varies considerably according to the underlying histological substrate [6,7].

Post-testicular NOA is a less common but clinically important category that includes ejaculatory dysfunction (retrograde ejaculation, anejaculation due to spinal cord injury or diabetes mellitus) and rare congenital bilateral absence of the vas deferens (CBAVD) in which the obstruction is so proximal or the epididymis so dysplastic that it functionally mimics NOA [3]. In clinical practice, the distinction between OA and NOA is made through a combination of clinical history, physical examination, hormonal evaluation, and genetic testing, and is critical because it determines the therapeutic approach: OA is typically managed with surgical reconstruction or simple sperm aspiration, whereas NOA requires more advanced techniques such as microdissection testicular sperm extraction [4].

2.1.2 Etiology of Non-Obstructive Azoospermia

The etiological landscape of NOA is remarkably heterogeneous, reflecting the complexity of the genetic, hormonal, developmental, environmental, and idiopathic factors that can disrupt spermatogenesis. A comprehensive understanding of these etiologies is essential for accurate diagnosis, prognostic counseling, and selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies.

Genetic causes constitute a major and well-characterized category. Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY and its mosaic variants) is the most common genetic cause of NOA, accounting for approximately 10–15% of all NOA cases [8,9]. The presence of an additional X chromosome leads to testicular fibrosis, progressive Leydig cell insufficiency, and near-universal azoospermia, though mosaic forms (46,XY/47,XXY) may retain residual spermatogenic foci that can be targeted during micro-TESE. Importantly, sperm retrieval success rates in Klinefelter patients undergoing micro-TESE range from 30% to 70% across series, and the retrieved spermatozoa can produce normal 46,XY offspring through ICSI [8,9]. Y-chromosome microdeletions affecting the azoospermia factor (AZF) regions on the long arm (Yq11) represent the second most common genetic cause, identified in approximately 5–15% of NOA patients [10]. Three critical regions — AZFa, AZFb, and AZFc — harbor genes essential for spermatogenesis. Complete AZFa deletions, which remove the USP9Y and DBY genes, result in SCO syndrome with essentially zero probability of sperm retrieval. Complete AZFb deletions similarly produce maturation arrest with no retrievable sperm. However, AZFc deletions, which remove the DAZ gene cluster, retain variable residual spermatogenesis, and sperm retrieval is possible in 30–70% of cases [10,11]. The distinction among AZF deletion subtypes is therefore critical for preoperative counseling, as complete AZFa or AZFb deletions represent contraindications to micro-TESE.

Beyond chromosomal and Y-microdeletion etiologies, an expanding catalog of monogenic causes has been identified through next-generation sequencing and exome studies, including mutations in TEX11, TEX14, SYCE1, DMC1, and other genes involved in meiotic recombination, synaptonemal complex formation, and spermatogonial stem cell maintenance [12]. While individually rare, these monogenic causes collectively account for an increasing proportion of previously "idiopathic" NOA as genomic testing becomes more comprehensive.

Hormonal etiologies reflect disruption of the HPG axis at any level. Elevated follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) levels, typically exceeding 12–15 mIU/mL, are the hallmark of primary testicular failure, reflecting the loss of negative feedback from inhibin B and the diminished Sertoli cell mass [5]. Elevated luteinizing hormone (LH) reflects compensatory pituitary stimulation in the setting of Leydig cell insufficiency, and low serum testosterone levels confirm the functional consequence of this Leydig cell failure [5,13]. The hormonal profile therefore provides both diagnostic information (distinguishing pre-testicular from testicular causes) and prognostic information, as the degree of FSH elevation has been inconsistently but repeatedly associated with micro-TESE outcomes [14,15].

Cryptorchidism (undescended testes) is a major developmental risk factor for NOA, particularly when bilateral and when orchiopexy is performed late (after age 2 years) [16]. The elevated intra-abdominal temperature impairs spermatogenesis, and the histological damage is often irreversible even after surgical correction. Varicocele, defined as abnormal dilation of the pampiniform venous plexus, is identified in a subset of NOA patients, and varicocele repair (varicocelectomy) has been reported in some series to improve spermatogenesis sufficiently to allow sperm to appear in the ejaculate in 10–40% of cases, potentially obviating the need for micro-TESE [17]. However, the role of varicocele repair in NOA remains controversial, and many patients require surgical sperm retrieval regardless. Additional acquired causes include gonadotoxic exposures (alkylating chemotherapy, testicular radiation), orchitis (post-pubertal mumps), testicular trauma, and systemic conditions such as uncontrolled diabetes mellitus and chronic liver disease [3,4].

Despite comprehensive evaluation, approximately 30–40% of NOA cases remain idiopathic, with no identifiable genetic, hormonal, developmental, or environmental cause [3,18]. This substantial proportion of unexplained cases underscores the incomplete understanding of spermatogenic biology and motivates ongoing research into novel biomarkers and predictive tools.

2.1.3 Pathophysiology of Spermatogenesis and Its Disruption in NOA

Spermatogenesis is one of the most complex and tightly regulated biological processes in the human body, involving the transformation of diploid spermatogonial stem cells into highly specialized haploid spermatozoa over a cycle of approximately 74 days [19]. This process occurs within the seminiferous tubules of the testes and is orchestrated by an intricate interplay between Sertoli cells (which provide structural support, nutritional signals, and the blood-testis barrier), Leydig cells in the interstitium (which produce testosterone in response to LH stimulation), and the germ cells themselves, which undergo a precisely choreographed sequence of mitotic proliferation, meiotic division, and morphological maturation [19,20].

The spermatogenic process can be divided into three principal phases. The first phase, spermatocytogenesis, involves the mitotic expansion of type A spermatogonia (including the self-renewing dark and actively dividing pale subtypes) into type B spermatogonia, which commit to differentiation and enter meiosis as primary spermatocytes. The second phase, meiosis, involves two successive divisions: meiosis I, in which homologous chromosomes pair, recombine, and segregate to produce secondary spermatocytes, and meiosis II, in which sister chromatids separate to yield haploid round spermatids. The third phase, spermiogenesis, involves the dramatic morphological transformation of round spermatids into elongated spermatids and ultimately mature spermatozoa, including nuclear condensation, acrosome formation, flagellar assembly, and cytoplasmic shedding [19,20]. Finally, spermiation releases the mature spermatozoa into the tubular lumen for transport through the rete testis, efferent ductules, and epididymis.

In NOA, this carefully orchestrated process is disrupted at one or more stages. In Sertoli-cell-only syndrome (SCO), the most severe histopathological pattern, the seminiferous tubules are devoid of germ cells entirely, with only Sertoli cells lining the tubular basement membrane. SCO reflects a failure of spermatogonial stem cell establishment, maintenance, or survival, and is associated with Klinefelter syndrome, AZFa deletions, and severe developmental insults [6]. Maturation arrest (MA) represents a block at a specific stage of germ cell development, most commonly at the primary spermatocyte stage (meiotic arrest) or at the round spermatid stage. Early maturation arrest (at the spermatogonial or primary spermatocyte stage) carries a worse prognosis than late maturation arrest (at the round spermatid stage), as the latter indicates that spermatogenesis has progressed further and may harbor focal areas of complete spermatogenesis [6,7]. Hypospermatogenesis describes a pattern in which all stages of spermatogenesis are present but at markedly reduced numbers, and this pattern generally carries the most favorable prognosis for sperm retrieval [6,7].

Critically, the histopathological patterns in NOA are not uniform throughout the testis. Individual tubules within the same testis can exhibit different patterns — some showing SCO, others showing maturation arrest, and focal areas showing complete spermatogenesis [21]. This topographical heterogeneity is the biological foundation upon which microdissection TESE is based: by systematically examining the testicular parenchyma under high-power optical magnification, the surgeon can identify and selectively extract the dilated, opaque tubules that are most likely to contain active spermatogenesis, even when surrounding tissue is azoospermic [21,22].

2.1.4 Diagnostic Workup

The diagnostic evaluation of NOA follows a structured algorithm designed to confirm the diagnosis, identify the underlying etiology, and stratify patients for appropriate intervention. The workup begins with a detailed clinical history encompassing reproductive history, developmental milestones (cryptorchidism, pubertal timing), prior genital surgery or trauma, medical comorbidities, medication use (particularly exogenous androgens, chemotherapy, or medications affecting the HPG axis), and family history of infertility or genetic disorders [3,4].

Physical examination focuses on testicular volume assessment (by orchidometry or ultrasonography), consistency of the testes (soft testes suggesting spermatogenic failure), presence of epididymal fullness (suggesting obstruction), vas deferens palpation (to exclude CBAVD), and evaluation for varicocele and secondary sexual characteristics [3,4]. Testicular volume is a particularly important clinical parameter, as testicular volume correlates with the mass of seminiferous epithelium and therefore with spermatogenic capacity. Normal adult testicular volume is typically 15–25 mL per testis, and volumes below 12 mL are suggestive of impaired spermatogenesis [23].

The hormonal evaluation includes serum FSH, LH, total testosterone, estradiol (E2), and prolactin. Elevated FSH (>12 mIU/mL) with small testes is the classical endocrine signature of primary testicular failure [5]. The testosterone-to-estradiol (T/E2) ratio and testosterone-to-LH ratio have been proposed as additional endocrine markers with prognostic value for sperm retrieval [14,15]. Genetic testing is recommended for all NOA patients and includes karyotyping (to detect Klinefelter syndrome and other chromosomal abnormalities) and Y-chromosome microdeletion analysis (to identify AZF deletions that inform prognosis and may contraindicate surgical retrieval in AZFa/AZFb cases) [10,11].

Testicular biopsy, performed either as a diagnostic procedure prior to micro-TESE or as part of the micro-TESE procedure itself, provides histopathological confirmation of the spermatogenic pattern. Bilateral testicular biopsies are recommended to capture the heterogeneity of histopathological patterns between the two testes [6,7]. More recently, advanced imaging modalities such as high-resolution scrotal ultrasonography (including assessments of testicular dimensions, echotexture, and the rete testis) have been investigated as non-invasive adjuncts to the preoperative workup [24].

2.1.5 Treatment Landscape

The management of NOA has evolved substantially over the past three decades, driven by advances in surgical sperm retrieval techniques and assisted reproductive technology. The treatment approach depends on the underlying etiology. Patients with hypogonadotropic hypogonadism may respond to medical therapy with gonadotropins (human chorionic gonadotropin [hCG] and recombinant FSH), which can restore spermatogenesis in up to 70% of cases when the testes are intrinsically normal [5]. For patients with varicocele-associated NOA, varicocele repair may improve spermatogenesis in a subset, though the evidence remains debated [17].

For the majority of NOA patients, however, surgical sperm retrieval followed by ICSI represents the primary therapeutic pathway to biological fatherhood. The evolution of sperm retrieval techniques from conventional TESE to micro-TESE has been a transformative development in andrology and is discussed in detail in Section 2.2. When surgical retrieval fails, patients are counseled regarding alternative options including donor sperm insemination, adoption, and emerging experimental approaches such as in vitro spermatogenesis from spermatogonial stem cells [25].


2.2 Microdissection Testicular Sperm Extraction (Micro-TESE)

2.2.1 Historical Development

The history of surgical sperm retrieval for NOA represents a fascinating progression from relatively crude extraction techniques to the microsurgical precision of modern micro-TESE. The concept of retrieving spermatozoa directly from testicular tissue was first demonstrated by Craft and Yovich in 1995, and Devroey et al. subsequently showed that testicular spermatozoa could achieve fertilization through ICSI [26,27]. The initial approach, conventional testicular sperm extraction (c-TESE), involved either an open biopsy with excision of multiple random pieces of testicular parenchyma or a percutaneous needle aspiration (TESA). While these techniques proved that sperm could indeed be found within the testes of some NOA patients, their success rates were modest (typically 25–45%), and they were associated with significant drawbacks, including substantial tissue damage, risk of testicular devascularization, and a fundamentally random sampling strategy that failed to exploit the topographical heterogeneity of spermatogenesis within the NOA testis [26,27].

The pivotal innovation came from Schlegel in 1999, who introduced microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE), a technique that fundamentally transformed the approach to surgical sperm retrieval in NOA [22]. Schlegel recognized that even in testes with predominantly atrophic seminiferous tubules, focal areas of preserved spermatogenesis could exist, and these areas could be identified under the operating microscope by their larger diameter and more opaque appearance compared to the surrounding sclerotic or atrophic tubules [22]. By using high-power optical magnification (typically 20–25× magnification), the surgeon could systematically inspect the exposed testicular parenchyma and selectively extract only those tubules with the morphological characteristics suggestive of active spermatogenesis, thereby maximizing the yield of spermatozoa while minimizing tissue damage and preserving the testicular vasculature.

2.2.2 Surgical Technique

The micro-TESE procedure is typically performed under general anesthesia, though regional anesthesia has been used in selected cases. The testis is delivered through a median raphe or transverse scrotal incision, and the tunica albuginea is opened along an equatorial or polar plane under the operating microscope [22,28]. The testicular parenchyma is then systematically examined under 20–25× magnification. The surgeon identifies and biopsies dilated, opaque seminiferous tubules, which are more likely to contain active spermatogenesis, while avoiding the thin, translucent, and collapsed tubules characteristic of Sertoli-cell-only or fibrotic tissue. The retrieved tissue samples are immediately processed by an embryologist in the adjacent laboratory, who mechanically dissects and enzymatically digests the tissue to identify spermatozoa. The procedure is typically bilateral, with examination of both testes, and the surgeon proceeds methodically through the parenchyma, often requiring 2–4 hours of operative time for a thorough examination [22,28].

The microsurgical approach offers several advantages over conventional TESE. First, it enables targeted sampling based on tubular morphology, exploiting the known correlation between tubular diameter and spermatogenic activity. Second, it permits identification and preservation of the intratesticular vasculature, reducing the risk of postoperative testicular devascularization, atrophy, and hormonal compromise. Third, it allows examination of a much larger volume of testicular parenchyma than random biopsy, increasing the probability of finding the focal spermatogenic foci that characterize many NOA cases [22,28,29].

2.2.3 Success Rates Across the Literature

Sperm retrieval rates for micro-TESE vary across published series, generally ranging from 30% to 63%, with the variability attributable to differences in patient selection, underlying etiologies, surgical experience, and the criteria for defining "successful retrieval" [1,2,28,29]. Large meta-analyses and systematic reviews have consistently demonstrated that micro-TESE achieves higher sperm retrieval rates than conventional TESE or TESA, with an estimated 10–20% absolute improvement in retrieval probability [29]. In a landmark systematic review and meta-analysis by Bernie et al., micro-TESE achieved a weighted mean SRR of approximately 52%, compared to 35% for c-TESE [29]. However, even with optimal microsurgical technique, a substantial proportion of NOA patients (approximately 40–60%) will have unsuccessful procedures, underscoring the need for reliable preoperative predictive tools.

Specific patient populations show differential success rates. Patients with hypospermatogenesis on prior biopsy have the highest SRR (60–80%), followed by those with late maturation arrest (40–60%), early maturation arrest (20–40%), and SCO (15–40%) [6,7,28]. Klinefelter syndrome patients achieve SRRs of approximately 30–70% across series, with some evidence suggesting that younger age and prior hormonal optimization may improve outcomes [8,9]. AZFc microdeletion patients have reported SRRs of 30–70%, while complete AZFa or AZFb deletions are associated with essentially zero retrieval probability [10,11].

2.2.4 Prognostic Factors

The identification of reliable preoperative prognostic factors for micro-TESE success has been a major focus of andrological research. Numerous clinical, hormonal, genetic, and histopathological variables have been investigated, with variable and sometimes contradictory results across studies.

Hormonal markers have received the most attention. Serum FSH has been the most extensively studied hormonal predictor, with some studies reporting an inverse association between FSH levels and SRR (suggesting that higher FSH, reflecting more severe spermatogenic failure, predicts worse outcomes) while others have found no significant independent association after adjusting for other variables [14,15]. Similarly, serum testosterone, LH, inhibin B, and derived ratios (T/E2, T/LH, T/FSH) have been variably associated with micro-TESE outcomes across studies, with no single hormonal marker achieving sufficient predictive accuracy to guide clinical decision-making in isolation [14,15].

Patient age has been investigated as a prognostic factor, with some studies suggesting that younger age (particularly below 35–40 years) is associated with better outcomes, while others have found no significant age effect [28,30]. Testicular volume, assessed by orchidometry or ultrasonography, has been positively associated with SRR in some series, consistent with the assumption that larger testes may retain more spermatogenic tissue [23,24]. Histopathological pattern on diagnostic biopsy, when available, remains one of the strongest predictive factors, as discussed above [6,7].

Genetic factors, particularly Klinefelter syndrome status and Y-microdeletion subtype, provide important prognostic information. Novel imaging biomarkers such as rete testis thickness have also been recently proposed [24]. Body mass index, medical comorbidities, duration of infertility, and prior surgical history have been investigated with less consistent results. The multifactorial nature of prognosis and the inconsistent findings across studies have motivated the application of machine learning methods that can integrate multiple variables simultaneously and capture non-linear relationships, as discussed in Section 2.3.

2.2.5 Complications and Patient Counseling

Micro-TESE is generally considered a safe procedure, but it is not without risks. Reported complications include postoperative pain, hematoma formation, testicular edema, and, rarely, testicular devascularization leading to atrophy [28,29]. Transient decreases in serum testosterone have been observed in the postoperative period, and some patients experience persistent hormonal compromise, though the microsurgical approach has been associated with less hormonal damage than conventional TESE due to its vascular-sparing technique [28]. The psychological impact of an unsuccessful procedure on the couple is substantial and must be addressed during preoperative counseling.

Effective preoperative counseling should include realistic expectations regarding the probability of success (typically 30–60% depending on clinical factors), discussion of the implications of a negative result (need for alternative pathways to parenthood), the potential for repeat procedures (salvage micro-TESE), and the risks and recovery timeline of the surgery itself [28,29]. It is within this counseling framework that accurate preoperative predictive tools would be most valuable, enabling personalized risk estimates that can inform shared decision-making between the clinician and the patient.

2.2.6 Comparison with Other Sperm Retrieval Methods

Several alternative techniques for testicular or epididymal sperm retrieval have been described, including testicular sperm aspiration (TESA), percutaneous epididymal sperm aspiration (PESA), microsurgical epididymal sperm aspiration (MESA), and conventional open TESE [26,27]. TESA and PESA are minimally invasive percutaneous techniques that are suitable for OA but have limited utility in NOA due to low retrieval rates. Conventional open TESE, while more effective than percutaneous techniques in NOA, involves random tissue sampling without the benefit of optical magnification and is associated with greater tissue loss and vascular injury. MESA is primarily indicated for OA with epididymal obstruction. Among all techniques, micro-TESE has emerged as the gold standard for NOA owing to its superior SRR, reduced tissue damage, and ability to exploit the topographical heterogeneity of spermatogenesis under magnification [22,28,29].


2.3 Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Medicine

2.3.1 Overview of Machine Learning Paradigms

Machine learning (ML), a subdomain of artificial intelligence (AI), encompasses computational methods that enable systems to learn patterns from data without explicit programming [31]. ML has emerged as a transformative technology in medicine, offering the potential to extract complex, non-linear relationships from high-dimensional clinical datasets that may elude traditional statistical methods [32]. The three primary ML paradigms — supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and reinforcement learning — provide complementary approaches to different types of clinical problems.

Supervised learning, the paradigm most relevant to clinical prediction modeling, involves training an algorithm on a labeled dataset in which both input features (predictors) and the desired output (outcome label) are known [31,32]. The algorithm learns a mapping function from inputs to outputs that can generalize to new, unseen instances. Supervised learning is further subdivided into classification (predicting discrete categorical outcomes, such as successful vs. unsuccessful sperm retrieval) and regression (predicting continuous outcomes). The present thesis focuses on binary classification, as the primary outcome of micro-TESE (sperm retrieval success or failure) is inherently dichotomous.

Unsupervised learning operates on unlabeled data and seeks to discover inherent structure, such as clusters of similar patients or latent patterns in clinical features [31]. Common methods include k-means clustering, hierarchical clustering, principal component analysis, and autoencoders. While not the primary analytical framework in this thesis, unsupervised techniques have been used in exploratory analyses to identify patient subgroups with differential treatment responses and to reduce dimensionality in high-dimensional feature spaces [33].

Reinforcement learning involves an agent that learns to make sequential decisions by interacting with an environment and receiving feedback in the form of rewards or penalties [31]. Clinical applications of reinforcement learning include dynamic treatment regimen optimization, sequential medication dosing, and real-time ICU management, though these applications remain largely experimental and are not directly relevant to preoperative prediction in micro-TESE [34].

2.3.2 Key Algorithms in Supervised Classification

The landscape of supervised classification algorithms is broad, and the selection of appropriate models for a given clinical prediction task depends on the characteristics of the dataset, the nature of the features, sample size, class balance, and the interpretability requirements of the clinical context. The following subsections describe the major algorithmic families relevant to this thesis.

Linear models form the foundation of statistical classification and include logistic regression (LR) and stochastic gradient descent (SGD) classifiers. Logistic regression models the log-odds of the positive class as a linear function of the input features, yielding calibrated probability estimates through the logistic (sigmoid) function [35]. LR is highly interpretable, with coefficients directly reflecting the strength and direction of each predictor's association with the outcome. Regularized variants include L1 regularization (Lasso), which promotes sparsity by driving irrelevant feature coefficients to zero; L2 regularization (Ridge), which shrinks coefficients to reduce multicollinearity; and Elastic Net, which combines both penalties [36]. The SGD classifier optimizes the logistic or hinge loss function using stochastic gradient descent, enabling efficient training on large datasets while supporting various regularization schemes [37].
