Linear models serve as essential baselines in any ML benchmarking study, as they quantify the predictive information available through linear feature combinations alone.

Tree-based models partition the feature space through recursive binary splits based on feature thresholds, producing hierarchical decision structures. A single decision tree (DT) is highly interpretable but prone to overfitting and instability [38]. Random Forest (RF) addresses these limitations by constructing an ensemble of decorrelated decision trees, each trained on a bootstrap sample of the data with random feature subsets at each split, and aggregating their predictions through majority voting (classification) or averaging (regression) [39]. The randomization mechanisms in RF reduce variance and improve generalization while retaining the ability to capture non-linear relationships and interactions. Extra Trees (Extremely Randomized Trees) extends the RF concept by randomizing not only the feature subsets but also the split thresholds, creating even greater diversity among the ensemble members and often producing smoother decision boundaries [40].

Boosting methods represent a powerful family of ensemble algorithms that sequentially train weak learners (typically shallow decision trees) to correct the errors of previous learners, progressively improving the overall model's predictive performance. AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting) was the first widely successful boosting algorithm, assigning increasing weight to misclassified instances so that subsequent trees focus on the most difficult cases [41]. Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM) generalized the boosting concept by framing each iteration as gradient descent in function space, fitting new trees to the negative gradient (pseudo-residuals) of the loss function [42]. This framework gave rise to several highly optimized implementations that currently dominate tabular data benchmarks:

XGBoost (Extreme Gradient Boosting) introduced regularization terms in the objective function (L1 and L2 penalties on leaf weights), approximate split finding using quantile sketches, sparsity-aware algorithms for efficient handling of missing values, and column subsampling to reduce overfitting [43]. These innovations made XGBoost the most widely used ML algorithm in competitive data science for several years.

LightGBM (Light Gradient Boosting Machine) further optimized gradient boosting through a histogram-based split-finding algorithm, gradient-based one-side sampling (GOSS) to focus on instances with larger gradients, and exclusive feature bundling (EFB) to reduce the effective dimensionality of sparse features [44]. LightGBM's leaf-wise tree growth strategy (as opposed to XGBoost's level-wise approach) enables deeper, more complex trees with fewer iterations, often achieving comparable or superior performance with significantly faster training.

CatBoost (Categorical Boosting) was specifically designed to handle categorical features natively through ordered target statistics, which replace traditional one-hot encoding and avoid the target leakage inherent in naive target-based encoding schemes [45]. CatBoost also introduced ordered boosting, a modification of the standard gradient boosting procedure that reduces the estimation bias of leaf values by using different subsets of data for computing gradients and fitting the model, resulting in reduced overfitting, particularly on smaller datasets. CatBoost's native handling of categorical features and its theoretical guarantees against overfitting make it particularly well-suited for clinical datasets with mixed feature types [45].

Instance-based methods, exemplified by K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN), make predictions based on the similarity of a new instance to its closest neighbors in the training set [46]. KNN assigns the majority class of the k nearest neighbors (determined by a distance metric such as Euclidean, Manhattan, or Minkowski distance) as the predicted class. KNN is non-parametric and makes no assumptions about the underlying data distribution, allowing it to capture arbitrarily complex decision boundaries. However, KNN is computationally expensive at prediction time (requiring distance computations to all training instances), sensitive to the curse of dimensionality, and sensitive to irrelevant features and feature scaling [46].

Probabilistic classifiers estimate class posterior probabilities based on Bayes' theorem. Naive Bayes classifiers assume conditional independence of features given the class label, a simplifying assumption that often does not hold in practice but frequently yields surprisingly competitive performance [47]. Gaussian Naive Bayes (GNB) additionally assumes that continuous features follow a Gaussian distribution within each class. Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA) relaxes the equal-covariance assumption of Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) and models each class with its own covariance matrix, fitting quadratic decision boundaries [48]. Probabilistic classifiers are computationally efficient, naturally produce calibrated probabilities, and serve as useful baselines for assessing the contribution of more complex modeling approaches.

Neural networks, specifically the Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), consist of multiple layers of interconnected artificial neurons that learn hierarchical feature representations through backpropagation [49]. A typical MLP for tabular classification includes an input layer (dimensionality matching the feature set), one or more hidden layers with non-linear activation functions (ReLU, tanh, or sigmoid), and an output layer with a softmax or sigmoid activation for classification. MLPs can approximate arbitrary non-linear functions and capture complex feature interactions that tree-based and linear models may miss. However, MLPs require careful tuning of architecture (number and width of hidden layers), learning rate, regularization (dropout, weight decay), and batch size, and they are more prone to overfitting on small-to-moderate datasets than ensemble methods [49,50].

Support Vector Machines (SVM) seek to find the hyperplane that maximizes the margin between classes in a high-dimensional feature space [51]. The kernel trick enables SVMs to implicitly map features into higher-dimensional spaces where linear separation may be achievable, with the Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel being the most commonly used non-linear kernel. SVMs are effective in high-dimensional spaces and are relatively resistant to overfitting in cases where the number of features exceeds the number of samples. However, SVMs are computationally expensive for large datasets, sensitive to feature scaling, and their probabilistic outputs (obtained through Platt scaling) may be less well-calibrated than natively probabilistic models [51,52].

2.3.3 Model Evaluation Metrics

Rigorous evaluation of classification models requires a comprehensive suite of metrics that capture different aspects of predictive performance [53,54].

The Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC, or simply AUC) is the most widely used discrimination metric in clinical prediction modeling. The ROC curve plots sensitivity (true positive rate) against 1-specificity (false positive rate) across all possible classification thresholds, and the AUC quantifies the probability that a randomly chosen positive instance is ranked higher than a randomly chosen negative instance [53]. An AUC of 0.5 represents random prediction, while an AUC of 1.0 represents perfect discrimination. In clinical prediction modeling, AUC values of 0.7–0.8 are generally considered acceptable, 0.8–0.9 as excellent, and above 0.9 as outstanding, though the clinical utility depends on the specific application [54].

Accuracy represents the overall proportion of correct predictions but can be misleading in imbalanced datasets, where a classifier that predicts only the majority class can achieve deceptively high accuracy [53]. Sensitivity (recall, true positive rate) measures the proportion of actual positives correctly identified, which in the micro-TESE context represents the ability to identify patients who will have successful sperm retrieval. Specificity (true negative rate) measures the proportion of actual negatives correctly identified, representing the ability to identify patients who will not have successful retrieval. Positive Predictive Value (PPV, or precision) represents the proportion of positive predictions that are truly positive, while Negative Predictive Value (NPV) represents the proportion of negative predictions that are truly negative [53].

The F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a balanced measure when both false positives and false negatives are clinically important [53]. Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) provides a balanced measure that accounts for all four cells of the confusion matrix and is particularly informative for imbalanced datasets [55].

The Brier score measures the mean squared difference between predicted probabilities and actual outcomes, combining calibration and discrimination into a single metric [56]. A lower Brier score indicates better overall probabilistic prediction, with 0 representing perfect calibration and discrimination and 0.25 representing random prediction for balanced datasets. The non-informative reference Brier score (based solely on prevalence) provides a baseline for comparison.

2.3.4 Cross-Validation Strategies

Cross-validation (CV) is the standard approach for estimating generalization performance and selecting hyperparameters in ML models [57,58]. Simple k-fold CV divides the dataset into k equally sized folds, trains the model on k−1 folds, and evaluates on the held-out fold, rotating through all folds and averaging performance. Stratified k-fold CV ensures that each fold preserves the class distribution of the overall dataset, which is critical for imbalanced datasets [57].

However, using the same CV procedure for both hyperparameter selection and performance estimation introduces optimistic bias, as the performance estimate is inflated by the overfitting of hyperparameters to the specific CV splits [58]. Nested cross-validation addresses this bias by employing two nested loops: an outer loop for performance estimation and an inner loop for hyperparameter optimization. In 5×5 nested CV, the outer loop divides the data into 5 folds, and for each outer fold, the inner loop performs a 5-fold CV on the remaining 4 outer folds to select the optimal hyperparameters, which are then used to train a model that is evaluated on the held-out outer fold [58]. This procedure provides a nearly unbiased estimate of generalization performance and is considered the gold standard for model evaluation when sample sizes are insufficient for a large independent test set [58].

2.3.5 Hyperparameter Optimization

The performance of most ML algorithms is sensitive to hyperparameter settings, and systematic optimization is essential for fair model comparison. Grid search evaluates all possible combinations of hyperparameters from predefined grids but is computationally expensive and scales exponentially with the number of hyperparameters [59]. Random search samples random combinations from the hyperparameter space and has been shown to be more efficient than grid search, particularly when some hyperparameters are more important than others [60]. Bayesian optimization uses a probabilistic surrogate model (typically a Gaussian process or tree-structured Parzen estimator) to model the relationship between hyperparameters and performance, sequentially selecting the most promising configurations based on an acquisition function that balances exploration and exploitation [61]. Bayesian optimization is particularly efficient for expensive-to-evaluate models and complex hyperparameter spaces, typically achieving near-optimal configurations in 50–100 iterations.

2.3.6 Handling Class Imbalance

Class imbalance, in which one outcome class is substantially more prevalent than the other, is a common challenge in clinical prediction modeling that can bias ML algorithms toward predicting the majority class [62]. In the present study, the failure-to-success ratio of 1.72:1 (63.3% failure, 36.7% success) represents moderate imbalance that, while less extreme than some clinical applications (e.g., rare disease screening), can nonetheless impact model performance.

Several strategies have been developed to address class imbalance [62,63]. Resampling techniques include oversampling the minority class, undersampling the majority class, or hybrid approaches. The Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) is the most widely used oversampling method, generating synthetic minority class instances by interpolating between existing minority instances and their nearest neighbors in feature space [63]. SMOTE and its variants can improve sensitivity and F1 score but must be applied carefully to avoid data leakage: synthetic instances must be generated only from training data within cross-validation folds, never from the full dataset before splitting. Algorithm-level approaches include adjusting class weights in the loss function to penalize misclassification of the minority class more heavily, and threshold optimization to select operating points that balance sensitivity and specificity according to clinical priorities [62]. The Youden index (J = sensitivity + specificity − 1) is a common criterion for threshold selection that maximizes the sum of sensitivity and specificity [64].

2.3.7 Feature Selection and Engineering

Feature selection aims to identify the most informative subset of features for prediction, reducing dimensionality, mitigating overfitting, improving interpretability, and potentially enhancing generalization [65]. Filter methods (e.g., univariate statistical tests, mutual information, correlation analysis) rank features independently of the classifier. Wrapper methods (e.g., forward selection, backward elimination, recursive feature elimination) evaluate feature subsets using the classifier's performance but are computationally expensive. Embedded methods (e.g., L1 regularization, tree-based feature importance) integrate feature selection into the model training process [65].

Feature engineering, the process of creating new informative features from raw data, is equally critical and often more impactful than algorithm selection [66]. Domain-specific feature engineering, such as deriving the T/E2 ratio from testosterone and estradiol measurements or extracting quantitative histopathological indicators from free-text pathology reports through natural language processing (NLP), can capture clinically meaningful information that may not be accessible from raw features alone.

2.3.8 Overfitting Prevention and Data Leakage

Overfitting, in which a model learns noise and idiosyncrasies in the training data rather than generalizable patterns, is a pervasive risk in ML, particularly with small-to-moderate sample sizes and high-dimensional feature spaces [67]. Common overfitting prevention strategies include regularization (L1, L2, or elastic net penalties), early stopping (terminating training when validation performance ceases to improve), ensemble methods (which reduce variance through aggregation), dropout in neural networks, and limiting model complexity (tree depth, number of iterations) [67].

Data leakage, defined as the introduction of information from outside the training set into the model during training, is a more insidious source of inflated performance estimates [68,69]. Leakage can occur through numerous pathways, including applying preprocessing steps (feature scaling, imputation, feature selection) to the full dataset before splitting, using the test set for hyperparameter tuning, or including features that are causally downstream of the outcome [68,69]. The consequences of data leakage can be severe: published models with inflated performance estimates may fail catastrophically in clinical deployment, eroding trust in ML-based prediction tools [69]. Rigorous pipeline design, in which all preprocessing steps are encapsulated within cross-validation folds and no information from held-out data is used during training, is essential for producing credible and deployable models [68,69].


2.4 Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI)

2.4.1 The Black-Box Problem in Clinical Machine Learning

The increasing adoption of complex ML models in clinical decision-making has intensified concerns about the "black-box" problem — the inability of end users to understand why a model makes a particular prediction [70,71]. While high-performance models such as gradient-boosted ensembles and neural networks can achieve excellent discrimination, their internal decision processes involve thousands of parameters, non-linear transformations, and complex feature interactions that are opaque to clinicians. This opacity poses fundamental challenges for clinical adoption: clinicians are reluctant to rely on predictions they cannot interpret, patients have a right to understand the reasoning behind recommendations that affect their healthcare, and regulatory frameworks increasingly demand transparency and accountability in AI-assisted medical decisions [70,71].

The tension between model performance and interpretability — sometimes described as the accuracy-interpretability trade-off — has motivated the development of a rich ecosystem of Explainable AI (XAI) methods that aim to make complex models transparent without sacrificing predictive performance [72]. The clinical imperative for explainability is particularly acute in high-stakes decisions such as micro-TESE counseling, where the prediction influences whether a couple proceeds with an invasive, emotionally charged, and costly surgical procedure.

2.4.2 Taxonomy of XAI Methods

XAI methods can be classified along two principal axes: model-agnostic versus model-specific, and global versus local [72,73].

Model-agnostic methods can be applied to any ML model, treating it as a "black box" and analyzing only the relationship between inputs and outputs. LIME and permutation importance are examples of model-agnostic approaches. Model-specific methods exploit the internal structure of a particular algorithm to provide more efficient or more faithful explanations; for instance, TreeSHAP leverages the tree structure of gradient-boosted ensembles to compute exact Shapley values in polynomial time [73,74].

Global explanations characterize the overall behavior of the model across the entire dataset, identifying which features are generally most important and how they influence predictions on average. Local explanations, in contrast, explain individual predictions, identifying which features drove a particular patient's predicted probability up or down relative to the baseline [72,73]. Both types of explanation are essential for clinical applications: global explanations help clinicians understand and validate the model's learned relationships, while local explanations support individualized patient counseling.

2.4.3 SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)

SHAP, introduced by Lundberg and Lee in 2017, provides a unified framework for feature attribution based on Shapley values from cooperative game theory [74]. The Shapley value for a feature is defined as the weighted average of the marginal contribution of that feature to the prediction across all possible feature subsets, satisfying four desirable properties: efficiency (the feature contributions sum to the difference between the prediction and the baseline), symmetry (features with identical contributions receive identical values), dummy (features that contribute nothing receive a zero value), and additivity (the contributions are additive across coalitions) [74].

For tree-based models, the TreeSHAP algorithm exploits the recursive tree structure to compute exact Shapley values in O(TLD²) time, where T is the number of trees, L is the maximum number of leaves, and D is the maximum tree depth [75]. This efficiency enables SHAP analysis of large ensembles with hundreds or thousands of trees on clinical datasets of moderate size in practical computation time.

SHAP provides both global and local explanations. Global SHAP importance is computed as the mean absolute SHAP value for each feature across all instances, ranking features by their average impact on predictions. SHAP summary plots visualize the distribution of SHAP values for each feature, showing both importance (horizontal spread) and direction of effect (color coding). Local SHAP waterfall or force plots decompose an individual prediction into additive feature contributions, showing which features pushed the prediction up or down relative to the population average [74,75].

In clinical applications, SHAP has become the dominant XAI framework for tabular data due to its theoretical rigor, additive property, and the availability of efficient implementations for tree-based models. However, SHAP is not without limitations: it can be sensitive to feature correlations (where the attribution may be distributed unpredictably among correlated features), and the computational cost scales with the number of features and the model complexity [74].

2.4.4 LIME (Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations)

LIME, proposed by Ribeiro, Singh, and Guestrin in 2016, provides local explanations by approximating the complex model's behavior in the neighborhood of a specific instance with a simple, interpretable surrogate model (typically a sparse linear regression or decision tree) [76]. The LIME algorithm perturbs the instance of interest by sampling nearby points, obtains the complex model's predictions for these perturbed instances, and fits a weighted interpretable model to these predictions, where the weights decrease with distance from the original instance. The resulting sparse linear model identifies the features most influential for the specific prediction [76].

LIME is model-agnostic and can be applied to any classifier without knowledge of its internal structure. It produces intuitive explanations in the form of feature weights indicating the direction and magnitude of each feature's contribution to the local prediction. However, LIME has known limitations: the explanations can be unstable (sensitive to the random perturbation process), the fidelity of the local surrogate depends on the complexity of the underlying model's decision boundary in the neighborhood, and the choice of kernel width and number of features in the surrogate model introduces additional hyperparameters that can affect the explanation [76,77].

The complementary use of SHAP and LIME provides a more robust assessment of feature importance than either method alone. When both methods identify the same features as important for a given prediction, confidence in the explanation is strengthened; discordances between the two methods highlight regions of the feature space where explanations may be less reliable [77].

2.4.5 Partial Dependence Plots (PDP)

Partial Dependence Plots (PDP), introduced by Friedman in 2001, characterize the marginal effect of one or two features on the model's prediction, averaging over the values of all other features [78,79]. For a feature of interest, the PDP plots the average predicted probability as a function of that feature's values, while holding all other features at their observed distribution. This reveals the functional form of the relationship learned by the model — whether it is linear, monotonic, U-shaped, or exhibits threshold effects — providing insights that complement the importance rankings from SHAP and LIME [78,79].

PDPs are particularly valuable in clinical settings because they can reveal whether the model's learned relationships are biologically plausible. For example, a PDP showing that predicted sperm retrieval probability decreases monotonically with increasing FSH would be consistent with the known biology of FSH elevation as a marker of testicular failure [78]. Conversely, unexpected or counterintuitive PDP shapes may indicate model artifacts, confounding, or data quality issues that warrant further investigation.

Limitations of PDP include the assumption of feature independence (the averaged marginal effects may not accurately represent the feature's effect in the presence of strong correlations), the inability to capture heterogeneity in feature effects across different subgroups (individual conditional expectation [ICE] plots address this limitation), and the restriction to one or two features at a time [78,79,80].

2.4.6 Calibration Methods

Model calibration refers to the agreement between predicted probabilities and observed outcome frequencies [81,82]. A well-calibrated model produces probability estimates that accurately reflect the true likelihood of the event: if the model predicts a 40% probability of sperm retrieval for a group of patients, approximately 40% of those patients should indeed have successful retrieval. Calibration is distinct from discrimination: a model can achieve excellent AUC (ability to rank patients correctly) while being poorly calibrated (producing inaccurate probability estimates) [81,82].

Calibration is assessed visually through reliability diagrams (calibration plots), which group predictions into deciles and plot observed frequencies against mean predicted probabilities, and quantitatively through the Brier score (which combines calibration and discrimination) and the Hosmer-Lemeshow test [56,81,82]. Post-hoc recalibration methods can improve the calibration of poorly calibrated models without retraining. Platt scaling fits a logistic regression model to map the model's raw outputs to calibrated probabilities, assuming a sigmoid relationship between raw scores and true probabilities [83]. Isotonic regression fits a non-parametric, monotonically increasing function, providing more flexibility but potentially overfitting with small calibration datasets [84]. Platt scaling is generally preferred for smooth, monotonic calibration corrections, while isotonic regression may introduce step artifacts [83,84].

Adequate calibration is a prerequisite for clinical deployment of prediction models, as treatment decisions based on predicted probabilities require that these probabilities accurately reflect the true risk [81,82].

2.4.7 Decision Curve Analysis (DCA)

Decision Curve Analysis (DCA), proposed by Vickers and Elkin in 2006, provides a framework for evaluating the clinical utility of prediction models by quantifying the net benefit across a range of clinically reasonable threshold probabilities [85]. The threshold probability represents the minimum predicted probability at which a clinician or patient would choose to proceed with the intervention (in this context, micro-TESE). Net benefit is defined as the weighted difference between the proportion of true positives (correctly identified successful retrievals) and the proportion of false positives (incorrectly predicted successful retrievals), weighted by the ratio of the harm of a false positive to the benefit of a true positive [85].

DCA compares the net benefit of using the prediction model against two reference strategies: "treat all" (perform micro-TESE on all NOA patients regardless of predicted probability) and "treat none" (withhold micro-TESE from all patients). A model with clinical utility should demonstrate net benefit exceeding both reference strategies across a range of clinically relevant threshold probabilities [85,86]. DCA is increasingly recognized as essential for evaluating clinical prediction models, as it directly quantifies the clinical implications of using the model for decision-making, going beyond statistical metrics like AUC and calibration to address the question: "Would using this model improve clinical outcomes?" [85,86].

2.4.8 Clinical Trust and Adoption Requirements

The translation of ML prediction models from research prototypes to clinical tools requires more than statistical validation; it demands clinical trust, which is built through transparency, biological plausibility, external validation, and seamless integration into clinical workflows [87,88]. Clinicians are more likely to adopt predictive tools when they can understand and verify the model's reasoning (enabled by XAI), when the learned relationships are consistent with established pathophysiology, when the model has been validated in external populations, and when the tool is presented through intuitive interfaces that integrate into existing clinical workflows without disrupting practice patterns [87,88].

A clinical decision support system (CDSS) that operationalizes a prediction model must therefore incorporate not only the predictive output but also transparent explanations, calibrated probability estimates, reference ranges for clinical context, and visual indicators that support rapid clinical interpretation [88]. The design of such a system, informed by the XAI and calibration analyses described in this thesis, is presented in subsequent chapters.


2.5 Systematic Scoping Review: AI in Reproductive Medicine

As a foundational component of this doctoral research program, we conducted a systematic scoping review to comprehensively map the existing literature on artificial intelligence predictive models and advancements in microdissection testicular sperm extraction for non-obstructive azoospermia. This review was published in *Human Reproduction Open* in 2025 [2] and established the evidentiary basis upon which the subsequent analytical work of this thesis was built.

2.5.1 Search Strategy and Databases

The scoping review followed the methodological framework proposed by Arksey and O'Malley and refined by the Joanna Briggs Institute, adhering to the PRISMA extension for scoping reviews (PRISMA-ScR) [2]. A comprehensive literature search was conducted across multiple electronic databases, including PubMed/MEDLINE, Scopus, Web of Science, IEEE Xplore, and the Cochrane Library, spanning from inception through December 2024. The search strategy combined medical subject headings (MeSH) and free-text terms related to three conceptual domains: (1) non-obstructive azoospermia and micro-TESE (e.g., "non-obstructive azoospermia," "microdissection testicular sperm extraction," "micro-TESE," "sperm retrieval"), (2) artificial intelligence and machine learning (e.g., "machine learning," "artificial intelligence," "deep learning," "prediction model," "neural network," "random forest," "gradient boosting"), and (3) clinical outcomes (e.g., "sperm retrieval rate," "prediction," "prognosis"). Boolean operators were used to combine search terms within and across domains, and database-specific syntax was adapted for each platform [2].

Reference lists of included studies and relevant review articles were manually searched to identify additional eligible publications. No language restrictions were applied during the initial search, though the final included studies were in English or had English abstracts with sufficient methodological detail for data extraction.

2.5.2 Study Selection and PRISMA Flow

A two-stage screening process was employed. First, titles and abstracts were independently screened by two reviewers, with discrepancies resolved by a third reviewer. Second, full texts of potentially eligible studies were retrieved and assessed against predefined inclusion criteria: (1) studies involving patients with NOA, (2) studies employing AI/ML methods for prediction or classification, (3) studies with quantitative outcome measures related to sperm retrieval or related clinical endpoints. Exclusion criteria included studies focused exclusively on obstructive azoospermia, studies using AI solely for image analysis without clinical prediction, case reports, and editorials or commentaries without original data [2].

The PRISMA flow diagram documented the number of records identified, screened, assessed for eligibility, and included at each stage, providing a transparent audit trail of the study selection process.

2.5.3 Findings from the Review

The scoping review identified a growing but still limited body of literature on AI applications in NOA prediction and micro-TESE outcomes. Several key themes emerged from the review:

First, the number of studies applying ML to micro-TESE prediction was small, reflecting the nascent state of this interdisciplinary field. Most studies were published after 2020, indicating rapid recent growth in research interest [2]. Second, sample sizes were predominantly small, with most studies enrolling fewer than 500 patients, limiting statistical power, model complexity, and generalizability. Third, the diversity of ML algorithms evaluated in individual studies was limited, with most studies comparing only 1–3 algorithms (typically logistic regression against a single ML method). Fourth, validation strategies were heterogeneous and often insufficient, with many studies relying on simple train-test splits or basic k-fold cross-validation without nested procedures or external validation cohorts. Fifth, the integration of explainability methods was rare, with most studies reporting only model performance metrics without any formal assessment of feature importance or model interpretability [2].

2.5.4 Trends in AI Adoption in Andrology

The review also documented broader trends in AI adoption in andrology beyond micro-TESE prediction. Applications included computer-assisted semen analysis (CASA) for sperm motility and morphology assessment, deep learning-based sperm identification and classification from microscopy images, prediction of IVF/ICSI outcomes using ML, and automated quality control of embryology laboratory procedures [2]. These applications represent different stages of maturity, ranging from well-established CASA systems to emerging experimental applications.

A notable finding was the limited translation of AI research in andrology from proof-of-concept studies to clinical deployment. The gap between model development and clinical integration was attributed to several factors, including insufficient sample sizes for robust validation, lack of external validation, absence of explainability, inadequate reporting quality, and the absence of structured clinical decision support frameworks that could facilitate bedside use [2].

2.5.5 Quality Assessment

The methodological quality of included studies was assessed using adapted tools appropriate for prediction model studies. Several recurring quality concerns were identified: risk of overfitting due to small sample sizes with relatively large feature sets, inadequate reporting of missing data handling, failure to account for class imbalance, and lack of calibration assessment [2]. These findings directly informed the design of the present study, which was conceived to address each of the identified methodological limitations comprehensively.


2.6 Critical Analysis of Prior Studies on ML for Micro-TESE Prediction

A detailed analysis of the 14 studies identified through our scoping review and subsequent literature monitoring reveals both the promise and the substantial methodological limitations of existing ML approaches to micro-TESE prediction. This section presents a comprehensive study-by-study analysis followed by a cross-study synthesis highlighting recurrent gaps.

2.6.1 Individual Study Analysis

**Li et al. (2026)** published in *Translational Andrology and Urology* a study of 167 men that introduced rete testis thickness (RTT) as a novel non-invasive ultrasonographic predictor of sperm retrieval outcomes [13]. Using binary logistic regression, the authors combined RTT with FSH and LH to achieve a notably high AUC of 0.950. While the introduction of RTT as a novel biomarker is a valuable contribution, the study is limited by its small sample size (n=167), the use of only a single statistical method (logistic regression), the absence of cross-validation or nested resampling, and the lack of any ML algorithms beyond the baseline logistic model. The exceptionally high AUC in such a small sample raises concerns about potential overfitting, and the result warrants replication in larger cohorts before clinical adoption.

**Bachelot et al. (2023)** published in the *Journal of Medical Internet Research* a study of 201 patients (175 training, 26 prospective temporal validation) that compared eight ML models, with Random Forest emerging as the best performer (AUC 0.900) [6]. The study identified inhibin B and varicocele history as the most predictive features and employed temporal validation, which is a methodological strength. However, the sample size remains small, the temporal validation set of only 26 patients is insufficient for reliable performance estimation, and the study did not employ nested cross-validation. The absence of formal XAI assessment and calibration analysis limits the interpretability and clinical utility assessment of the findings.

**Zeadna et al. (2020)** published in *Human Reproduction* one of the early ML applications in this domain, comparing gradient-boosted trees (GBT) with logistic regression in 119 patients, achieving an AUC of 0.807 for GBT with high sensitivity (91%) but low specificity (51%) [5]. A significant limitation is that this study was based on conventional TESE (c-TESE) rather than micro-TESE, limiting its direct applicability to the micro-TESE context. The small sample size, limited algorithm comparison (only two models), and absence of nested CV or external validation further constrain the generalizability of the findings.

**Liu et al. (2023)** published in *Reproductive and Developmental Medicine* a study of 217 patients using an integrative logistic regression model that achieved AUC 0.781 [9]. The study identified Klinefelter syndrome history, cryptorchidism, FSH levels, and testicular pathology as key predictors. While the clinical features identified are consistent with established prognostic factors, the restriction to a single modeling approach (logistic regression) limits the exploration of non-linear relationships, and the modest sample size constrains model complexity.

**Kobayashi et al. (2023)** published in *BioMed Research International* a study of 430 training patients plus 20 test patients that used an AutoML tool combining gradient-boosted trees and artificial neural networks, achieving AUC 0.725 [7]. The T/E2 ratio emerged as the most important feature, consistent with its known biological significance. Strengths include the larger training sample and the use of AutoML for systematic model selection. However, the test set of only 20 patients is severely underpowered for reliable performance estimation, and the AutoML approach, while convenient, provides limited transparency regarding the specific model architectures and hyperparameters selected.

**Deng et al. (2023)** published in the *Asian Journal of Andrology* a study of 200 patients using binary logistic regression, achieving AUC 0.720, with AMH, inhibin B, Klinefelter syndrome, Y-microdeletion, and cryptorchidism identified as significant predictors [8]. The restriction to logistic regression and the modest sample size are consistent limitations. However, the inclusion of AMH and inhibin B as predictors provides information about biomarkers not available in all clinical settings.

**Ceyhan et al. (2025)** published in *Urology* a multi-center nomogram study involving 3,093 patients — the largest sample in the literature — using multivariate logistic regression to develop a nomogram for predicting testicular sperm extraction success [10]. Testicular volume and age emerged as the most impactful features. The multi-center design and large sample size are significant strengths. However, the study did not employ ML algorithms beyond logistic regression, did not report AUC for a direct comparison with ML approaches, and the nomogram format, while clinically intuitive, does not capture the complex non-linear interactions that ML methods can model.

**Ory et al. (2022)** published in the *World Journal of Men's Health* a study of 240 patients (160 training, 80 external validation) using Random Forest to predict sperm parameter upgrade after varicocelectomy, achieving AUC 0.720 [89]. While not directly predicting micro-TESE outcomes, this study is relevant to the broader landscape of ML applications in male infertility. The external validation cohort is a methodological strength that is absent from most other studies in this field.

**Goss et al. (2026)** published in *Fertility and Sterility* a proof-of-concept study involving 22 patients that developed a deep learning "co-pilot" for real-time rare sperm identification during micro-TESE [90]. This innovative application represents a fundamentally different use case from preoperative prediction — intraoperative assistance rather than preoperative counseling — and the extremely small sample size reflects its proof-of-concept nature. The study demonstrates the potential for AI to augment surgical decision-making during the procedure itself, complementing preoperative prediction tools.

**Cao et al. (2024)** published in *BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making* a study of 345 patients using XGBoost with SHAP to predict clinical pregnancy following sperm retrieval and ICSI, achieving AUC 0.825 [91]. This study focuses on a downstream outcome (pregnancy) rather than sperm retrieval itself, but is notable for its integration of SHAP-based explainability, identifying female age as the most important predictor. The inclusion of XAI methods represents a methodological advancement compared to most studies in the field.

**Xiao et al. (2025)** published in *Scientific Reports* a study of 352 patients using a gradient-boosted decision tree (GBDT) nomogram to differentiate NOA from OA, achieving an impressively high AUC of 0.974 [92]. FSH, inhibin B, testicular volume, and semen pH were identified as key discriminators. While the clinical question (differential diagnosis of NOA vs. OA) differs from sperm retrieval prediction, the identified features overlap substantially with those used in micro-TESE prediction models.

**Verratti et al. (2024)** published in *Current Urology Reports* a comprehensive narrative review of AI applications in male infertility [93]. The review highlighted the predominance of single-center studies, the need for robust multi-center validation, and the limited clinical translation of existing ML models. The authors emphasized the gap between model development and clinical deployment as a major barrier to the field's progress.

**Ibañez Vazquez et al. (2026)** published in *Frontiers in Urology* a review article on AI for sperm analysis and assisted reproductive technology [94]. The review discussed CASA limitations, the promise of deep learning for automated semen analysis, and the emerging role of ML in predicting ART outcomes. The authors called for standardized reporting, larger cohorts, and prospective validation studies.

**Kam et al. (2025)** published in the *World Journal of Men's Health* a state-of-the-art review on AI in NOA that covered both preoperative prediction and real-time intraoperative sperm identification [1]. The review provided a comprehensive synthesis of the field, highlighting the heterogeneity of approaches, the limited sample sizes, and the need for integrated frameworks that combine prediction, explainability, and clinical decision support.

2.6.2 Cross-Study Synthesis and Gap Analysis

The critical analysis of these 14 studies reveals several recurrent and interconnected limitations that collectively define the research gap addressed by this thesis:

**Small sample sizes:** The majority of prediction studies enrolled fewer than 500 patients, with many below 250. The largest prediction-focused sample (Ceyhan et al., n=3,093) used only logistic regression without ML algorithms. Small samples limit the complexity of models that can be trained without overfitting, restrict the number of features that can be included, and produce unstable performance estimates with wide confidence intervals.

**Limited model diversity:** Most studies compared only 1–3 algorithms, typically logistic regression versus a single ML method (most commonly Random Forest or gradient-boosted trees). No study prior to the present work benchmarked a comprehensive set of models spanning all major ML paradigms (linear, tree-based, boosting, ensemble, neural network, instance-based, kernel, probabilistic). This narrow comparison prevents the identification of the optimal algorithm for the specific dataset characteristics.

**Lack of proper validation:** Nested cross-validation, the gold standard for unbiased performance estimation when sample sizes preclude a large independent test set, was not employed in any of the 14 studies. Most studies relied on simple train-test splits or basic k-fold CV, both of which produce optimistically biased performance estimates when combined with hyperparameter tuning.

**Missing XAI integration:** With the notable exception of Cao et al. (who applied SHAP), formal explainability analysis was absent from prediction studies. The lack of multi-method XAI (combining SHAP, LIME, and PDP) means that feature importance rankings have not been validated for concordance across methodologically independent explanation approaches, limiting confidence in the robustness of identified predictors.

**No CDSS development:** None of the identified studies translated their prediction models into a structured clinical decision support system with defined reference ranges, validation logic, and a user-facing interface. The absence of a translational framework represents a critical gap between model development and clinical utility.

**Absence of NLP for pathology reports:** Testicular histopathology is one of the strongest prognostic factors for micro-TESE outcomes, yet existing ML studies either excluded pathology information entirely (due to its free-text format) or used coarsely simplified categorical representations. No prior study employed NLP to systematically extract quantitative histopathological indicators from bilateral pathology reports, despite the rich prognostic information these reports contain.

**Limited feature sets:** Most studies utilized a restricted set of predictors (typically 5–15 features), reflecting both the small sample sizes and the absence of systematic feature engineering. The inclusion of NLP-derived pathology features, derived hormonal ratios, and comprehensive anthropometric and sonographic measurements in the present study expands the feature set to 73 features, enabling a more thorough exploration of the predictive landscape.

**Inconsistent reporting quality:** Many studies did not report essential information such as confidence intervals for AUC, calibration assessment, class imbalance handling strategies, or missing data treatment. This inconsistency makes it difficult to compare studies fairly and assess their reliability. The adoption of standardized reporting frameworks such as TRIPOD+AI [95] and PROBAST+AI [96] in the present study addresses this gap.


2.7 Rationale for Selection of 16 Machine Learning Models

2.7.1 Dataset Characteristics and Their Implications for Model Selection

The design of the machine learning benchmarking strategy in this thesis was driven by the specific characteristics of the analytical dataset, which informed both the selection of algorithms and the rationale for comprehensive benchmarking across all major ML paradigms. The dataset comprises 2,413 NOA patients with 73 features (55 original and 18 NLP-engineered), a binary outcome (sperm retrieval success/failure), mixed feature types (continuous hormonal and anthropometric measurements, categorical variables such as genetic diagnoses and clinical history, and ordinal NLP-derived histopathological indicators), moderate class imbalance (36.7% success rate, yielding a failure-to-success ratio of approximately 1.72:1), and a tabular structure with no temporal, sequential, or image-based components [97].

These characteristics have important implications for algorithm selection. First, the tabular data structure favors algorithms specifically designed for structured data over deep learning architectures optimized for images, text, or sequences. Extensive benchmarking studies have demonstrated that gradient-boosted tree ensembles consistently outperform deep learning on tabular data of moderate size [98,99]. Second, the mixed feature types require algorithms that can handle both continuous and categorical variables natively or through appropriate encoding. Third, the moderate class imbalance necessitates strategies that prevent majority-class bias. Fourth, the moderate sample size (n=2,413) is sufficient for training moderately complex models but not for training very deep or very wide neural networks without overfitting risk. Fifth, the relatively high-dimensional feature space (73 features) requires regularization or feature selection to prevent the curse of dimensionality.

Given these characteristics, a comprehensive benchmarking approach was adopted, evaluating 16 distinct models spanning all major ML paradigms. The rationale for this comprehensive approach, rather than selecting a single "best guess" algorithm, is threefold: (1) no algorithm dominates all datasets, and the optimal choice depends on the specific data characteristics (the "no free lunch" theorem); (2) comprehensive benchmarking provides empirical evidence for algorithm selection rather than relying on assumptions; and (3) the comparison across paradigms reveals the nature of the predictive signal — whether it is primarily linear, tree-decomposable, locally structured, or requires complex feature interactions.

2.7.2 Gradient Boosting Family (CatBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, Gradient Boosting Machine)

The gradient boosting family represents the current state of the art for tabular classification and was hypothesized a priori as the most promising algorithmic class for this dataset. Four gradient boosting implementations were included to enable a fine-grained comparison within this dominant paradigm.

**CatBoost** was selected for its native handling of categorical features through ordered target statistics, which is directly relevant to the mixed feature types in this dataset [45]. The presence of categorical variables such as genetic diagnoses (Klinefelter syndrome, Y-microdeletion subtypes), clinical history (cryptorchidism, varicocele), and ordinal NLP-derived histopathological categories makes CatBoost's native categorical handling a theoretical advantage over algorithms that require manual encoding. CatBoost's ordered boosting mechanism provides additional robustness against overfitting, particularly valuable at the moderate sample size of 2,413 observations. Furthermore, CatBoost's compatibility with TreeSHAP enables efficient computation of exact Shapley values for explainability. In the context of the 16-model ensemble, CatBoost represents the paradigm of categorical-aware boosting with theoretical overfitting guarantees.

**XGBoost** was included as the most widely validated gradient boosting implementation, with extensive track record in clinical prediction modeling and Kaggle competitions [43]. Its regularization framework (L1 and L2 penalties on leaf weights), approximate split-finding algorithms, and native missing-value handling are well-suited to the dataset characteristics. XGBoost's level-wise tree growth strategy produces balanced trees that may generalize differently than the leaf-wise trees of LightGBM, providing a complementary perspective within the boosting family.

**LightGBM** was selected for its histogram-based split-finding algorithm and gradient-based one-side sampling (GOSS), which enable efficient training on the full 73-feature space [44]. Its leaf-wise growth strategy can produce more complex trees with fewer iterations, potentially capturing deeper feature interactions. LightGBM's exclusive feature bundling (EFB) is particularly relevant for the sparse features created by missingness indicators and one-hot encoding.

**Gradient Boosting Machine (GBM)** was included as the scikit-learn implementation of the classic gradient boosting framework, providing a reference implementation without the advanced optimizations of CatBoost, XGBoost, and LightGBM [42]. Comparing GBM against the optimized implementations reveals the incremental value of specific algorithmic innovations (native categorical handling, histogram-based splits, ordered boosting) over the foundational algorithm.

2.7.3 Ensemble Aggregation Methods (Random Forest, Extra Trees)

**Random Forest** was selected as the archetypal bagging ensemble, providing variance reduction through bootstrap aggregation and random feature selection [39]. RF's robustness to outliers, ability to capture non-linear interactions, and natural out-of-bag error estimation make it a strong baseline for tree-based prediction. In clinical settings, RF has been one of the most successfully applied ML algorithms, and its inclusion enables direct comparison with the boosting paradigm. In the context of the present study, RF represents the paradigm of parallel ensemble aggregation, contrasting with the sequential learning of boosting methods.

**Extra Trees** extends the RF concept by introducing additional randomization in the split-threshold selection, creating greater diversity among ensemble members and often producing smoother decision boundaries [40]. Extra Trees was included to evaluate whether the additional randomization improves generalization beyond standard RF on this dataset, and to represent the extreme of the randomization spectrum within ensemble methods.

2.7.4 Boosting with Weak Learners (AdaBoost)

**AdaBoost** was included as the foundational boosting algorithm, employing instance reweighting rather than gradient-based optimization [41]. AdaBoost's focus on misclassified instances makes it sensitive to noisy data and outliers, but also enables it to adapt to difficult-to-classify patients. Its inclusion provides a historical reference point and evaluates whether the simple reweighting mechanism is sufficient for this dataset or whether gradient-based approaches provide meaningful improvement.

2.7.5 Single Decision Tree

**Decision Tree** was included as the simplest tree-based model and serves as an interpretability anchor [38]. A single decision tree provides fully transparent, rule-based predictions that can be directly visualized and verified by clinicians. While a single tree is expected to underperform ensemble methods due to high variance and overfitting, its inclusion quantifies the improvement obtained through ensembling and establishes a lower bound on tree-based prediction performance for this dataset.

2.7.6 Linear Models (Logistic Regression, SGD Classifier)

**Logistic Regression** serves as the fundamental baseline classifier and the de facto standard in clinical research [35]. Its inclusion is essential for determining whether the additional complexity of ML models provides meaningful improvement over a linear predictor — a critical question for clinical adoption, as LR's full interpretability represents a significant practical advantage. LR with regularization also provides feature selection (L1) and multicollinearity handling (L2) capabilities. In the context of the 16-model comparison, LR represents the paradigm of linear, additive effects and serves as the benchmark against which more complex models must justify their opacity.

**SGD Classifier** was included as a scalable linear classifier that optimizes through stochastic gradient descent, supporting various loss functions and regularization penalties [37]. Its inclusion provides a computationally efficient alternative to standard LR and enables evaluation of different regularization strategies within the linear paradigm.

2.7.7 Support Vector Machine (SVM with RBF Kernel)

**SVM with RBF kernel** was selected to represent the kernel methods paradigm, which implicitly maps features into a high-dimensional reproducing kernel Hilbert space where non-linear decision boundaries become linear separations [51]. SVMs are particularly effective when the decision boundary is complex but smooth, and the RBF kernel is the most commonly used non-linear kernel for general-purpose classification. SVM's margin-maximization objective provides theoretical generalization guarantees that complement the empirical risk minimization approaches of ensemble methods. The inclusion of SVM evaluates whether kernel-based non-linear transformations capture predictive information that tree-based methods miss.

2.7.8 Instance-Based Method (KNN)

**K-Nearest Neighbors** was included to represent the instance-based learning paradigm, which makes predictions based on local similarity in the feature space without learning explicit model parameters [46]. KNN's non-parametric nature and local prediction mechanism make it uniquely sensitive to the local geometry of the feature space. In the micro-TESE context, KNN evaluates whether patients with similar clinical profiles (nearest neighbors) tend to have similar outcomes, providing a different lens on the data than parametric or tree-based methods. KNN's performance also serves as an indicator of the local structure in the feature space: poor KNN performance may suggest that the predictive signal is distributed globally rather than locally.

2.7.9 Neural Network (Multi-Layer Perceptron)

**Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)** was included to represent the neural network paradigm and evaluate whether deep feature interactions beyond those captured by tree splits contribute to prediction [49,50]. The MLP's universal function approximation capability makes it theoretically capable of learning arbitrarily complex feature relationships, though in practice its performance on tabular data of moderate size is often inferior to gradient-boosted ensembles due to the difficulty of optimizing the many hyperparameters (architecture, learning rate, regularization, batch size) and the tendency to overfit [98]. The MLP's inclusion evaluates whether the additional representational capacity of neural networks provides value for this specific dataset.

2.7.10 Probabilistic Classifiers (Naive Bayes/GNB, QDA)

**Gaussian Naive Bayes (GNB)** was included to represent the probabilistic classification paradigm, which estimates class-conditional probabilities under the assumption of feature independence [47]. While the independence assumption is violated in clinical datasets (where hormonal markers, anthropometric measures, and genetic factors are correlated), GNB often performs surprisingly well in practice, particularly as a fast baseline that provides naturally calibrated probability estimates. GNB's inclusion evaluates the information content available under the strongest possible simplifying assumptions.

**Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA)** was included to represent a more flexible probabilistic approach that models each class with its own covariance matrix, fitting quadratic decision boundaries [48]. QDA relaxes the equal-covariance assumption of LDA and provides a bridge between the strict independence of Naive Bayes and the full flexibility of kernel methods. QDA evaluates whether class-specific covariance structures contribute to discriminating between successful and unsuccessful sperm retrieval.

2.7.11 Philosophy of Comprehensive Benchmarking

The decision to evaluate 16 models across all major ML paradigms reflects a deliberate philosophy of comprehensive, assumption-free benchmarking [97,99]. Rather than selecting a small number of models based on prior assumptions about which algorithm would perform best, this approach empirically evaluates the entire spectrum of major ML paradigms and allows the data to determine the optimal algorithm. This philosophy offers several advantages: it avoids the confirmation bias inherent in testing only the experimenter's preferred algorithm; it reveals the nature of the predictive signal by showing which paradigms succeed and which fail; it enables robust comparison that accounts for the sensitivity of individual algorithms to hyperparameter settings; and it provides a more complete evidence base for clinical translation, as clinicians and reviewers can be confident that the selected model outperformed a comprehensive set of alternatives rather than a narrow, potentially cherry-picked comparison set.

The results of this benchmarking — with CatBoost achieving the highest AUC of 0.8306, followed closely by XGBoost (0.8214) and LightGBM (0.8213), and with gradient-boosted methods uniformly outperforming linear, kernel, instance-based, neural, and probabilistic approaches — empirically confirm the a priori hypothesis that gradient-boosted tree ensembles are the optimal paradigm for this dataset, while also demonstrating that the specific algorithmic innovations of CatBoost (native categorical handling, ordered boosting) provide a meaningful, if modest, advantage over the alternatives.


2.8 Research Gap and Conceptual Framework

2.8.1 Synthesis of Identified Research Gaps

The comprehensive literature review presented in the preceding sections reveals a coherent set of interconnected research gaps that collectively define the motivation and scope of this thesis. These gaps can be organized into six domains:

**Gap 1: Insufficient Sample Size and Statistical Power.** The majority of existing ML studies for micro-TESE prediction have been conducted on cohorts of fewer than 500 patients, limiting the statistical power to train complex models, evaluate numerous features, and produce stable performance estimates. The largest cohort in the prediction-focused literature (Ceyhan et al., n=3,093) used only logistic regression, while ML-focused studies have not exceeded approximately 430 patients in their training sets. The present study, with its analytical cohort of 2,413 patients, represents the largest pure ML benchmark for micro-TESE sperm retrieval prediction to date, providing sufficient sample size for training and evaluating complex models with 73 features while maintaining rigorous validation through 5×5 nested cross-validation.

**Gap 2: Limited Algorithm Diversity and Comparative Benchmarking.** Prior studies have typically compared only 1–3 algorithms, most commonly logistic regression against a single ML method. This narrow comparison fails to identify the optimal algorithm for the specific dataset characteristics and prevents systematic evaluation of different ML paradigms. The present study addresses this gap by benchmarking 16 models spanning all major ML paradigms: gradient boosting (CatBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, GBM), ensemble aggregation (Random Forest, Extra Trees), adaptive boosting (AdaBoost), single tree (Decision Tree), linear models (Logistic Regression, SGD Classifier), kernel methods (SVM), instance-based methods (KNN), neural networks (MLP), and probabilistic classifiers (GNB, QDA).

**Gap 3: Inadequate Validation and Performance Estimation.** The absence of nested cross-validation in existing studies means that reported performance estimates are likely optimistically biased due to hyperparameter overfitting. Simple train-test splits and basic k-fold CV, when combined with hyperparameter tuning, produce inflated AUC estimates that may not reflect true generalization performance [58]. The present study employs 5×5 nested cross-validation with Bayesian/randomized hyperparameter search (50 iterations per model) within the inner loop, providing nearly unbiased performance estimates that accurately reflect expected real-world generalization.

**Gap 4: Absence of Multi-Method Explainability.** With rare exceptions, existing studies have not integrated formal XAI methods, and none have employed a multi-method explainability framework combining SHAP, LIME, and PDP. The absence of cross-method concordance analysis means that reported feature importance rankings lack methodological validation — it remains unknown whether different explanation methods would identify the same features as important. The present study addresses this gap through comprehensive multi-method XAI, demonstrating that the top four predictive features (LH, Age, FSH, Testosterone) are consistently identified across SHAP, LIME, and PDP analyses.

**Gap 5: Missing Clinical Translation via CDSS.** The translation gap between model development and clinical deployment is perhaps the most consequential limitation of the existing literature. No prior study has designed a clinical decision support system that operationalizes the prediction model with defined reference ranges, validation logic, calibrated probability displays, and XAI-informed explanations. Without such a translational framework, even a well-performing and well-explained model remains a research artifact rather than a clinical tool. The present study addresses this gap through the design and specification of an 11-feature CDSS with colour-coded validation logic, SHAP-based explanation displays, and calibrated probability gauges.

**Gap 6: Untapped Pathology Report Information via NLP.** Free-text bilateral testicular pathology reports contain rich, detailed, and prognostically important histopathological information that has been either excluded or coarsely simplified in existing ML studies. No prior study has employed NLP to systematically extract quantitative histopathological indicators from bilateral pathology reports and incorporate them as engineered features in the prediction model. The present study developed a custom NLP pipeline that extracted 16 quantitative histopathological indicators (8 per testis side) from free-text pathology reports, expanding the feature set from 55 to 73 variables and capturing nuanced bilateral histopathological information that was previously inaccessible to ML models.

2.8.2 Conceptual Framework

The conceptual framework of this thesis integrates the six identified research gaps into a unified multi-phase research design that addresses each gap systematically. The framework is structured around four sequential and interconnected phases:

**Phase 1: Data Engineering and NLP-Based Feature Extraction.** This phase addresses Gap 6 by developing a custom NLP pipeline for extracting quantitative histopathological indicators from free-text bilateral pathology reports. The pipeline applies rule-based and pattern-matching algorithms calibrated to the specific terminology and reporting conventions of the Royan Institute pathology service, extracting 16 quantitative indicators (Sertoli-cell-only percentage, complete spermatogenesis percentage, maturation arrest at four stages, hypospermatogenesis, and severe hypospermatogenesis for each testis) plus two cohort metadata flags. The NLP pipeline was validated against a 100-record gold standard annotated by an expert andrology pathologist, achieving per-indicator concordance exceeding 95%.

**Phase 2: Comprehensive Model Development and Benchmarking.** This phase addresses Gaps 1, 2, and 3 by training and comparing 16 ML models using 5×5 nested cross-validation on the 2,413-patient cohort with 73 features. All preprocessing steps (RobustScaler standardization, kNN imputation with missingness-indicator encoding, SMOTE oversampling) are applied strictly within cross-validation folds to prevent data leakage. Hyperparameter optimization uses 50-iteration Bayesian/randomized search within the inner loop. The champion model selection is based on AUC, with comprehensive reporting of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, F1, MCC, Brier score, and calibration curves, following TRIPOD+AI [95] and PROBAST+AI [96] reporting standards.

**Phase 3: Multi-Method Explainability and Clinical Validation.** This phase addresses Gap 4 by applying three complementary XAI methods (SHAP, LIME, PDP) to the champion model (CatBoost, AUC 0.8306). Global SHAP analysis identifies the most important features and their directional effects. Local SHAP and LIME provide individual-level explanations for representative cases. PDP reveals the functional forms of feature-outcome relationships. Cross-method concordance analysis validates the stability of feature importance rankings across methodologically independent approaches. Calibration analysis assesses the accuracy of predicted probabilities, and Decision Curve Analysis evaluates clinical utility across the range of clinically relevant threshold probabilities.

**Phase 4: Clinical Decision Support System Design.** This phase addresses Gap 5 by translating the model, its explanations, and its calibration into a structured CDSS specification. The CDSS operationalizes 11 evidence-supported clinical features with reference ranges, colour-coded validation logic (within-range, borderline, out-of-range indicators), calibrated probability displays with colour-banded risk zones, SHAP-based top-feature explanation charts, and structured counseling text generation. The CDSS is designed for stateless web deployment, enabling integration into the preoperative counseling workflow without requiring database infrastructure or patient data persistence.

2.8.3 Addressing Each Gap

The relationship between identified gaps and the thesis design is summarized as follows. Gap 1 (sample size) is addressed through the 2,413-patient Royan Institute cohort, the largest pure ML benchmark in the micro-TESE prediction literature. Gap 2 (model diversity) is addressed through the 16-model comparison spanning all major ML paradigms. Gap 3 (validation) is addressed through 5×5 nested cross-validation with leakage-controlled preprocessing. Gap 4 (explainability) is addressed through multi-method XAI (SHAP, LIME, PDP) with cross-method concordance analysis. Gap 5 (clinical translation) is addressed through the 11-feature CDSS specification with calibration, DCA, and structured counseling output. Gap 6 (pathology NLP) is addressed through the custom NLP pipeline extracting 16 bilateral histopathological indicators from free-text reports.

The transparent disclosure of a pipeline correction (from v1 with data leakage to the authoritative v2 with leakage control) further contributes to the methodological rigor and reproducibility of this work, demonstrating the practical consequences of data leakage in clinical ML and providing a real-world case study in responsible model development [68,69]. The corrected v2 CatBoost model (AUC 0.8306) represents the definitive result and serves as the foundation for all subsequent XAI, calibration, DCA, and CDSS analyses.

This multi-phase framework, grounded in the systematic identification and targeted resolution of six research gaps, positions the thesis as a comprehensive contribution to the field of AI-assisted clinical decision-making in reproductive medicine.
