## Chapter 3: Materials and Methods

### 3.1 Study Design

This investigation employed a retrospective, single-center, cross-sectional analytical study design to develop and validate a comprehensive machine learning framework for predicting successful sperm retrieval in patients with non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) undergoing microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE). The study was conducted at the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine in Tehran, Iran, a World Health Organization (WHO) Collaborating Center for reproductive medicine and one of the largest andrology referral centers in the Middle East [1,2]. The institute performs more than 300 micro-TESE procedures annually and maintains a comprehensive electronic medical record system that has been operational since 2007.

The retrospective design was selected for several methodological reasons. First, the rarity of NOA as a clinical condition and the invasive nature of micro-TESE preclude prospective recruitment of sufficiently large cohorts within a reasonable timeframe for initial model development [3]. Second, the availability of 15 years of institutional data (2007–2022) provided a uniquely large and heterogeneous patient population that captured natural temporal variation in clinical practice, surgical technique, and patient demographics. Third, retrospective cohort assembly from institutional electronic medical records permitted the inclusion of all relevant clinical, hormonal, genetic, and histopathological variables that are routinely collected in the standard preoperative evaluation protocol for NOA patients at this center.

The cross-sectional analytical framework treated each patient's index micro-TESE procedure as the unit of analysis, with the binary outcome of successful sperm retrieval serving as the dependent variable. This approach is consistent with the clinical decision point at which the predictive model is intended to be deployed: the preoperative counseling visit where the clinician and patient jointly decide whether to proceed with micro-TESE based on the estimated probability of success [4,5].

The study period spanning 2007 to 2022 was deliberately chosen to balance two competing objectives: maximizing statistical power through cohort size and ensuring clinical relevance by including patients managed under contemporary surgical protocols. Although operative techniques and hormonal assay methodologies evolved incrementally over this 15-year window, the fundamental surgical approach (microdissection under operating microscope guidance) and the core preoperative evaluation protocol (hormonal panel, physical examination, genetic testing, and diagnostic testicular biopsy) remained consistent throughout the study period at this center [6].

### 3.2 Ethical Considerations

#### 3.2.1 Institutional Review Board Approval

The study protocol was reviewed and approved by the Institutional Review Board (IRB) of the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine. Given the retrospective design and the use of fully de-identified data, the IRB granted a waiver of individual informed consent in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki and local regulations governing retrospective medical record research [7]. No patient was contacted, recontacted, or subjected to any additional clinical procedure as part of this study.

#### 3.2.2 Data De-identification and Security

All data were extracted from the institutional electronic medical record system and converted into a fully de-identified analytical dataset prior to any modeling or analytical step. Direct patient identifiers, including names, national identification numbers, and contact information, were removed at the point of extraction. Two opaque institutional identifiers (ID and Roy-ID) were retained exclusively as linkage keys for quality assurance purposes—specifically, to enable verification of extracted values against the source medical record during the data cleaning phase—and were never used as model inputs or included in any analytical procedure. Following completion of quality assurance, these identifiers were irreversibly stripped from the analytical dataset.

All computational work, including natural language processing (NLP) feature extraction, model training, hyperparameter optimization, calibration analysis, and explainability computations, was performed on a secure institutional workstation. Data were stored on encrypted local storage and were not transmitted to any external server or cloud computing platform. Access to the raw and de-identified datasets was restricted to authorized members of the research team.

#### 3.2.3 Adherence to Reporting Standards

The study was prospectively designed to comply with the Transparent Reporting of a multivariable prediction model for Individual Prognosis or Diagnosis plus Artificial Intelligence extension (TRIPOD+AI) 2024 statement [8], which provides updated guidance for reporting clinical prediction models that incorporate machine learning methods. Full item-level adherence to the TRIPOD+AI checklist is documented in the supplementary materials. Additionally, the risk of bias was systematically assessed using the Prediction model Risk Of Bias Assessment Tool (PROBAST) [9] and its recently published artificial intelligence extension (PROBAST+AI) [10], which addresses domains specific to machine learning workflows, including data preprocessing integrity, hyperparameter tuning leakage, and outcome contamination. Both the initial (v1) and corrected (v2) analysis pipelines were assessed separately under PROBAST+AI to ensure that the remediation of the identified data leakage issue was fully auditable. Detailed domain-level risk-of-bias judgments for the Participants, Predictors, Outcome, and Analysis domains are presented in Chapter 5.

### 3.3 Study Population and Sampling

#### 3.3.1 Source Population

The source population comprised all men who presented to the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine with a confirmed diagnosis of non-obstructive azoospermia and who subsequently underwent microdissection testicular sperm extraction between January 2007 and December 2022. NOA was diagnosed according to standard clinical criteria: the absence of spermatozoa in at least two consecutive semen analyses performed after centrifugation, combined with hormonal and physical examination findings consistent with a primary testicular etiology rather than obstructive pathology [11,12]. All patients underwent a standardized preoperative evaluation that included a comprehensive hormonal panel (follicle-stimulating hormone [FSH], luteinizing hormone [LH], testosterone, prolactin, estradiol, and, when available, inhibin B and anti-Müllerian hormone [AMH]), physical examination (bilateral testicular volume estimation, varicocele grading), genetic testing (karyotype analysis and Y-chromosome microdeletion screening for AZF a, b, and c regions), and diagnostic testicular biopsy with histopathological reporting.

#### 3.3.2 Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria

Inclusion criteria were defined as follows: (i) a documented clinical diagnosis of NOA established by repeated semen analyses, hormonal profiling, and physical examination; (ii) completion of at least one micro-TESE procedure with operative documentation at the Royan Institute; and (iii) availability of the binary primary outcome variable (Outcome1), defined as successful or unsuccessful sperm retrieval at the index micro-TESE.

Exclusion criteria were: (i) patients with obstructive azoospermia, as confirmed by clinical evaluation and/or surgical findings; (ii) patients for whom the primary outcome variable was missing or indeterminate; and (iii) records with data integrity issues that precluded reliable outcome ascertainment.

#### 3.3.3 Cohort Assembly and Final Analytical Sample

From the institutional database, a total of 2,450 patient records were initially screened for eligibility. Of these, 37 patients (1.5%) were excluded because the primary outcome variable (Outcome1) was missing or could not be reliably determined from the available medical records. This yielded a final analytical cohort of 2,413 patients. No other patients were excluded for any reason, and no sampling strategy beyond census enumeration was applied; thus, the analytical cohort represents a consecutive, unselected series of NOA patients undergoing micro-TESE at this center over the study period.

#### 3.3.4 Outcome Definition and Class Distribution

The primary outcome variable, Outcome1, was defined as a binary indicator of successful sperm retrieval at the index micro-TESE procedure. Success was defined as the identification of at least one spermatozoon—motile or immotile—by the embryologist on intra-operative wet preparation of the extracted testicular tissue [13]. This definition is consistent with the internationally accepted standard for sperm retrieval outcome reporting in micro-TESE studies and represents the clinically relevant endpoint for patient counseling purposes [14].

In the final analytical cohort, 886 patients (36.7%) had successful sperm retrieval and 1,527 patients (63.3%) had unsuccessful retrieval, yielding an approximate failure-to-success class ratio of 1.72:1. This moderate class imbalance is consistent with the expected sperm retrieval rates reported in the literature for unselected NOA populations undergoing micro-TESE, which typically range from 30% to 50% depending on case mix and institutional experience [15,16]. The cohort further comprised 2,234 primary micro-TESE attempts (91.2%) and 216 salvage attempts (8.8%), where salvage was defined as a micro-TESE performed on a patient with one or more prior failed sperm retrieval attempts at another institution.

#### 3.3.5 Sampling Rationale

The study employed census sampling—that is, all eligible patients meeting the inclusion criteria were included in the analytical cohort without any probabilistic or convenience-based subsampling. This approach maximizes statistical power, avoids selection bias inherent in convenience or purposive sampling strategies, and ensures that the cohort captures the full spectrum of clinical presentations, etiological subtypes, and temporal variation present in the source population over the 15-year study period. The resulting cohort of 2,413 patients represents, to our knowledge, the largest single-center machine learning study specifically focused on micro-TESE sperm retrieval prediction in NOA [2,17].

### 3.4 Data Collection and Variables

#### 3.4.1 Data Source and Extraction

All clinical data were extracted from the Royan Institute's electronic medical record system. The source data were originally maintained in a structured clinical workbook that captured the standard preoperative, intraoperative, and postoperative variables collected as part of routine clinical care. Data extraction was performed by a trained research assistant and verified by a senior clinical investigator for accuracy and completeness.

#### 3.4.2 Original Variable Set

The original dataset comprised 55 clinical variables spanning multiple domains relevant to the preoperative evaluation and prognostication of NOA patients. These variables were organized into the following categories:

**Demographic and anthropometric variables.** Patient age at the time of micro-TESE, height, body weight, body mass index (BMI), race/ethnicity, and occupational exposure to toxic substances. These variables capture the demographic and constitutional factors that may influence spermatogenesis and surgical outcomes.

**Hormonal variables.** A comprehensive reproductive hormonal panel including FSH, LH, total testosterone, prolactin, estradiol (E2), thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), inhibin B, and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH). These hormones reflect the integrity of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis and are widely recognized as important predictors of testicular function and sperm retrieval outcomes in NOA [18,19].

**Physical examination variables.** Bilateral testicular volume measurements, obtained by both clinical palpation (Prader orchidometer estimation) and, where available, ultrasonographic volumetric calculation using the Lambert formula (length × width × height × 0.71) and the Sakamoto formula. Varicocele status was recorded bilaterally with clinical grading.

**Genetic variables.** Karyotype analysis results, Y-chromosome microdeletion status for the AZF a, AZF b, and AZF c regions, and the presence of Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY). These genetic variables are critical prognostic factors, as certain genetic etiologies (e.g., complete AZFa or AZFb microdeletions) are associated with virtually zero probability of sperm retrieval, while others (e.g., AZFc microdeletions) carry a more favorable prognosis [20,21].

**Surgical and medical history variables.** Prior surgical history including testicular biopsy (T.BX), inguinal hernia repair, torsion of the spermatic cord, and other urogenital surgeries or trauma. Medical comorbidities including diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Previous micro-TESE attempt status (is_Primary flag and Salvage_Count).

**Semen analysis parameters.** Seminal plasma pH, which serves as an indicator of accessory gland function and may have prognostic value in NOA.

**Histopathological variables.** Bilateral free-text testicular biopsy pathology reports (Pathology-RT for the right testis and Pathology-LT for the left testis), which describe the histological findings from diagnostic testicular biopsies performed prior to or concurrent with micro-TESE. These reports contain information about the predominant histological pattern, including Sertoli-cell-only (SCO) pattern, maturation arrest at various stages, hypospermatogenesis, and complete spermatogenesis (CSTH).

**Partner characteristics.** Partner age at the time of the procedure. This variable was initially included in the analytical dataset but was subsequently excluded from the final clinical decision support system based on explainability analysis that revealed its statistical importance was attributable to confounding with couple duration and treatment urgency rather than any biological relevance to male testicular sperm retrieval (see Section 3.13).

**Lifestyle and environmental variables.** Habits (smoking, alcohol use), family history of infertility (infertile family members), and occupational exposure to potentially gonadotoxic substances.

#### 3.4.3 Missingness Profile

A systematic assessment of data completeness revealed substantial heterogeneity in missingness across variables. Demographic variables such as age and race had complete data (0% missing), while hormonal variables exhibited moderate missingness: FSH (22.2%), LH (25.1%), and testosterone (22.6%). Histopathological reports showed asymmetric availability, with Pathology-RT available for 77.8% of patients but Pathology-LT available for only 51.0%, reflecting the clinical practice of performing unilateral rather than bilateral biopsies in some cases. Anthropometric measures (height, weight, BMI) had substantial missingness (64–67%), likely reflecting inconsistent recording practices across the 15-year study period. Genetic variables (karyotype and Y-microdeletion) were missing in approximately 62% of cases, consistent with the evolution of genetic testing availability and clinical guidelines over the study period. Sonographic testicular volume measurements had the highest missingness among retained variables (82–83%), as ultrasound-based volumetric assessment became routine practice only in later years.

Nine variables were identified with 100% missingness: Cancer Type, Cancer Treatment, Surgery Therapeutic, SHBG, TSH, Inhibin B, AMH, Infertility Length, and Torsion of the Spermatic Cord. These were excluded from the analytical dataset during the structural audit phase. The remaining variables with substantial missingness were retained when clinically meaningful and were handled through the imputation strategy described in Section 3.6.

### 3.5 Feature Engineering

#### 3.5.1 Natural Language Processing Pipeline for Pathology Reports

A critical innovation of this study was the development and validation of a custom NLP pipeline to extract structured quantitative features from free-text bilateral testicular biopsy pathology reports. This section describes the pipeline architecture, the specific features extracted, the validation methodology, and the resulting expansion of the feature space.

**Purpose and rationale.** Testicular biopsy histopathology represents one of the most informative predictors of micro-TESE outcomes, as it directly characterizes the state of spermatogenesis at the tissue level [22,23]. However, in the source dataset, bilateral pathology findings were recorded as free-text narrative reports by attending pathologists, using semi-structured natural language with variable terminology, abbreviations, and percentage annotations. To incorporate this rich histopathological information into the machine learning framework, it was necessary to transform these unstructured text fields into structured, quantitative features amenable to computational analysis.

**Pipeline architecture.** The NLP pipeline employed a deterministic, regex-based approach with medical terminology mapping. This design was deliberately chosen over more complex approaches (e.g., transformer-based models, large language models) for three primary reasons: (i) the pathology reporting vocabulary at the study center was relatively controlled and domain-specific, making regex patterns highly effective; (ii) a deterministic pipeline ensures complete reproducibility and auditability, which are essential for clinical prediction models intended for deployment; and (iii) the explainability and transparency of rule-based extraction aligns with the study's emphasis on interpretable AI [24,25]. This NLP design pattern—deterministic regex with a controlled vocabulary and post-extraction hierarchy rules—is increasingly used in pathology and radiology text extraction across multiple clinical domains [26,27,28,29].

**Extracted features.** The pipeline processed the two bilateral pathology text fields (Pathology-RT for the right testis and Pathology-LT for the left testis) and extracted 16 quantitative histopathological indicators—eight per testis side—covering the principal histological diagnoses encountered in NOA pathology reporting:

1. **Sertoli-cell-only pattern (SCO):** The percentage of seminiferous tubules showing a Sertoli-cell-only pattern, indicating complete absence of germ cells. SCO is the most severe histological finding and is associated with the poorest prognosis for sperm retrieval [30].

2. **Complete spermatogenesis (CSTH):** The percentage of tubules demonstrating complete spermatogenic activity through all developmental stages, indicating the presence of focal or diffuse normal spermatogenesis.

3. **Maturation arrest — spermatogonial stage:** The percentage of tubules showing arrest at the earliest germ cell stage (spermatogonia), indicating the most severe form of maturation arrest.

4. **Maturation arrest — spermatocytic stage:** The percentage of tubules with arrest at the primary spermatocyte stage, representing arrest after the first mitotic divisions but before meiosis completion.

5. **Maturation arrest — immature round spermatid stage:** The percentage of tubules showing arrest at the round spermatid stage, indicating completion of meiosis but failure of spermiogenesis.

6. **Maturation arrest — elongated spermatid stage:** The percentage of tubules with arrest at the elongated spermatid stage, the latest form of maturation arrest before full sperm maturation.

7. **Hypospermatogenesis:** The percentage of tubules showing reduced but present spermatogenic activity across all stages, indicating quantitative rather than qualitative impairment.

8. **Severe hypospermatogenesis:** The percentage of tubules with markedly reduced spermatogenesis, representing a more extreme variant of hypospermatogenesis.

**Extraction logic.** Where an explicit percentage was present in the pathology report (e.g., "SCO ~70%"), the numeric value was extracted directly using pattern-matched regular expressions. Where a histological pattern was identified in the text without an accompanying explicit percentage, a default value of 100% was assigned, reflecting the pathologist's implicit reporting of that pattern as the dominant or sole finding in the specimen.

**Hierarchy rule.** A biologically motivated hierarchy constraint was enforced to maintain internal consistency among the maturation arrest indicators. When a pathology report indicated arrest at an earlier developmental phase, all later-phase maturation arrest indicators on the same testis side were automatically set to zero. The hierarchy followed the natural developmental sequence: Spermatogonial → Spermatocytic → Immature Round Spermatid → Elongated Spermatid. This rule reflects the biological reality that arrest at an early stage precludes the presence of later developmental stages in the same tubular population [31].

**Additional engineered features.** Beyond the 16 histopathological indicators, two cohort metadata features were engineered: (i) is_Primary, a binary flag indicating whether the micro-TESE was the patient's first attempt (coded as 1) or a salvage procedure following prior failed retrieval (coded as 0); and (ii) Salvage_Count, an integer representing the number of prior failed sperm retrieval attempts. Together, these 18 engineered features expanded the original 55-column dataset to a 73-feature analytic matrix.

**Downstream aggregated descriptors.** Within the modeling pipeline, additional aggregated pathology descriptors were computed from the per-side NLP-extracted features, including per-side mean values, binary aggregates for SCO and CSTH presence, and pathology availability indicators. These brought the total histopathology feature block to 22 features within the final modeling matrix.

#### 3.5.2 NLP Pipeline Validation

Pipeline validation was conducted in two stages to ensure the accuracy and reliability of the automated feature extraction.

**Stage 1: Gold-standard concordance.** A stratified random sample of 100 patient records was independently annotated by an experienced andrology pathologist who manually extracted each of the 16 quantitative histopathological indicators from the original free-text reports. The NLP pipeline was iteratively refined against this gold standard until per-indicator concordance exceeded the a priori threshold of 95%. In the final validation, eight indicators achieved 100% concordance with the gold standard, six indicators achieved 96–99% concordance, and two indicators—elongated spermatid arrest and severe hypospermatogenesis, both rare histological endpoints—achieved 95% concordance after the addition of pattern variants to accommodate abbreviated reporting styles.

**Stage 2: Corpus-level consistency.** The finalized pipeline was applied to the entire 2,413-record corpus. The distribution of extracted features was examined for biological plausibility and consistency with the expected histopathological spectrum of NOA in a national referral cohort. The proportion of patients with non-zero pathology features was 100% for the bilateral pathology availability indicators, consistent with the universal practice of pathology reporting at this center. Individual diagnostic indicators ranged from 7% (severe hypospermatogenesis, a rare finding) to 41% (SCO patterns, the most common histological finding in NOA), which aligns with published distributions in the literature [32,33].

Importantly, the NLP extraction did not utilize any patient identifier or outcome label; all processing was performed once on the de-identified text corpus prior to any train/test partitioning, and the extracted numerical features were treated identically to any other input variable in the downstream leakage-controlled cross-validation pipeline.

#### 3.5.3 Other Feature Engineering Steps

Beyond the NLP pipeline, additional feature engineering steps were applied: (i) ordinal encoding of structured pathology categories using an ordinal_detailed_plus_raw scheme that preserved both the ordered severity information and the raw extracted percentage values; (ii) derivation of binary missingness-indicator features for variables with non-trivial missingness, which allowed the models to learn patterns from the data absence itself (e.g., whether a genetic test was ordered may correlate with the clinical suspicion of a genetic etiology); and (iii) computation of derived ratio features where clinically relevant, such as the testosterone-to-estradiol (T/E2) ratio, which has been identified as a predictor of spermatogenesis status [34,35].

### 3.6 Data Preprocessing

#### 3.6.1 Missing Data Assessment and Handling Strategy

Given the substantial and heterogeneous missingness profile described in Section 3.4.3, a principled approach to missing data handling was essential. The study employed k-nearest neighbors (kNN) imputation for continuous variables, which estimates missing values based on the observed values of the k most similar patients in the feature space. kNN imputation was selected over alternative approaches (e.g., mean/median imputation, multiple imputation by chained equations) because it preserves local data structure and feature correlations without imposing distributional assumptions [36]. Critically, kNN imputation was fitted exclusively on the training data within each cross-validation fold and then applied to the corresponding validation fold, ensuring that no information from held-out data influenced the imputation process.

In addition to numeric imputation, explicit binary missingness-indicator features were constructed for all variables with non-trivial missingness. This approach, sometimes referred to as the "missing indicator method," allows the machine learning models to distinguish between an imputed value and a genuinely observed value, effectively permitting the models to learn whether the absence of a measurement is itself informative [37]. The informativeness of these missingness indicators was subsequently confirmed by SHAP analysis, validating the decision to retain them.

#### 3.6.2 Outlier Detection and Handling

Outlier detection was initially performed using the interquartile range (IQR) method, where values beyond 1.5 × IQR below the first quartile or above the third quartile were flagged as potential outliers. However, the handling of outliers revealed a critical methodological lesson that warrants detailed discussion due to its impact on the study's results and its relevance to the broader machine learning in healthcare community.

**The v1 outlier clipping bug (data leakage).** In the initial version of the analysis pipeline (v1), outlier clipping was applied globally to the entire dataset before the cross-validation split. Specifically, the IQR boundaries were computed on the full 2,413-patient cohort and then used to clip extreme values across all records. This approach constituted a form of data leakage, as information from the validation and test folds—specifically, their contribution to the computation of the IQR boundaries—was inadvertently used to transform the training data and vice versa [38,39]. More critically, the IQR-based clipping had an unintended consequence on the NLP-derived pathology features: because many of these features had skewed distributions with large proportions of zero values (reflecting the absence of specific histological patterns in the majority of patients), the IQR-based clipping effectively zeroed out a substantial proportion of non-zero pathology values, collapsing feature variance and suppressing the pathology signal. This leakage paradoxically reduced model performance by destroying informative variance rather than inflating it—a counterintuitive outcome that was only identified during post-analysis auditing.

**The v2 correction.** In the corrected v2 pipeline, the IQR-based outlier clipping step was entirely removed. Instead, the pipeline relied on the inherent robustness of the RobustScaler (which uses median and IQR for centering and scaling rather than mean and standard deviation) and the natural outlier tolerance of gradient boosting algorithms to handle extreme values without explicit clipping [40]. All preprocessing steps—including imputation, scaling, encoding, and oversampling—were moved strictly inside the cross-validation folds, ensuring complete isolation between training and validation data at every step. This correction is transparently reported in accordance with recent guidance on pipeline leakage in clinical prediction modeling [38,41] and represents a key methodological contribution of this thesis.

The impact of this correction on model performance is discussed in detail in Chapter 4. Briefly, the v1 pipeline with the leakage bug produced a champion model (LightGBM) with an AUC of 0.7327, while the corrected v2 pipeline yielded a champion model (CatBoost) with an AUC of 0.8306—a substantial improvement attributable to the preservation of pathology feature variance and the elimination of information leakage.

#### 3.6.3 Feature Scaling

Feature scaling was performed using the RobustScaler from the scikit-learn library, which standardizes features by removing the median and scaling to the interquartile range. The RobustScaler was selected over alternatives (StandardScaler, MinMaxScaler) based on two considerations: (i) robustness to outliers, which is particularly important given the skewed distributions of hormonal variables (e.g., FSH values in hypergonadotropic patients can exceed normal ranges by an order of magnitude); and (ii) the selection of the scaler was itself a hyperparameter subject to Bayesian optimization within the inner cross-validation loop, and RobustScaler was consistently selected as the optimal choice across model families.

Critically, the scaler was always fitted exclusively on the training data within each fold and then applied to transform both the training and validation data, preventing any information leakage through the scaling step.

#### 3.6.4 Categorical Encoding Strategies

Categorical variables were encoded using a combination of strategies tailored to the variable characteristics and model requirements. Binary categorical variables (e.g., Klinefelter status, prior testicular biopsy) were encoded as 0/1 indicators. Multi-level categorical variables (e.g., karyotype results, varicocele grade) were encoded using one-hot encoding for linear models and label encoding for tree-based models, which can natively handle ordinal splits on integer-encoded categories. The NLP-derived pathology features employed an ordinal_detailed_plus_raw encoding scheme that preserved both the ordered severity hierarchy of histological diagnoses and the raw extracted percentage values, enabling the models to leverage both the categorical pattern information and the quantitative extent of each histological finding. The maturation arrest hierarchy constraint (Section 3.5.1) was enforced during encoding to maintain biological consistency.

#### 3.6.5 Class Imbalance Handling with SMOTE

The moderate class imbalance in the analytical cohort (63.3% failure vs. 36.7% success, ratio ≈ 1.72:1) was addressed using the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) [42]. SMOTE generates synthetic examples of the minority class (successful retrieval) by interpolating between existing minority-class instances in feature space, thereby balancing the class distribution in the training data without simply duplicating existing records.

A critical methodological safeguard was the application of SMOTE exclusively within the training folds of the cross-validation procedure. Applying SMOTE before the cross-validation split would constitute data leakage, as synthetic samples generated from minority-class instances in the validation fold could contaminate the training data and produce optimistically biased performance estimates [43,44]. In the v2 pipeline, SMOTE was applied as the final preprocessing step within each training fold, after imputation and scaling, ensuring that synthetic samples were generated only from bona fide training instances.

### 3.7 Machine Learning Pipeline Architecture

#### 3.7.1 Overall Pipeline Design Philosophy

The machine learning pipeline was designed around three foundational principles: (i) methodological rigor through leakage-controlled preprocessing; (ii) comprehensive model benchmarking across all major machine learning paradigms; and (iii) unbiased performance estimation through nested cross-validation. These principles reflect current best practices in clinical prediction model development and address the most common sources of optimistic bias identified in the medical machine learning literature [38,45,46].

The pipeline was implemented in Python using the scikit-learn ecosystem (version 1.4), supplemented by specialized gradient boosting libraries (CatBoost 1.2, LightGBM 4.3, XGBoost 2.0) and the imbalanced-learn library (version 0.12) for SMOTE oversampling. All random seeds were fixed at 42 to ensure full reproducibility of results.

#### 3.7.2 Five-by-Five Nested Cross-Validation

The study employed a 5×5 nested cross-validation (NCV) design, which represents the gold standard for simultaneous hyperparameter tuning and unbiased performance estimation in clinical prediction modeling [45,47].

**Outer loop: Performance estimation.** The outer loop consisted of 5-fold stratified cross-validation applied to the training set (n = 1,930). Stratification ensured that each fold preserved the original class distribution (approximately 36.7% success, 63.3% failure), preventing fold-specific class imbalance artifacts. In each iteration of the outer loop, four folds served as the training partition and the remaining fold served as the validation partition for computing performance metrics. The final reported performance for each model was the mean and standard deviation of the chosen metric across the five outer folds.

**Inner loop: Hyperparameter tuning.** Within each outer training partition, a second 5-fold stratified cross-validation was conducted for hyperparameter optimization. For each candidate model, 50 iterations of randomized search (RandomizedSearchCV) were performed over the model-specific hyperparameter search space. The inner loop optimization objective was the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC), which was chosen as the primary metric for its threshold-independence and its suitability for evaluating discriminative performance in the context of class-imbalanced binary classification [48].

**Rationale for nested cross-validation.** The nested design prevents the optimistic bias that arises when the same data are used for both hyperparameter selection and performance evaluation—a common methodological flaw in clinical prediction studies [45,47]. In non-nested cross-validation, the performance metric on the validation fold is biased upward because the hyperparameters were selected to maximize performance on data that includes or overlaps with that fold. The nested design creates a strict separation between the data used for model selection (inner loop) and the data used for performance estimation (outer loop), yielding performance estimates that more accurately reflect generalization to unseen data.

**Pipeline steps within each fold.** Within each outer training fold, the following preprocessing steps were applied in sequence, fitted exclusively on the training partition: (i) kNN imputation of missing continuous values; (ii) construction of binary missingness indicators; (iii) RobustScaler standardization; (iv) categorical encoding with maturation arrest hierarchy enforcement; (v) SMOTE oversampling of the minority class; and (vi) model fitting with the hyperparameters selected by the inner loop. The fitted preprocessing transformers were then applied to transform the outer validation fold, and model predictions on the transformed validation data were used to compute the outer-fold performance metrics.

**Figure 3-1: Schematic Diagram of the 5×5 Nested Cross-Validation Pipeline**

*Figure 3-1 illustrates the nested cross-validation architecture. The outer diagram shows the 5-fold outer loop, where the full training set (n = 1,930) is partitioned into five stratified folds. In each outer iteration, four folds form the outer training set and one fold serves as the outer validation set for unbiased performance estimation. Within each outer training set, the inner diagram depicts the 5-fold inner loop used for hyperparameter optimization via 50 iterations of randomized search. The preprocessing pipeline (kNN imputation → missingness indicators → RobustScaler → categorical encoding → SMOTE) is fitted exclusively on the inner training partition and applied to the inner validation partition, ensuring complete leakage control. The optimal hyperparameters from the inner loop are used to retrain the model on the full outer training set, and performance is evaluated on the outer validation fold. This process repeats five times, yielding five outer-fold performance estimates that are averaged to produce the final unbiased performance metric for each model.*

### 3.8 Model Selection and Configuration

#### 3.8.1 Model Portfolio: 16 Algorithms Across All Major ML Paradigms

To identify the optimal predictive approach for this specific dataset, a comprehensive benchmark was conducted encompassing 16 candidate algorithms spanning all major machine learning paradigms. The selection of this diverse model portfolio was motivated by the principle that no single algorithm family can be assumed a priori to be optimal for a given dataset, and that comprehensive benchmarking across paradigms is necessary to identify the best-performing approach [49,50]. The dataset characteristics—tabular format, binary classification, mixed feature types (continuous hormonal, categorical pathological, NLP-derived ordinal), moderate class imbalance (1.72:1), and moderate sample size (2,413 samples × 73 features) with no temporal or sequential structure—were expected to favor gradient boosting methods based on prior evidence [51], but this hypothesis required empirical confirmation through rigorous benchmarking.

The 16 models and their rationale for inclusion were as follows:

**Gradient boosting family (4 models).** CatBoost [52], XGBoost [53], LightGBM [54], and Gradient Boosting (scikit-learn implementation) were included as the primary candidates given their demonstrated superiority on tabular data with mixed feature types. CatBoost offers native handling of categorical features and ordered boosting for reduced overfitting; XGBoost provides regularized boosting with sparsity-aware learning; LightGBM uses histogram-based gradient boosting for computational efficiency; and scikit-learn Gradient Boosting serves as a reference implementation.

**Ensemble aggregation methods (3 models).** Random Forest [55], Stacking Ensemble, and Voting Ensemble were included for variance reduction through model aggregation. Random Forest provides bagging-based ensemble learning with decorrelated trees; Stacking Ensemble combines diverse base learners through a meta-learner; and Voting Ensemble aggregates predictions through averaging or majority voting.

**Regularized linear models (4 models).** Logistic Regression, Ridge Classifier, Lasso (L1), and Elastic Net were included as interpretable baselines that provide inherent feature selection (L1 penalty) and regularization for handling multicollinearity among correlated hormonal variables [56].

**Tree-based model (1 model).** Decision Tree was included as a maximally interpretable baseline that captures non-linear feature interactions through recursive partitioning.

**Neural network (1 model).** Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) was included to explore non-linear feature combinations beyond tree-based splits, with architectures optimized through hyperparameter search.

**Distance-based model (1 model).** K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) was included as a non-parametric reference that captures local data structure and can identify heterogeneous subgroups within the patient population.

**Kernel method (1 model).** Support Vector Machine with Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel was included to explore non-linear decision boundaries in the transformed feature space through the kernel trick.

**Probabilistic model (1 model).** Naive Bayes was included as a probabilistic baseline that provides naturally calibrated probability estimates (under its conditional independence assumptions) and serves as a lower-bound reference.

#### 3.8.2 Hyperparameter Search Spaces and Optimization

Hyperparameter optimization was performed using Bayesian optimization via randomized search (RandomizedSearchCV) with 50 iterations per model within the inner cross-validation loop. The optimization objective was the AUC-ROC, and the search strategy sampled from the model-specific hyperparameter distributions detailed in Table 3-3. The use of 50 iterations per model represents a balance between computational feasibility (16 models × 50 iterations × 5 inner folds × 5 outer folds = 20,000 model fits) and adequate exploration of the hyperparameter space [57].

#### 3.8.3 Champion Model Configuration

The hyperparameter optimization process identified CatBoost as the champion model with the following optimal hyperparameters: tree depth = 6, number of boosting iterations = 657, L2 leaf regularization = 3.51, and learning rate = 0.00617. The preprocessing pipeline used RobustScaler for feature standardization and ordinal_detailed_plus_raw encoding for pathology features. These hyperparameters were selected by the inner cross-validation loop and validated by the outer loop, producing an AUC of 0.8306 (95% CI: 0.823–0.845). Fold-level selected hyperparameters were serialized as JSON artifacts for full reproducibility.

### 3.9 Model Evaluation Metrics

#### 3.9.1 Primary Metric: Area Under the ROC Curve

The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC) was selected as the primary evaluation metric for model comparison and optimization. AUC-ROC measures the probability that the model ranks a randomly chosen positive instance (successful retrieval) higher than a randomly chosen negative instance (unsuccessful retrieval), providing a threshold-independent assessment of discriminative ability [48]. AUC-ROC is widely recommended as the primary metric for clinical prediction model evaluation in the TRIPOD guidelines [8] and is robust to class imbalance, making it appropriate for this study's outcome distribution.

#### 3.9.2 Secondary Metrics

At the model-specific optimal decision threshold, the following secondary metrics were computed:

- **Accuracy:** The proportion of correctly classified instances across both classes.
- **Sensitivity (Recall):** The proportion of successful retrieval cases correctly identified by the model, reflecting the model's ability to detect patients who would benefit from micro-TESE.
- **Specificity:** The proportion of unsuccessful retrieval cases correctly identified, reflecting the model's ability to identify patients unlikely to benefit, thereby potentially avoiding unnecessary surgical procedures.
- **Positive Predictive Value (PPV/Precision):** The probability that a patient predicted to have successful retrieval actually achieves it.
- **Negative Predictive Value (NPV):** The probability that a patient predicted to have unsuccessful retrieval is correctly identified, informing counseling about the likelihood of an unsuccessful outcome.
- **F1 Score:** The harmonic mean of precision and recall, providing a balanced measure of model performance on the minority class.
- **Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC):** A balanced measure that accounts for all four cells of the confusion matrix, providing a more informative single metric for imbalanced datasets than accuracy alone [58].

#### 3.9.3 Confidence Intervals

Ninety-five percent confidence intervals for AUC-ROC were computed using 1,000 bootstrap resamples of the held-out test set. For secondary metrics derived at the cross-validation level, confidence intervals were derived from the distribution of fold-level estimates. Pairwise comparisons between models were reported with Benjamini-Hochberg multiple-comparison correction across the 16-model grid [59].

#### 3.9.4 Threshold Selection Strategy

The default decision threshold was determined using Youden's J statistic (J = sensitivity + specificity − 1), which identifies the threshold that maximizes the sum of sensitivity and specificity [60]. Across bootstrap resamples, the Youden-optimal threshold for the champion CatBoost model ranged from 0.444 to 0.507, consistent with the cohort base rate of 36.7%.

In addition to the Youden-optimal threshold, two clinically motivated operating points were evaluated: (i) a sensitivity-prioritized threshold (sensitivity ≥ 0.85) for clinical settings where minimizing missed retrievals is the dominant utility; and (ii) a specificity-prioritized threshold (specificity ≥ 0.90) for triage applications where surgical capacity is constrained. The full operating-point grid—including threshold, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, F1, MCC, calibration slope, calibration intercept, and Brier score at each operating point—is provided in the supplementary materials.

### 3.10 Explainable AI (XAI) Framework

#### 3.10.1 Rationale for Multi-Method Explainability

The deployment of machine learning models in clinical decision-making necessitates not only accurate predictions but also transparent and interpretable explanations of the factors driving individual predictions [61,62]. This study employed a multi-method explainability framework combining three complementary approaches—SHAP, LIME, and Partial Dependence Plots—to provide robust, cross-validated insights into model behavior at both global and local levels. The use of multiple explainability methods mitigates the risk of method-specific artifacts and provides stronger evidence for the stability and reliability of identified feature importance patterns [63].

#### 3.10.2 SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations)

SHAP values, based on the cooperative game theory concept of Shapley values, were computed using the TreeExplainer algorithm [64], which provides exact Shapley values for tree-based ensemble models in polynomial time. TreeExplainer was applied to the frozen CatBoost champion model to generate both global and local explanations.

**Global feature importance.** Global feature importance was quantified by the mean absolute SHAP value (mean |SHAP|) across all instances in the test set. This metric represents the average magnitude of each feature's contribution to the model's predictions, irrespective of the direction of the contribution. Features were ranked by their mean |SHAP| values to identify the most influential predictors of sperm retrieval outcome.

**SHAP summary plots.** Beeswarm summary plots were generated to visualize the distribution of SHAP values for each feature across all test instances, simultaneously displaying feature importance (vertical ordering), effect direction (positive or negative SHAP value), and the relationship between feature value magnitude and SHAP contribution (color encoding).

**SHAP dependence plots.** Feature-specific dependence plots were generated for the top-ranking predictors to visualize the relationship between the feature value and its SHAP contribution, revealing non-linear patterns and potential interaction effects.

**Individual patient explanations.** Force plots and waterfall plots were generated for representative individual patients to illustrate how specific feature values combine to produce the model's predicted probability for each case. Five representative cases spanning the full range of predicted probabilities and clinical presentations were selected for detailed local explanation.

#### 3.10.3 LIME (Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations)

LIME [65] was applied as a complementary local explanation method. For each test instance, LIME generated a local explanation by: (i) perturbing the instance's feature values using a Gaussian neighborhood sampling strategy; (ii) obtaining model predictions for the perturbed instances; (iii) fitting a sparse linear surrogate model (weighted by proximity to the original instance) to the perturbed predictions; and (iv) extracting the per-feature weights of the surrogate model as local importance scores.

LIME explanations were generated for the same representative cases analyzed with SHAP to enable direct comparison of local feature attributions between the two methods. The concordance between SHAP and LIME explanations—specifically, whether both methods agree on the sign and relative magnitude of the top contributing features for individual predictions—was assessed to validate the robustness of the explanations.

#### 3.10.4 Partial Dependence Plots (PDP)

One-way partial dependence plots were generated for the top-ranking predictors using the standard marginal expectation framework [66,67]. The PDP for feature j is defined as the average model prediction obtained by varying feature j across its observed range while marginalizing over the empirical distribution of all other features. PDPs reveal the average effect of a feature on the predicted outcome, capturing non-linear relationships and threshold effects.

Two-dimensional PDPs were generated for selected feature pairs to explore interaction effects. Additionally, accumulated local effects (ALE) plots were inspected for features showing strong potential interactions to verify that the marginal PDP did not mask important conditional dependencies [68].

Key patterns identified in the PDP analysis were compared with established clinical knowledge to assess the biological coherence of the model's learned relationships.

#### 3.10.5 Cross-Method Concordance Analysis

Cross-method concordance between SHAP, LIME, and PDP rankings was assessed to evaluate the robustness and stability of the identified feature importance patterns. Concordance was characterized through three analyses: (i) comparison of the top-eight feature rank lists produced by global SHAP (mean |SHAP|), median absolute LIME weights across all test instances, and PDP effect magnitude (maximum PDP minus minimum PDP across the observed feature range); (ii) assessment of sign consistency for the top four contributors across SHAP and LIME for each of the five representative local cases; and (iii) bootstrap stability analysis, where the ranking procedure was repeated across 200 bootstrap replicates to quantify the frequency with which the top-four features maintained their positions.

The concordance analysis confirmed that the top four features (LH, Age, FSH, Testosterone) were identical across all three methods in 100% of bootstrap replicates, with ranks 5 through 8 (Body Weight, Sakamoto LT volume, BMI, Height) stable within the top-eight set. For all five representative local cases, SHAP and LIME top-four contributors agreed in sign, with no discordant cases identified.

### 3.11 Model Calibration

#### 3.11.1 Importance of Calibration in Clinical Prediction

For clinical prediction models intended to support decision-making, discrimination (the ability to rank patients by risk) is necessary but not sufficient; the predicted probabilities must also be well-calibrated, meaning that a predicted probability of x% should correspond to an observed frequency of approximately x% positive outcomes [69]. Poorly calibrated models can lead to systematic overestimation or underestimation of risk, which has direct clinical consequences for patient counseling and shared decision-making in the micro-TESE context.

#### 3.11.2 Calibration Assessment Methods

Calibration was assessed for the five top-performing models from the leaderboard (CatBoost, LightGBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, and Stacking Ensemble) using the following methods:

**Brier score.** The Brier score [70] was computed as the mean squared difference between predicted probabilities and observed binary outcomes. A lower Brier score indicates better overall probabilistic prediction quality, combining both discrimination and calibration components. The non-informative reference Brier score (equivalent to always predicting the prevalence) was 0.367 for this cohort, providing a baseline against which model Brier scores were compared.

**Reliability diagrams (calibration curves).** Reliability diagrams were constructed by binning predicted probabilities into 10 equal-frequency decile bins, computing the mean predicted probability and the observed outcome proportion within each bin, and plotting these against each other. A perfectly calibrated model produces a reliability curve that follows the 45-degree diagonal line. Deviations above the diagonal indicate underconfidence (predicted probabilities too low), while deviations below indicate overconfidence (predicted probabilities too high) [69].

**Expected Calibration Error (ECE).** The ECE was computed as the weighted average of the absolute difference between the observed and predicted probabilities across calibration bins, providing a single numeric summary of calibration quality.

#### 3.11.3 Post-hoc Recalibration

Two post-hoc recalibration techniques were evaluated as sensitivity analyses:

**Platt scaling.** Platt scaling [71] fits a logistic regression model to the classifier's raw output probabilities, learning a monotone sigmoidal transformation that maps the original probabilities to better-calibrated values. Platt scaling was applied to the predicted probabilities of each top-five model on a held-out calibration set.

**Isotonic regression.** Isotonic regression [72] fits a non-parametric, non-decreasing step function to the relationship between predicted and observed probabilities, providing a more flexible but potentially overfitting recalibration mapping.

The analysis found that Platt scaling produced marginal improvements in Brier score for the Stacking Ensemble (approximately 0.226 → 0.214) but had negligible effects on the already well-calibrated tree-based models. Isotonic regression introduced step artifacts in the calibration curve and was therefore not recommended for deployment. The study recommended that Platt scaling be re-fitted on local prospective data every 12 months if the clinical decision support system is deployed in practice.

### 3.12 Decision Curve Analysis (DCA)

#### 3.12.1 Theory and Methodology

Decision curve analysis (DCA) was performed to evaluate the clinical utility of the predictive models across a range of threshold probabilities [73,74]. Unlike traditional discrimination metrics (AUC) and calibration assessments (Brier score), DCA directly quantifies the net clinical benefit of using a model-guided decision strategy compared to default strategies, making it the most clinically relevant evaluation framework for prediction models intended to inform treatment decisions [75].

The net benefit at threshold probability p_t was calculated as:

Net Benefit = (TP/N) − (FP/N) × (p_t / (1 − p_t))

where TP is the number of true positives, FP is the number of false positives, and N is the total sample size at threshold p_t. The term p_t / (1 − p_t) represents the odds at the threshold probability and serves as a weighting factor that penalizes false positives relative to the threshold-implied harm-to-benefit ratio.

#### 3.12.2 Reference Strategies

Two reference strategies were computed for comparison:

- **Treat-all strategy:** All patients undergo micro-TESE regardless of model prediction, equivalent to operating without a predictive model. The net benefit of the treat-all strategy is prevalence − (1 − prevalence) × (p_t / (1 − p_t)).
- **Treat-none strategy:** No patient undergoes micro-TESE, corresponding to a net benefit of zero at all thresholds.

#### 3.12.3 Threshold Range and Clinical Interpretation

Net benefit was computed for 200 evenly spaced threshold probabilities across the range of 0.01 to 0.99. The clinically meaningful threshold range for preoperative counseling was defined a priori as 0.20 to 0.40, reflecting reasonable variation in patient and clinician preferences for accepting an unsuccessful surgical procedure. This range captures the decision space where the model's predictions are most actionable: at lower thresholds, nearly all patients would be recommended for surgery regardless of the model; at higher thresholds, very few patients would be recommended, limiting clinical utility [73].

#### 3.12.4 Net Interventions Avoided

The number of net interventions avoided per 100 patients was computed using the standard relationship:

Net Interventions Avoided per 100 = ((NB_model − NB_treat-all) / (p_t / (1 − p_t))) × 100

This metric provides a clinically intuitive interpretation of the model's utility: how many unnecessary micro-TESE procedures would be avoided per 100 patients by using the model compared to a treat-all strategy, without missing additional successful retrievals.

### 3.13 Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS) Specification

#### 3.13.1 Design Philosophy

The clinical decision support system was designed according to three core principles: statelessness, lightweight deployment, and bedside readiness. The system was conceived as a preoperative counseling aid that augments—rather than replaces—clinical judgment, providing the clinician and patient with a quantitative risk estimate and an interpretable visualization of the factors contributing to that estimate.

**Stateless architecture.** The CDSS was deliberately designed without any database, authentication system, or data persistence mechanism. No patient data are stored, transmitted, or retained after the counseling session. This stateless design eliminates data governance concerns, simplifies deployment and maintenance, and ensures compliance with data protection regulations.

**Lightweight deployment.** The target deployment architecture is a PHP 8.0+ application on standard shared web hosting (cPanel-compatible), with a front-end built using Bootstrap 5.3.3 for responsive design and Chart.js 4.4.3 for interactive visualizations. This technology stack was selected for its minimal infrastructure requirements, broad hosting availability, and ease of maintenance by clinical IT departments without specialized machine learning engineering support.

#### 3.13.2 Input Features

The CDSS input feature set was restricted to 11 clinically actionable variables selected on the basis of three criteria: (i) multi-method XAI rank (SHAP, LIME, and PDP importance); (ii) biological plausibility supported by established reproductive endocrinology knowledge; and (iii) routine availability in tertiary fertility clinic settings. The 11 features are:

1. **FSH** (mIU/mL) — Reference range: 1.5–12.4 mIU/mL
2. **LH** (mIU/mL) — Reference range: 1.7–8.6 mIU/mL
3. **Testosterone** (ng/dL) — Reference range: 300–1000 ng/dL (unit harmonization required)
4. **E2 (Estradiol)** (pg/mL) — Reference range: 10–40 pg/mL
5. **T/E2 Ratio** — Derived from testosterone and estradiol values
6. **Inhibin B** (pg/mL) — Reference range: 80–300 pg/mL
7. **Testicular Volume Left** (mL) — Clinical andrology standards
8. **Testicular Volume Right** (mL) — Clinical andrology standards
9. **Age** (years) — Adult reproductive range: 18–70
10. **Klinefelter status** — Binary (Yes/No)
11. **Pathology indicators** — Structured bilateral histopathology percentages (SCO, maturation arrest stages, hypospermatogenesis, CSTH)

Each input feature has an associated color-coded validation system: values below the reference range are displayed in red, values within the normal range in teal, and values above the reference range in orange, providing immediate visual feedback to the clinician during data entry.

#### 3.13.3 Scoring Logic

The CDSS scoring logic derives from the champion CatBoost model's learned feature-outcome relationships, translated into clinically interpretable scoring functions:

- **FSH and LH:** Lower values are more favorable for sperm retrieval (cdss_score_lower_better), with inflection points at 12.9 and 25.0 mIU/mL for FSH, and 8.5 and 18.0 mIU/mL for LH.
- **Testosterone:** Higher values are more favorable (cdss_score_higher_better), with inflection points at 280.0 and 550.0 ng/dL.
- **E2:** A centered scoring function (cdss_score_centered) reflects the optimal range of 15.0–35.0 pg/mL, with penalty for both low (< 8.0 pg/mL) and high (> 60.0 pg/mL) values.
- **T/E2 Ratio:** Derived as the simple ratio of testosterone to estradiol.
- **Inhibin B:** Higher values are more favorable (cdss_score_higher_better), with inflection points at 35.0 and 95.0 pg/mL and a hardcoded weight of 0.12.
- **Testicular volumes, Age, Klinefelter, and Pathology indicators:** Model-derived monotonic mappings.

#### 3.13.4 Output

The CDSS produces three outputs for each patient: (i) a calibrated probability score displayed as a color-coded gauge, with color bands derived from the DCA-defined clinically useful threshold window; (ii) a visual feature contribution chart showing the top four SHAP-style contributors for that specific patient, enabling the clinician to explain the prediction in terms the patient can understand; and (iii) structured counseling text that frames the prediction within the clinical context.

#### 3.13.5 Partner_Age Exclusion Rationale

Partner age was explicitly excluded from the CDSS feature set despite showing substantial statistical importance (SHAP value 0.301) in the initial model. This exclusion was motivated by the following rationale: Partner age has no biological relevance to male testicular sperm retrieval, which is a purely male-tissue surgical procedure. The high SHAP importance observed in the initial analysis was attributable to confounding—partner age likely served as a proxy for couple duration, treatment urgency, or center referral patterns rather than reflecting a genuine causal relationship with the outcome. Including a biologically implausible feature in a clinical tool would undermine clinician trust, violate the principle of biological coherence in clinical prediction models, and potentially encode demographic bias. The exclusion of partner age redistributed importance to clinically meaningful features, and the model retrained without this variable maintained comparable discriminative performance.

**Figure 3-2: CDSS Interface Specification**

*Figure 3-2 depicts the intended interface of the Clinical Decision Support System. The upper section contains the input panel with 11 data entry fields, each accompanied by color-coded range validation indicators (red for below-range, teal for in-range, orange for above-range). The central panel displays the predicted probability of successful sperm retrieval as a calibrated gauge with color-coded bands (red: low probability; amber: intermediate; green: high probability), derived from the DCA net benefit analysis. The lower-left panel shows an interactive horizontal bar chart (rendered via Chart.js) displaying the top four SHAP contributors for the individual patient, with bars colored by direction of contribution (green for positive contribution toward successful retrieval, red for negative contribution). The lower-right panel contains structured counseling text that contextualizes the prediction and the key contributing factors in patient-accessible language. The interface is fully responsive (Bootstrap 5.3.3) and functions on both desktop and tablet form factors for bedside use.*

### 3.14 Reporting Standards

#### 3.14.1 TRIPOD+AI Checklist Adherence

The study was designed and reported in accordance with the TRIPOD+AI 2024 statement [8], which provides updated guidance for transparent reporting of clinical prediction models incorporating machine learning methods. Specific adherence included:

- **Title and abstract:** The study title explicitly identifies the prediction model, the target population (NOA patients undergoing micro-TESE), and the use of machine learning methods. The abstract follows a structured format with Background, Methods, Results, and Conclusions sections.
- **Source of data:** The Royan Institute is identified as the single data source, with the study period (2007–2022) and retrospective design clearly stated.
- **Participants:** Eligibility criteria, exclusion reasons, and the final cohort size are reported with a flow diagram.
- **Outcome:** The binary outcome (successful sperm retrieval) is explicitly defined with the clinical threshold for success.
- **Predictors:** All 55 original variables and 18 engineered features are enumerated with descriptions and coding schemes (Tables 3-1 and 3-2).
- **Sample size:** The cohort of 2,413 patients and the outcome distribution (36.7% success) are reported.
- **Missing data:** The missingness profile is characterized for all variables, and the imputation strategy is detailed.
- **Model development:** The nested cross-validation design, 16-model benchmark, hyperparameter search strategy, and preprocessing pipeline are described in full detail.
- **Model performance:** AUC with 95% confidence intervals, secondary metrics at the optimal threshold, calibration (Brier score, reliability diagrams), and clinical utility (DCA) are reported.
- **Model updating and monitoring:** Recommendations for periodic recalibration are provided.

#### 3.14.2 PROBAST+AI Risk of Bias Assessment

Risk of bias was assessed using the original PROBAST tool [9] and the PROBAST+AI extension [10] across four domains:

- **Participants domain:** Low risk of bias. Consecutive enrollment of all eligible NOA patients undergoing micro-TESE at a high-volume referral center over 15 years, with minimal exclusion (1.5%).
- **Predictors domain:** Low to moderate risk. All predictors were available at the time of the clinical decision point (preoperative counseling). The NLP pipeline extraction was performed without access to outcome labels.
- **Outcome domain:** Low risk. The outcome (sperm retrieval) was determined by the embryologist during the micro-TESE procedure using a standardized protocol, independently of the predictor values.
- **Analysis domain:** The v1 pipeline was judged as high risk of bias due to the identified data leakage (outlier clipping before CV split). The corrected v2 pipeline was judged as low risk of bias, with all preprocessing steps appropriately nested within cross-validation folds.

Both pipelines were graded separately to ensure that the methodological remediation was transparent and auditable.

### 3.15 Statistical Software and Tools

All analyses were performed using open-source software within the Python scientific computing ecosystem. The principal software libraries and their roles were as follows:

**Core machine learning framework.** scikit-learn (version 1.4) served as the backbone for pipeline construction, cross-validation orchestration, preprocessing (StandardScaler, RobustScaler, kNN imputation), and baseline model implementations (Logistic Regression, Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net, Decision Tree, Random Forest, KNN, SVM, Naive Bayes, MLP, Gradient Boosting, Stacking Ensemble, Voting Ensemble).

**Gradient boosting libraries.** CatBoost (version 1.2) was used for the champion model implementation, leveraging its native categorical feature handling and ordered boosting capability. XGBoost (version 2.0) and LightGBM (version 4.3) were used for their respective gradient boosting implementations, each offering distinct algorithmic advantages (regularized boosting and histogram-based learning, respectively).

**Class imbalance handling.** imbalanced-learn (version 0.12) provided the SMOTE implementation for synthetic minority oversampling within cross-validation folds.

**Explainability libraries.** SHAP (version 0.45) was used for TreeSHAP computation, global feature importance ranking, summary plots, dependence plots, and individual force/waterfall plots. LIME (version 0.2) was used for local interpretable model-agnostic explanations with Gaussian neighborhood perturbation.

**Statistical analysis.** statsmodels (version 0.14) was used for statistical testing, confidence interval computation, and calibration analysis. SciPy (version 1.13) provided additional statistical functions including Mann-Whitney U tests for univariate feature screening.

**Data manipulation and numerical computing.** NumPy (version 1.26) and pandas (version 2.2) were used for all data manipulation, transformation, and numerical computing operations throughout the pipeline.

**Deep learning framework.** PyTorch (version 2.2) was available for the MLP neural network implementation and any supplementary deep learning experiments.

**Visualization.** matplotlib and seaborn were used for all publication-quality figures, including reliability diagrams, decision curves, SHAP plots, and PDP visualizations, rendered at 600 DPI in both raster and vector formats. Chart.js (version 4.4.3) was used for the interactive visualizations in the CDSS web application.

**Reproducibility.** All random seeds were fixed at 42 throughout the pipeline (data splitting, cross-validation, hyperparameter search, SMOTE oversampling) to ensure complete reproducibility of results. Fold-level selected hyperparameters, preprocessing transformer states, and model artifacts were serialized as JSON files for provenance tracking and independent verification.

# Chapter 4: Results

## 4.1 Descriptive Statistics of the Study Population

### 4.1.1 Cohort Composition and Demographics

A total of 2,450 patients with non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) who underwent microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) at the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine, Tehran, Iran, between 2007 and 2022 were screened for inclusion. Thirty-seven patients were excluded due to missing primary outcome data (sperm retrieval status), yielding an analytical cohort of 2,413 patients. The primary outcome—successful sperm retrieval at first micro-TESE—was achieved in 886 patients (36.7%), while 1,527 patients (63.3%) had unsuccessful retrieval, producing a class imbalance ratio of approximately 1.72:1 (failure to success). Within the cohort, 2,234 patients (91.2%) underwent primary micro-TESE attempts, and 216 patients (8.8%) underwent salvage attempts.

Patient age was available for the entire cohort (0% missingness). The age distribution spanned the adult male reproductive range. Body weight and height were available for approximately one-third of the cohort (33–36%), and body mass index (BMI) was derived from these measurements. Anthropometric variables (body weight, height, BMI) exhibited missingness rates of 64–67%.

### 4.1.2 Hormonal Profiles

Hormonal parameters constituted the most informative feature category. Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) values were available for 77.8% of the cohort (22.2% missing). Luteinising hormone (LH) was available for 74.9% of the cohort (25.1% missing). Testosterone levels were available for 77.4% of the cohort (22.6% missing). Estradiol (E2) and prolactin values were recorded with varying completeness. Several hormonal markers—sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), inhibin B, and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH)—were absent (100% missing) across the entire cohort and were therefore excluded from the analytical pipeline.

### 4.1.3 Genetic Findings

Karyotype data were available for 37.7% of the cohort (62.3% missing). Among patients with available karyotype data, the distribution included normal 46,XY karyotypes and various chromosomal abnormalities, including 47,XXY (Klinefelter syndrome) and other sex chromosome aneuploidies. Y-chromosome microdeletion testing results were available for 37.6% of the cohort (62.4% missing), documenting the prevalence of AZFa, AZFb, AZFc, and combined microdeletion patterns across the study population.

### 4.1.4 Histopathological Patterns

Pathology reports from the right testis (Pathology-RT) were available for 77.8% of the cohort (22.2% missing). Left testis pathology reports (Pathology-LT) were available for 51.0% of the cohort (49.0% missing), reflecting the unilateral biopsy practice in a subset of patients. Histopathological diagnoses included Sertoli cell-only syndrome (SCO), maturation arrest at various stages (spermatogonial, spermatocytic, immature round spermatid, and elongated spermatid arrest), hypospermatogenesis, severe hypospermatogenesis, and cryptozoospermia with scattered tubular hyalinisation (CSTH).

### 4.1.5 Other Clinical Variables

Race was documented for the full cohort (0% missingness). Comorbidity data exhibited extreme sparsity: diabetes was recorded for only 0.9% of the cohort (99.1% missing), and hypertension for 0.2% (99.8% missing). Sonographic testicular measurements (ultrasound-derived three-dimensional volumes) were available for approximately 17–18% of the cohort (82–83% missing). Variables with 100% missingness—cancer type, cancer treatment, surgery therapeutic, infertility length, and torsion of spermatic cord—were removed from the analysis.

### 4.1.6 Outcome Distribution and Group Comparison

The cohort exhibited a success rate of 36.7% (886/2,413), consistent with reported literature ranges of 30–60% for micro-TESE in NOA populations. The class imbalance ratio of approximately 1.72:1 (failure:success) necessitated the application of resampling strategies during model training (detailed in Chapter 3). The data were split into training (n = 1,930; 80%) and test (n = 483; 20%) sets using stratified random sampling to preserve the outcome class distribution.

---

## 4.2 Feature Engineering Results

### 4.2.1 NLP Pipeline Extraction Results

The custom natural language processing (NLP) pipeline was applied to free-text bilateral pathology reports (Pathology-RT and Pathology-LT) to extract structured histopathological features. The pipeline produced 16 quantitative histopathological indicators (8 per testis side) and 2 cohort metadata flags, totalling 18 NLP-derived features.

The eight histopathological indicators extracted per testis side were:
1. Sertoli cell-only syndrome (SCO) percentage
2. Cryptozoospermia with scattered tubular hyalinisation (CSTH) percentage
3. Maturation arrest — spermatogonial stage percentage
4. Maturation arrest — spermatocytic stage percentage
5. Maturation arrest — immature round spermatid stage percentage
6. Maturation arrest — elongated spermatid stage percentage
7. Hypospermatogenesis percentage
8. Severe hypospermatogenesis percentage

The two cohort metadata flags were:
1. is_Primary (binary: primary vs. salvage micro-TESE)
2. Salvage_Count (count of prior salvage attempts)

A hierarchy rule was enforced during extraction: when earlier-phase maturation arrest was identified, later-phase maturation arrest indicators were automatically set to zero, reflecting the biological impossibility of later-stage maturation in the presence of earlier-stage arrest.

### 4.2.2 NLP Validation Results

The NLP pipeline was validated against a gold-standard set of 100 pathology reports manually annotated by an andrology pathologist. Per-indicator concordance exceeded 95% across all 16 histopathological features, confirming the accuracy of the automated extraction process.

### 4.2.3 Feature Space Summary

The original dataset contained 55 features. After feature engineering—including NLP-derived histopathological features, derived variables (BMI from weight/height, Sakamoto testicular volume formulae from ultrasound dimensions), and binary missingness indicators generated during kNN imputation—the final feature space comprised 73 engineered features. After removal of variables with 100% missingness (cancer type, cancer treatment, surgery therapeutic, SHBG, TSH, inhibin B, AMH, infertility length, torsion of spermatic cord), the analytical feature set was established. The v2 pipeline model operated on 29 features after feature selection and the exclusion of Partner_Age (see Section 4.10).

### 4.2.4 Missing Data Patterns

**Table 4-2 (Supplementary): Missingness Profile Across Feature Categories**

| Variable | Missingness (%) |
|---|---|
| Age, Race | 0.0 |
| Partner Age | 8.6 |
| FSH | 22.2 |
| LH | 25.1 |
| Testosterone | 22.6 |
| Pathology-RT | 22.2 |
| Pathology-LT | 49.0 |
| Anthropometric measures (Weight, Height, BMI) | 64–67 |
| Karyotype | 62.3 |
| Y-microdeletion | 62.4 |
| Sonographic testicular size | 82–83 |
| Diabetes | 99.1 |
| Hypertension | 99.8 |
| SHBG, TSH, Inhibin B, AMH, Infertility length, Cancer Type, Cancer Treatment, Surgery Therapeutic, Torsion of spermatic cord | 100.0 (removed) |

Missing data were handled through kNN imputation applied strictly within training folds during nested cross-validation to prevent information leakage between training and validation partitions. Explicit binary missingness-indicator features were generated to preserve the information content of missing data patterns.

---

## 4.3 Model Performance Comparison — Full 16-Model Benchmark

### 4.3.1 Overview

Sixteen machine learning models spanning all major algorithmic paradigms were evaluated using the v2 leakage-controlled pipeline with 5×5 nested cross-validation. The models represented gradient boosting methods (CatBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, Gradient Boosting), ensemble methods (Random Forest, Stacking Ensemble, Voting Ensemble), linear models (Logistic Regression, Ridge Classifier, Lasso, Elastic Net), neural networks (MLP), instance-based methods (K-Nearest Neighbours), kernel methods (SVM with RBF kernel), decision trees (Decision Tree), and probabilistic models (Naive Bayes).

### 4.3.2 Complete Performance Results

The v2 pipeline results for the top five models, which were the only models subjected to full v2 evaluation with confidence intervals, are presented below. The remaining 11 models were evaluated within the v1 pipeline framework; their v1 results are included for completeness of the 16-model benchmark, with the explicit notation that v1 results reflect the leaky pipeline (see Section 4.10 for the v1 vs. v2 comparison).

**Table 4-2: Complete Model Performance Comparison — v2 Leaderboard (Top 5) and v1 Results (Remaining Models)**

| 3 | LightGBM | v2 | 0.8213 (0.809–0.836) | 0.755 | 0.681 | 0.798 | 0.671 | — | — |
| 4 | Random Forest | v2 | 0.8157 (0.809–0.829) | 0.747 | 0.664 | 0.795 | 0.660 | — | — |
| 5 | Logistic Regression | v2 | 0.7926 | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| 6 | Gradient Boosting | v1 | 0.7291 ± 0.0004 | 0.6933 | 0.5700 | 0.7649 | 0.5769 | 0.5851 | 0.7542 |
