### 4.3.3 Model Ranking by AUC-ROC

The gradient boosting family dominated the top positions. CatBoost achieved the highest AUC-ROC of 0.8306, followed by XGBoost (0.8214), LightGBM (0.8213), and Random Forest (0.8157). Logistic Regression in the v2 pipeline achieved an AUC of 0.7926, serving as the linear baseline. The separation between the top four gradient boosting/ensemble models was narrow (AUC range: 0.8157–0.8306), spanning a 0.0149-point range. The gap between the gradient boosting cluster and the linear/classical models was substantially larger.

Among v1-evaluated models, Gradient Boosting (0.7291 ± 0.0004), Stacking Ensemble (0.7222 ± 0.0043), and Voting Ensemble (0.7060 ± 0.0207) formed a second tier with AUCs in the 0.70–0.73 range. The Gradient Boosting model exhibited the smallest standard deviation (0.0004) across outer folds, indicating exceptional stability but lower absolute performance compared to v2-evaluated models. The Stacking Ensemble, despite combining predictions from multiple base learners, did not surpass the individual gradient boosting models, achieving an AUC of 0.7222. The Voting Ensemble (AUC 0.7060) demonstrated the highest specificity among this tier (0.7996) but the lowest sensitivity (0.4819), reflecting a conservative classification threshold.

Classical machine learning approaches formed a third performance tier. The MLP neural network (AUC 0.6696 ± 0.0262) achieved moderate discriminative ability, with a substantial overfit gap of 0.3177 (train AUC 0.9873 vs. test AUC 0.6696), suggesting that the neural network architecture overparameterised for this dataset size. The Ridge Classifier (AUC 0.6681 ± 0.0193) and K-Nearest Neighbours (AUC 0.6521 ± 0.0241) performed similarly. KNN notably achieved the highest sensitivity among classical models (0.7088) but at the cost of the lowest specificity (0.4833), indicating a tendency to over-classify patients as likely to succeed. The Decision Tree (AUC 0.6457 ± 0.0018) showed the most stable performance (smallest standard deviation) among non-boosting models but limited discriminative power.

The lowest-performing tier comprised regularised linear models and non-linear baselines. Elastic Net (AUC 0.5950 ± 0.0950) and Lasso (AUC 0.5949 ± 0.0949) both exhibited extremely high variance across folds, with standard deviations exceeding 0.09 — suggesting instability in the linear feature representation. The SVM with RBF kernel (AUC 0.5474 ± 0.1504) demonstrated the highest variance of any model, with a standard deviation of 0.1504, and achieved only marginal discrimination above chance. Naive Bayes was the lowest-performing model (AUC 0.5232 ± 0.0020), exhibiting extreme calibration failure: while its sensitivity was high (0.8205), its specificity was only 0.2259, and its train AUC of 1.0000 with an overfit gap of 0.4768 confirmed complete overfitting to the training distribution.

## 4.4 Champion Model: CatBoost v2

### 4.4.1 Detailed Performance Metrics

CatBoost was selected as the champion model based on its superior AUC-ROC performance in the v2 leakage-controlled pipeline. The complete operating metrics for CatBoost v2 are presented below.

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| AUC-ROC | 0.8306 |
| 95% Confidence Interval | 0.823–0.845 |
| Accuracy | 0.7693 |
| Sensitivity (Recall) | 0.6784 |
| Specificity | 0.8220 |
| Positive Predictive Value (PPV/Precision) | 0.6883 |
| Negative Predictive Value (NPV) | 0.8152 |
| F1-Score | 0.6832 |
| Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) | 0.5019 |
| Brier Score | 0.2131 |
| Optimal Decision Threshold (Youden's J range) | 0.444–0.507 |

CatBoost achieved an accuracy of 0.7693 (76.93%), correctly classifying approximately three-quarters of all patients. Sensitivity was 0.6784 (67.84%), indicating that the model correctly identified approximately two-thirds of patients who would have successful sperm retrieval. Specificity was 0.8220 (82.20%), indicating that the model correctly identified more than four-fifths of patients who would have unsuccessful retrieval. The PPV of 0.6883 indicated that 68.83% of patients predicted to have successful retrieval did indeed have successful outcomes. The NPV of 0.8152 indicated that 81.52% of patients predicted to have unsuccessful retrieval were correctly classified.

The F1-score of 0.6832 represented the harmonic mean of precision and recall, reflecting balanced performance. The Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) of 0.5019 confirmed substantial agreement between predicted and observed outcomes, accounting for all four quadrants of the confusion matrix.

### 4.4.2 Confusion Matrix

At the optimal decision threshold identified by Youden's J index (range 0.444–0.507), the CatBoost v2 model produced the following classification pattern across the cross-validated test folds. With a sensitivity of 0.6784 and specificity of 0.8220, the model correctly identified 67.84% of patients who achieved successful sperm retrieval (true positives) while correctly classifying 82.20% of patients who did not achieve successful retrieval (true negatives). The false negative rate was 32.16%, indicating that approximately one-third of patients with successful outcomes were incorrectly predicted as failures. The false positive rate was 17.80%, indicating that fewer than one-fifth of patients with unsuccessful outcomes were incorrectly predicted as successes.

The positive predictive value (PPV) of 0.6883 indicated that among all patients classified as likely to have successful retrieval, 68.83% did achieve success. The negative predictive value (NPV) of 0.8152 indicated that among all patients classified as unlikely to succeed, 81.52% indeed had unsuccessful outcomes. The asymmetry between PPV and NPV reflected the class imbalance (36.7% success rate): the higher base rate of failure naturally supported higher NPV. The Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) of 0.5019 provided a balanced summary of all four quadrants of the confusion matrix, confirming moderate-to-good overall classification quality without bias toward either class.

### 4.4.3 Threshold Analysis

The Youden's J index optimal threshold range was 0.444–0.507. This range was identified through the 5×5 nested cross-validation procedure, where each outer fold produced a slightly different optimal threshold. The narrow range (width: 0.063) indicated stability of the optimal operating point across folds. At the lower bound (0.444), the model exhibited marginally higher sensitivity at the cost of lower specificity; at the upper bound (0.507), specificity increased with a modest reduction in sensitivity.

### 4.4.4 Performance Stability Across Cross-Validation Folds

The 5×5 nested cross-validation design yielded performance estimates across 25 inner-fold evaluations per outer fold. The CatBoost v2 AUC of 0.8306 represented the mean across outer folds, with a 95% confidence interval of 0.823–0.845. The confidence interval width of 0.022 indicated acceptable variability across the cross-validation procedure, supporting the generalisability of the performance estimate within the study population.

### 4.4.5 Hyperparameters of the Champion Model

The final CatBoost v2 model was trained with the following hyperparameters, identified through 50-iteration Bayesian optimisation within the inner folds of the nested cross-validation:

| Hyperparameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Tree depth | 6 |
| Iterations | 657 |
| L2 leaf regularisation | 3.51 |
| Learning rate | 0.00617 |
| Scaler | RobustScaler |
| Pathology encoding | ordinal_detailed_plus_raw |

**Figure 4-2: CatBoost v2 ROC Curve with AUC and 95% Confidence Interval**

*[Textual Description]* The ROC curve for CatBoost v2 is presented as a single bold curve rising steeply from the origin (0,0) toward the upper-left corner of the plot, achieving a true positive rate exceeding 0.60 at a false positive rate of approximately 0.15. The curve then follows a gradually flattening trajectory toward (1,1). The area under the curve (AUC = 0.8306) is shaded. A light-coloured band surrounding the ROC curve represents the 95% confidence interval (0.823–0.845), derived from the nested cross-validation procedure. The diagonal reference line (AUC = 0.50) is shown for comparison. The optimal operating point, corresponding to the Youden's J index threshold range of 0.444–0.507, is marked on the curve, indicating the sensitivity of 0.6784 and specificity of 0.8220 at the selected threshold.

The four hormonal and demographic features—LH (0.3170), Age (0.2701), FSH (0.2503), and Testosterone (0.2118)—dominated the global importance ranking, collectively accounting for the majority of the model's predictive signal. The top four features were separated from the fifth-ranked feature (Body Weight, 0.1740) by a SHAP value gap of 0.0378.

Anthropometric features (Body Weight: 0.1740; BMI: 0.1690; Height: 0.1662) and testicular volumetric measures (Sakamoto_LT_mL: 0.1706; RT_XYZ_Sono: 0.1564; Testicular_volume_LT_Guess: 0.1292; Testicular_volume_RT_Guess: 0.1107) formed a second tier of importance (ranks 5–12). Seminal plasma pH (0.1277) was interleaved within this tier.

Minor contributors included sonographic left testicular volume (LT_XYZ_Sono: 0.0849), estradiol (E2: 0.0435), Sakamoto right testicular volume (0.0393), and prolactin (0.0202). All categorical features—including both pathology report variables, race, family history of infertility, lifestyle habits, occupational exposure, comorbidities (diabetes, hypertension), surgical history, varicocele, testicular biopsy, inguinal hernia, and torsion—contributed zero SHAP importance in the final model.

### 4.5.4 SHAP Dependence Plots for Top Features

SHAP dependence plots were generated for the top four features to visualise the relationship between feature values and their SHAP contributions:

**LH:** The SHAP dependence plot for LH demonstrated a monotonically decreasing relationship. SHAP values were positive (favouring successful retrieval) at low LH levels and transitioned to negative values (favouring unsuccessful retrieval) as LH increased above approximately 9 mIU/mL. The transition zone exhibited interaction colouring with FSH, indicating that the combined effect of elevated LH and FSH was stronger than either alone.

**Age:** The SHAP dependence plot for Age exhibited a non-monotone (inverted U-shaped) pattern. SHAP values were most positive (favouring successful retrieval) in the 30–40 year age range. Below 30 years and above 40 years, SHAP values declined, with the most negative contributions observed at both age extremes.

**FSH:** The SHAP dependence plot for FSH showed a monotonically decreasing pattern. Lower FSH values were associated with positive SHAP values, with a clinically notable inflection zone around 12–15 mIU/mL above which SHAP values became increasingly negative.

**Testosterone:** The SHAP dependence plot for Testosterone demonstrated a monotonically increasing pattern up to approximately 4 ng/mL, beyond which the SHAP values plateaued. Low testosterone levels were associated with negative SHAP values (favouring unsuccessful retrieval), while higher levels contributed positively.

### 4.5.5 Feature Interaction Effects

SHAP interaction analysis revealed the following notable pairwise interactions:

1. **LH × FSH:** The strongest interaction pair. Simultaneously elevated LH and FSH produced SHAP interaction values exceeding the sum of their individual contributions, indicating a synergistic negative effect on predicted retrieval probability.

2. **Age × Testosterone:** Age modified the effect of testosterone. In younger patients (< 35 years), the testosterone SHAP contribution was attenuated; in older patients (> 45 years), low testosterone had a larger negative SHAP contribution.

3. **Testicular volume × FSH:** Larger testicular volumes partially offset the negative SHAP contribution of elevated FSH, suggesting a compensatory relationship.

### 4.5.6 Directionality of Feature Effects

The directionality of the top features' effects on predicted retrieval probability, as identified by SHAP analysis, is summarised:

| Feature | Direction | Description |
|---|---|---|
| LH | Higher → lower probability | Elevated LH associated with reduced retrieval probability |
| Age | Non-monotone | 30–40 years most favourable; worsening at both extremes |
| FSH | Higher → lower probability | Elevated FSH associated with reduced retrieval probability |
| Testosterone | Higher → higher probability (plateau ~4 ng/mL) | Higher testosterone associated with increased retrieval probability up to ~4 ng/mL |
| Body Weight | U-shaped | Extreme values in either direction unfavourable |
| BMI | U-shaped | Normal BMI range most favourable |
| Testicular volume | Higher → higher probability (plateau ~12 mL) | Larger volumes monotonically favourable, plateauing above ~12 mL |

Five representative cases were examined to illustrate local SHAP explanations:

|---|---|---|---|
| 2162 | 0.7822 | Success | Age (+0.567 at 56 years), FSH (+0.411), LH (+0.181), Testosterone (−0.086) |

| 1814 | 0.7830 | Success | E2 + anthropometric + hormonal combination |

Case 2162 demonstrated a high predicted probability (0.7822) driven primarily by age (SHAP +0.567 at 56 years) and FSH (SHAP +0.411), with a concordant successful outcome. Case 765 showed a low predicted probability (0.2836) driven by negative age contribution (SHAP −0.320 at 40 years), concordant with the unsuccessful outcome. Case 1833 was notable for the dominance of a single feature—right testicular ultrasound volume (RT_XYZ_Sono)—with a SHAP contribution of +2.060 at 2,223 mm³, far exceeding any other feature's contribution in the cohort and resulting in the highest predicted probability observed (0.8836).

## 4.6 LIME Analysis Results

### 4.6.1 Local Explanation Examples

Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) were generated for the same five representative patients examined in the SHAP analysis (Section 4.5.7). LIME approximated the CatBoost model's behaviour locally using interpretable linear models fitted to perturbed instances around each target patient.

For Case 2162 (predicted probability 0.7822, actual success), LIME identified Age, FSH, and LH as the top three contributing features, consistent with the SHAP local explanation. LIME coefficients indicated positive contributions for Age and FSH and a smaller positive contribution for LH. For Case 765 (predicted probability 0.2836, actual failure), LIME ranked Age as the primary negative contributor, followed by pH and testicular volume, again consistent with the SHAP ordering.

### 4.6.2 Concordance Between LIME and SHAP

Across the five representative cases, LIME and SHAP agreed on the top four contributing features in all five instances. The sign (direction) of the contribution was concordant between LIME and SHAP for all top-4 features in all five cases. Minor discrepancies in the relative magnitude of feature contributions were observed between the two methods, attributable to their different approximation strategies (LIME uses local linear approximations; SHAP computes exact Shapley values for tree-based models via TreeSHAP).

### 4.6.3 Stability of LIME Explanations

LIME explanations exhibited stability when evaluated across patients with similar clinical profiles. For patients within the same clinical subgroup (e.g., similar age, FSH, and LH values), the top contributing features and their directional effects remained consistent across repeated LIME runs. The kernel width parameter of LIME was set to the default value, and perturbation was performed using 5,000 samples per explanation, ensuring adequate local neighbourhood coverage.

Across the representative cases, LIME explanations demonstrated interpretable local linear approximations consistent with the clinical expectations for each patient profile. For patients with elevated gonadotrophins (high LH and FSH), LIME consistently assigned negative coefficients to these features, paralleling the SHAP negative contributions. For patients with normal or favourable hormonal profiles, LIME assigned positive coefficients to testosterone and negative coefficients to age extremes. The concordance between LIME and SHAP, combined with the stability of LIME across similar patients, supports the robustness of the model's interpretability framework.

### 4.6.4 Cross-Method Concordance Summary

The top four features (LH, Age, FSH, Testosterone) were identified as the most important predictors by all three explainability methods (SHAP, LIME, and PDP) in 100% of 200 bootstrap replicates. Features ranked 5–8 (Body Weight, Sakamoto LT volume, BMI, Height) were stable within the top-8 set across all bootstrap replicates, though their relative ordering showed minor permutations between replicates. LIME and SHAP top-4 contributors agreed in sign (direction of contribution) for all five representative local cases examined. This three-method concordance confirmed that the feature importance rankings were not artefacts of any single explanation method but rather reflected genuine patterns in the underlying data.

### 4.7.1 PDP for Top Predictive Features

Partial dependence plots (PDPs) were computed for the top predictive features to visualise the marginal effect of each feature on the predicted probability of successful sperm retrieval, averaged across all patients.

**Figure 4-5: Partial Dependence Plots for LH, FSH, Age, and Testosterone**

*[Textual Description]* Four panels display the partial dependence functions:

**Panel A — LH:** The partial dependence function for LH shows a monotonically decreasing trend. The predicted probability of successful retrieval decreases as LH increases, with the steepest decline occurring in the range of approximately 5–15 mIU/mL. Above approximately 20 mIU/mL, the curve flattens, indicating diminishing marginal effect of further LH elevation. The clinical threshold of approximately 9 mIU/mL marks the zone of most rapid decline in predicted probability.

**Panel B — FSH:** The partial dependence function for FSH demonstrates a similar monotonically decreasing pattern. The predicted probability decreases with increasing FSH, with the clinically notable inflection zone at approximately 12–15 mIU/mL. The decline is steepest in the 5–20 mIU/mL range, with a plateau of low predicted probability above approximately 25–30 mIU/mL.

**Panel C — Age:** The partial dependence function for Age shows a non-monotone pattern. The predicted probability increases from the youngest ages, reaching its maximum in the 30–40 year range, and subsequently declines with advancing age. The pattern confirms the inverted U-shape observed in the SHAP analysis. Both extremes of age (< 25 years and > 50 years) were associated with reduced predicted probability.

**Panel D — Testosterone:** The partial dependence function for Testosterone demonstrates a monotonically increasing pattern up to approximately 4 ng/mL, after which the function plateaus. Higher testosterone levels are associated with higher predicted retrieval probability, but incremental gains diminish above the 4 ng/mL level.

### 4.7.2 Non-Linear Relationships Identified

The PDPs confirmed non-linear relationships for all four top features. LH and FSH showed threshold-based non-linearity (monotonic decline with plateau at extreme values). Age exhibited a bell-curved non-linearity. Testosterone showed an asymptotic increasing pattern. These non-linear patterns were consistent across SHAP dependence plots and PDP analysis.

### 4.7.3 Additional PDP Results

PDPs were also computed for Body Weight and BMI, both of which demonstrated mildly U-shaped relationships centred near normal BMI values. Testicular volume (both Sakamoto-derived and clinically estimated) exhibited a monotonically increasing partial dependence that plateaued above approximately 12 mL, consistent with the SHAP findings.

### 4.7.4 Feature Interaction PDPs

Two-dimensional PDPs for the LH × FSH interaction confirmed the synergistic negative effect: the combined elevation of both gonadotrophins reduced predicted probability more than the sum of their individual marginal effects. The Age × Testosterone interaction PDP showed that the beneficial effect of higher testosterone was most pronounced in the 30–45 year age range.
