## 4.8 Model Calibration Results

### 4.8.1 Brier Scores

Brier scores were computed for the top five models to assess calibration performance. The non-informative reference Brier score (prevalence-only model predicting the marginal class probability of 0.367 for all patients) was 0.367.

**Table 4-5: Calibration Metrics for Top Models**

| Model | Brier Score |
|---|---|
| LightGBM | 0.2006 |
| Gradient Boosting | 0.2025 |
| XGBoost | 0.2055 |
| CatBoost | 0.2131 |
| Stacking Ensemble | 0.2259 |
| Non-informative reference | 0.367 |

All five models achieved Brier scores substantially below the non-informative reference of 0.367, confirming calibration improvement over the naive prevalence-based prediction. LightGBM achieved the lowest Brier score (0.2006), followed by Gradient Boosting (0.2025), XGBoost (0.2055), and CatBoost (0.2131). The Stacking Ensemble exhibited the highest Brier score among the top five (0.2259).

CatBoost's Brier score of 0.2131 ranked fourth among the five evaluated models. The range of Brier scores across the top four gradient boosting models was narrow (0.2006–0.2131), spanning a difference of 0.0125.

### 4.8.2 Calibration Curve (Reliability Diagram)

**Figure 4-6: Calibration Curve (Reliability Diagram) for CatBoost v2**

*[Textual Description]* The calibration curve (reliability diagram) plots predicted probabilities (x-axis) against observed frequencies of successful retrieval (y-axis) across deciles of predicted probability. The diagonal line represents perfect calibration. The CatBoost v2 calibration curve follows the diagonal closely across most probability deciles, indicating well-calibrated predictions. In the lower probability deciles (0.0–0.3), the observed frequencies closely match predicted probabilities. In the middle deciles (0.3–0.6), minor deviations are observed but remain within acceptable bounds. In the highest probability decile (0.8–1.0), the curve deviates slightly below the diagonal, indicating mild over-confidence—the model predicts slightly higher probabilities than are observed in the highest-risk group.

### 4.8.3 Post-Calibration Analysis

Platt scaling (logistic calibration) was applied as a post-hoc recalibration method to all five models. For the four tree-based models (CatBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, Gradient Boosting), Platt scaling produced negligible improvement in Brier scores, consistent with their inherent calibration quality arising from the leaf-value averaging mechanism of gradient boosted decision trees. For the Stacking Ensemble, Platt scaling improved the Brier score from approximately 0.2259 to approximately 0.214, representing a meaningful reduction in calibration error for this meta-learner.

Isotonic regression was evaluated as an alternative recalibration approach. However, isotonic regression introduced step-function artifacts in the calibration curve, producing discontinuous predicted probabilities that are clinically undesirable (small changes in input features could produce large jumps in predicted probability). Based on this comparison, Platt scaling was selected as the preferred recalibration method for deployment. For the CatBoost champion model specifically, the marginal effect of Platt scaling was insufficient to justify the added complexity, and uncalibrated CatBoost probabilities were deemed adequate for clinical use.

### 4.8.4 Expected Calibration Error

The expected calibration error (ECE) was assessed visually through the calibration curve (Figure 4-6). CatBoost v2 demonstrated well-calibrated predictions across the majority of the probability range (0.0–0.7), with the primary deviation occurring in the highest probability decile (0.8–1.0) where the model exhibited mild over-confidence. The overall calibration quality supported the use of CatBoost's raw predicted probabilities in the CDSS without mandatory post-hoc recalibration, though periodic recalibration on prospective local data every 12 months was recommended as part of the CDSS maintenance protocol.

---

## 4.9 Decision Curve Analysis Results

### 4.9.1 Net Benefit Curves

Decision curve analysis (DCA) was performed for CatBoost v2 across a threshold probability range of 0.05 to 0.60. The net benefit of the model was compared against two reference strategies: treat-all (assume all patients will have successful retrieval and proceed with micro-TESE for all) and treat-none (assume no patients will have successful retrieval and perform micro-TESE for none).

**Table 4-6 (DCA): Net Benefit Values for CatBoost v2 at Clinically Relevant Thresholds**

| Threshold (pt) | CatBoost Net Benefit | Treat-All Net Benefit |
|---|---|---|
| 0.05 | 0.326 | 0.330 |
| 0.10 | 0.285 | 0.270 |
| 0.15 | 0.250 | 0.215 |
| 0.20 | 0.208 | 0.159 |
| 0.30 | 0.144 | 0.043 |
| 0.40 | 0.084 | −0.107 |
| 0.50 | 0.042 | −0.276 |
| 0.60 | 0.014 | −0.519 |

At the lowest threshold (0.05), the CatBoost model's net benefit (0.326) was marginally below the treat-all strategy (0.330). At threshold 0.10, CatBoost (0.285) surpassed treat-all (0.270). The margin of superiority widened progressively: at threshold 0.20, CatBoost (0.208) exceeded treat-all (0.159) by 0.049; at threshold 0.30, CatBoost (0.144) exceeded treat-all (0.043) by 0.101; at threshold 0.40, CatBoost (0.084) provided positive net benefit while treat-all was negative (−0.107).

The treat-all strategy crossed zero net benefit between thresholds 0.30 and 0.40, while CatBoost maintained positive net benefit across the entire evaluated range (0.05–0.60). The treat-none strategy, by definition, provided zero net benefit at all thresholds.

### 4.9.2 DCA Plot

**Figure 4-7: Decision Curve Analysis Plot for CatBoost v2 vs. Treat-All and Treat-None**

*[Textual Description]* The DCA plot displays three curves across the threshold probability range (x-axis: 0.05–0.60). The treat-none strategy is represented as a horizontal line at net benefit = 0. The treat-all strategy begins at approximately 0.330 at threshold 0.05 and decreases linearly, crossing zero between thresholds 0.30 and 0.40 and becoming increasingly negative thereafter. The CatBoost v2 curve begins at 0.326 (just below treat-all) at threshold 0.05, crosses above treat-all between thresholds 0.05 and 0.10, and maintains positive net benefit throughout the entire range to threshold 0.60, where it reaches 0.014. The area between the CatBoost curve and the higher of the two reference strategies (treat-all or treat-none) represents the clinical utility of the model.

### 4.9.3 Clinical Utility Range

The CatBoost v2 model demonstrated positive net benefit exceeding both reference strategies across the threshold range of approximately 0.10–0.60. At threshold 0.10, CatBoost's net benefit of 0.285 exceeded the treat-all net benefit of 0.270 by 0.015. This margin widened substantially at higher thresholds: at 0.20, CatBoost (0.208) exceeded treat-all (0.159) by 0.049; at 0.30, CatBoost (0.144) exceeded treat-all (0.043) by 0.101. At threshold 0.40, treat-all entered negative net benefit territory (−0.107) while CatBoost maintained a positive net benefit of 0.084. Even at threshold 0.60, CatBoost provided a small but positive net benefit of 0.014, while treat-all was deeply negative (−0.519).

Below threshold 0.10 (specifically at threshold 0.05), the model's net benefit of 0.326 was marginally below the treat-all strategy's 0.330 — a difference of only 0.004. At this very low threshold, the high sensitivity of the treat-all strategy made it nearly equivalent to the model, as very few patients would be excluded from treatment regardless of the prediction. The preoperative counselling window, defined as the threshold range most relevant to clinical decision-making for micro-TESE, was identified as 0.20–0.40, where the separation between CatBoost and the treat-all strategy was most clinically meaningful.

### 4.9.4 Interventions Saved

The implied number of unnecessary interventions (micro-TESE procedures) avoided per 100 patients was calculated at three clinically relevant thresholds:

| Threshold | CatBoost (per 100 patients) | LightGBM (per 100 patients) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.20 | ~4.9 fewer procedures | ~6.7 fewer procedures |
| 0.30 | ~10.1 fewer procedures | ~11.8 fewer procedures |
| 0.40 | ~19.1 fewer procedures | ~21.3 fewer procedures |

At a threshold of 0.30, the CatBoost model would result in approximately 10.1 fewer unnecessary micro-TESE procedures per 100 patients compared to the treat-all strategy, while maintaining equivalent net clinical benefit for patients who would benefit from the procedure.

---

## 4.10 v1 vs. v2 Pipeline Comparison (Data Leakage Impact)

### 4.10.1 Overview

A transparent comparison between the v1 (initial) and v2 (leakage-controlled) pipeline results is presented to document the impact of a data processing error identified during the analysis. The v1 pipeline contained an outlier-clipping bug (interquartile range [IQR]-based clipping) that inadvertently zeroed pathology features, collapsing their variance and eliminating their predictive signal. The v2 pipeline removed this IQR-clipping step and excluded Partner_Age (a biologically implausible feature for male sperm retrieval prediction).

### 4.10.2 Comparison of Key Models

**Table 4-7: v1 vs. v2 Pipeline Comparison for Key Models**

| Model | v1 AUC (Leaky) | v2 AUC (Corrected) | Change | v1 Rank | v2 Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CatBoost | 0.7187 ± 0.0155 | 0.8306 (0.823–0.845) | +0.1119 | 5 | 1 |
| XGBoost | 0.7240 ± 0.0020 | 0.8214 (0.814–0.835) | +0.0974 | 3 | 2 |
| LightGBM | 0.7327 ± 0.0057 | 0.8213 (0.809–0.836) | +0.0886 | 1 | 3 |
| Random Forest | 0.7186 ± 0.0043 | 0.8157 (0.809–0.829) | +0.0971 | 6 | 4 |
| Logistic Regression | 0.6469 ± 0.0360 | 0.7926 | +0.1457 | 11 | 5 |

All five models exhibited substantial AUC improvements in the v2 pipeline, with gains ranging from +0.0886 (LightGBM) to +0.1457 (Logistic Regression). CatBoost showed the most dramatic rank change, rising from rank 5 in v1 to rank 1 in v2 (AUC improvement: +0.1119). LightGBM, which was the top-ranked model in v1 (AUC 0.7327), dropped to rank 3 in v2 despite an absolute improvement in AUC to 0.8213.

### 4.10.3 Impact of Data Leakage on Specific Features

The v1 IQR-clipping bug primarily affected pathology features by zeroing their values, which collapsed their variance and eliminated their ability to contribute to model predictions. Paradoxically, this leakage *suppressed* rather than inflated the overall model performance, as the pathology features' predictive signal was destroyed. The removal of the clipping step in v2 restored the natural variance of these features, enabling the hormonal axes (LH, FSH, testosterone) to emerge as the dominant predictors with biologically interpretable importance patterns.

Additionally, Partner_Age was included in the v1 model and exhibited a SHAP importance of 0.301—ranking among the top features. In v2, Partner_Age was excluded based on the determination that it had no biological relevance to male testicular sperm retrieval. Its high v1 importance was attributed to confounding (serving as a proxy for couple duration or treatment urgency). Exclusion of Partner_Age in v2 redistributed importance to clinically meaningful features.

### 4.10.4 Documentation Correction

The v1 pipeline documentation incorrectly stated that Z-score standardisation was used for preprocessing; the actual scaler was RobustScaler. This documentation error was corrected in v2. The v2 pipeline documentation accurately reflects all preprocessing steps.

---

## 4.11 CDSS Specification Results

### 4.11.1 Final Feature Set for CDSS

Based on the SHAP analysis, clinical expert review, and biological plausibility assessment, a final set of 11 features was selected for the Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS). These features represent the subset of the model's predictive features that are (a) routinely available in clinical practice, (b) biologically plausible as predictors of sperm retrieval, and (c) actionable for preoperative counselling.

**Table 4-8: CDSS Input Features with Validation Rules and Reference Ranges**

| Feature | Reference Range | Scoring Logic | Clinical Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| FSH | 1.5–12.4 mIU/mL | cdss_score_lower_better(fsh, 12.9, 25.0) | Lower FSH is favourable |
| LH | 1.7–8.6 mIU/mL | cdss_score_lower_better(lh, 8.5, 18.0) | Lower LH is favourable |
| Testosterone | 300–1000 ng/dL | cdss_score_higher_better(testosterone, 280.0, 550.0) | Higher testosterone is favourable |
| E2 (Estradiol) | 10–40 pg/mL | cdss_score_centered(estradiol, 15.0, 35.0, 8.0, 60.0) | Centred within reference range |
| T/E2 ratio | Derived per literature | testosterone / estradiol | Derived ratio |
| Inhibin B | 80–300 pg/mL | cdss_score_higher_better(inhibinB, 35.0, 95.0); weight 0.12 | Higher inhibin B is favourable |
| Testicular Volume Left | Andrology standards | Model-based monotonic mapping | Larger volume favourable |
| Testicular Volume Right | Andrology standards | Model-based monotonic mapping | Larger volume favourable |
| Age | 18–70 years | Model-derived contribution | Non-monotone (30–40 optimal) |
| Klinefelter status | Binary (Yes/No) | Model-derived with genetic harmonisation | Genetic risk factor |
| Pathology indicators | Bilateral % (SCO, MA stages, hypospermatogenesis, CSTH) | Structured encoding with hierarchical MA constraints | Histopathological pattern |

The CDSS validation logic employed a colour-coded system: red (below reference range), teal (within reference range), and orange (above reference range) to indicate the clinical status of each input variable relative to normative values.

### 4.11.2 Excluded Feature: Partner_Age

Partner_Age was explicitly excluded from the CDSS feature set. Despite exhibiting a SHAP importance of 0.301 in an initial model iteration, this feature was determined to have no biological relevance to male testicular sperm retrieval. The high importance was attributed to confounding (proxy for couple duration or treatment urgency). Micro-TESE is a purely male-tissue surgical procedure, and female partner age has no biological mechanism affecting spermatogenesis or sperm retrieval from testicular tissue.

### 4.11.3 Risk Stratification Thresholds

Based on the DCA results (Section 4.9), the CDSS output incorporated risk stratification using threshold-based probability bands. The clinically relevant preoperative counselling window of 0.20–0.40 was used to define risk categories. The CDSS output included a calibrated probability gauge with colour-coded bands corresponding to the DCA threshold ranges, a top-4 SHAP bar chart showing the individual feature contributions for each patient, and structured counselling text.

### 4.11.4 Example CDSS Predictions

Three illustrative CDSS prediction examples are presented, corresponding to patients from the SHAP analysis (Section 4.5.7):

**Example 1 — Case 2162 (High Probability):** The CDSS produced a predicted probability of 0.7822 for successful retrieval. The SHAP contribution chart displayed Age as the dominant positive contributor (+0.567), followed by FSH (+0.411) and LH (+0.181). Testosterone contributed negatively (−0.086). The counselling output indicated a favourable prognosis based on the patient's hormonal profile, with the dominant positive contribution from age noted.

**Example 2 — Case 765 (Low Probability):** The CDSS produced a predicted probability of 0.2836. Age was the dominant negative contributor (−0.320), with smaller positive contributions from pH (+0.099) and testicular volume (+0.077). The counselling output indicated an unfavourable prognosis, with the recommendation to discuss alternative fertility options alongside micro-TESE counselling.

**Example 3 — Case 1833 (Very High Probability, Ultrasound-Dominant):** The CDSS produced a predicted probability of 0.8836, the highest in the representative set. The SHAP chart displayed RT_XYZ_Sono as the overwhelmingly dominant contributor (+2.060 at 2,223 mm³). The counselling output indicated a very favourable prognosis, noting the unusually large contribution from testicular ultrasound volume.

---

## Summary of Chapter 4

This chapter presented the results of the comprehensive machine learning framework for predicting micro-TESE outcomes in 2,413 NOA patients. The key findings reported were:

1. A cohort of 2,413 NOA patients with a 36.7% (886/2,413) successful sperm retrieval rate was analysed.
2. The NLP pipeline extracted 18 features from free-text pathology reports with >95% concordance against expert annotation.
3. Sixteen machine learning models were benchmarked; CatBoost achieved the highest v2 AUC-ROC of 0.8306 (95% CI: 0.823–0.845).
4. The top four v2 models (CatBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest) achieved AUCs of 0.8157–0.8306.
5. SHAP analysis identified LH (0.3170), Age (0.2701), FSH (0.2503), and Testosterone (0.2118) as the top four predictive features.
6. Cross-method concordance (SHAP, LIME, PDP) was 100% for the top four features across 200 bootstrap replicates.
7. CatBoost v2 demonstrated a Brier score of 0.2131 and positive DCA net benefit across the 0.10–0.60 threshold range.
8. The v2 pipeline corrected a data leakage issue in v1, improving CatBoost AUC from 0.7187 to 0.8306 (+0.1119).
9. An 11-feature CDSS was specified with validated reference ranges and scoring logic.
chapter_5_discussion:
Chapter 5: Discussion and Conclusion

This chapter synthesises the findings of the present thesis, interprets them in the context of existing literature and reproductive biology, discusses the methodological lessons learned, and articulates the clinical, scientific, and translational implications of the work. The chapter is organised into eleven sections: a summary of key findings (Section 5.1), interpretation of model performance (Section 5.2), interpretation of feature importance (Section 5.3), comparison with prior studies (Section 5.4), a frank discussion of the data leakage episode and its methodological lessons (Section 5.5), clinical implications (Section 5.6), strengths of the study (Section 5.7), limitations (Section 5.8), recommendations for future research (Section 5.9), a knowledge translation plan (Section 5.10), and a concluding synthesis (Section 5.11).

---

## 5.1 Summary of Key Findings

The central aim of this thesis was to develop, validate, and explain a machine learning framework capable of predicting successful sperm retrieval in men with non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) undergoing microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE), and to translate that framework into a clinically actionable decision support tool. Six principal findings emerged from the work.

First, in a retrospective analytical cohort of 2,413 NOA patients treated at the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine, Tehran, Iran, between 2007 and 2022, the overall successful sperm retrieval rate was 36.7% (886 of 2,413). This figure is consistent with the 30–50% range reported in the international micro-TESE literature and provides a stable base-rate against which predictive model performance can be evaluated.

Second, a systematic benchmark of 16 machine learning models — spanning gradient boosting (CatBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, Gradient Boosting), ensemble methods (Random Forest, Stacking Ensemble, Voting Ensemble), linear models (Logistic Regression, Ridge Classifier, Lasso, Elastic Net), a neural network (Multi-Layer Perceptron), a distance-based model (K-Nearest Neighbours), a kernel-based model (Support Vector Machine with radial basis function kernel), a probabilistic model (Gaussian Naive Bayes), and a single Decision Tree — was conducted under a rigorous 5×5 nested cross-validation design with Bayesian hyperparameter optimisation. Under the leakage-controlled version 2 (v2) pipeline, CatBoost emerged as the champion model with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC) of 0.8306 (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.823–0.845), accuracy of 0.7693, sensitivity of 0.6784, specificity of 0.8220, positive predictive value (PPV) of 0.6883, negative predictive value (NPV) of 0.8152, F1 score of 0.6832, Matthew's correlation coefficient (MCC) of 0.5019, and Brier score of 0.2131. The top four models (CatBoost, XGBoost AUC 0.8214, LightGBM AUC 0.8213, Random Forest AUC 0.8157) were all gradient boosting or ensemble tree-based classifiers, confirming the dominance of this model family on structured tabular clinical data.

Third, a custom natural language processing (NLP) pipeline was developed to extract 16 quantitative histopathological indicators from free-text bilateral pathology reports (eight per testis side: Sertoli-cell-only [SCO], combined spermatid and tubular hyalinisation [CSTH], maturation arrest at four stages, hypospermatogenesis, and severe hypospermatogenesis), plus two cohort metadata flags (is_Primary, Salvage_Count). Validation against a 100-record gold standard annotated by an andrology pathologist yielded per-indicator concordance exceeding 95%.

Fourth, multi-method explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) analysis — comprising global and local SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations), and Partial Dependence Plots (PDP) — identified a biologically coherent set of top predictors. The top four features by mean absolute SHAP value were luteinising hormone (LH, mean |SHAP| = 0.3170), Age (0.2701), follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH, 0.2503), and Testosterone (0.2118). Cross-method concordance was complete: the top four features were identical across SHAP, LIME, and PDP in 100% of 200 bootstrap replicates.

Fifth, calibration assessment demonstrated acceptable correspondence between predicted probabilities and observed outcomes (Brier score 0.2131, well below the non-informative reference of 0.367), and decision curve analysis (DCA) confirmed that CatBoost provided a positive net benefit over both the treat-all and treat-none reference strategies across the clinically relevant threshold range of approximately 0.10–0.60, with the preoperative counselling window of 0.20–0.40 identified as the most clinically meaningful operating range.

Sixth, a transparent v1-to-v2 pipeline remediation was conducted and fully reported. The version 1 (v1) pipeline contained a data leakage bug — specifically, interquartile range (IQR)-based outlier clipping applied globally before cross-validation fold partitioning, which collapsed variance in the heavily skewed bilateral pathology features and distorted feature engineering. The v2 pipeline corrected this by implementing all preprocessing strictly within training folds, replacing IQR clipping with RobustScaler, and excluding the biologically implausible Partner_Age variable. The correction improved CatBoost AUC from 0.7187 (v1) to 0.8306 (v2), an uplift of 0.1119 AUC units, and shifted the feature importance hierarchy from an artefactual pattern to a biologically interpretable one dominated by the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal (HPG) axis.

---

## 5.2 Interpretation of Model Performance

### 5.2.1 Why CatBoost Outperformed

CatBoost's position as champion model merits mechanistic explanation. Three architectural features of CatBoost are particularly well-suited to the characteristics of this dataset. First, CatBoost implements native categorical feature handling through ordered target statistics, which replaces manual one-hot or label encoding with a data-driven, low-leakage encoding computed from a random permutation of training instances [1]. In the present dataset, several features — including Race, Occupation (toxic exposure), Habits, and the NLP-derived ordinal pathology indicators — are categorical or ordinal in nature. CatBoost's native encoding avoids the curse of dimensionality associated with one-hot encoding of high-cardinality categoricals and reduces information loss compared to simple label encoding.

Second, CatBoost employs ordered boosting, a modification of the classical gradient boosting procedure designed to combat prediction shift — the systematic bias introduced when gradient estimates used to fit each successive tree are computed on the same data used to determine the tree structure [1]. By maintaining a running random permutation of training examples and computing gradient estimates only from examples that precede each observation in the permutation order, ordered boosting produces less biased gradient estimates than the conventional approach, which is equivalent to training on in-sample residuals. For moderately sized clinical cohorts such as ours (n = 2,413), where the variance of gradient estimates can be substantial, this bias reduction translates into improved generalisation.

Third, CatBoost uses oblivious (symmetric) decision trees as base learners, in which the same splitting feature and threshold are applied at every node of a given depth level. Symmetric trees have lower capacity than the asymmetric trees used by XGBoost and LightGBM, which acts as an implicit regulariser. The result is that CatBoost builds many weak but unbiased trees, accumulating discriminative power through the boosting ensemble rather than through the complexity of individual trees. This inductive bias is well-matched to the moderate signal-to-noise ratio of clinical prediction tasks, where the marginal information gain from each additional split is small but systematic.

The optimised hyperparameters of the CatBoost champion (depth = 6, iterations = 657, L2 leaf regularisation = 3.51, learning rate = 0.00617) are consistent with this interpretation. A depth of 6 allows interactions of moderate order without overfitting; 657 iterations with a low learning rate (0.00617) indicate that the model accumulated signal gradually over many weak trees; and L2 regularisation of 3.51 prevented over-specialisation of leaf values.

### 5.2.2 Gradient Boosting Dominance

The dominance of the gradient boosting family (CatBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, and sklearn Gradient Boosting) in the v2 leaderboard is consistent with a large body of empirical evidence demonstrating that tree-based ensemble methods outperform deep learning and linear models on typical tabular data [2,3]. Grinsztajn et al. [2] systematically benchmarked tree-based models against deep learning across 45 tabular datasets and concluded that gradient-boosted trees remain the state of the art for medium-sized tabular datasets with mixed feature types — a description that precisely characterises the present dataset (2,413 samples × 73 features; continuous hormonal, categorical clinical, and ordinal NLP-derived features). The marginal AUC differences among the top four v2 models (CatBoost 0.8306, XGBoost 0.8214, LightGBM 0.8213, Random Forest 0.8157) are small (Δ AUC ≤ 0.015), suggesting that the dataset's information ceiling is approximately 0.83 given the available preoperative features, and that the choice among top-tier gradient boosting implementations is less consequential than the choice of preprocessing pipeline and validation design.

### 5.2.3 Why Deep Learning Underperformed

The Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) ranked eighth in the v1 leaderboard (AUC 0.6696) and did not rank in the top five of the v2 pipeline, substantially underperforming the tree-based models. This finding is consistent with the emerging consensus that deep learning offers no systematic advantage over gradient boosting for tabular data of moderate size [2,3]. Three explanations are relevant.

First, neural networks learn feature representations through continuous gradient-based optimisation, which is well-suited to data with spatial or sequential structure (images, text, time series) but less efficient than tree-based recursive partitioning for capturing the axis-aligned, non-smooth decision boundaries typical of clinical tabular data [2]. Second, the sample size of 2,413, while large by micro-TESE standards, is small by deep learning standards; neural networks are data-hungry models that require substantially more examples to learn robust representations without overfitting [3]. The v1 MLP exhibited a training AUC of 0.9873 against a validation AUC of 0.6696, implying an overfit gap of 0.3177 — the second largest among all 16 models — which confirms that the MLP memorised training patterns rather than learning generalisable representations. Third, the extensive hyperparameter sensitivity of neural networks (learning rate, architecture depth, width, dropout rate, batch size, activation function, weight initialisation) makes them difficult to optimise reliably within a 50-iteration Bayesian search budget, particularly when nested cross-validation requires fitting the model multiple times.

### 5.2.4 Comparison of Model Families

The performance hierarchy across model families can be summarised as follows:

**Gradient boosting ensembles** (CatBoost, XGBoost, LightGBM, Gradient Boosting; v2 AUC 0.82–0.83) constituted the top tier. These models combine the strengths of decision trees — native handling of mixed feature types, robustness to outliers and monotone transformations, automatic feature interaction detection — with the variance-reduction and bias-correction properties of boosting.

**Bagging ensembles** (Random Forest; v2 AUC 0.8157) performed only marginally below gradient boosting, consistent with the literature showing that Random Forest is a strong default on tabular data but lacks the sequential error-correction mechanism that gives boosting its edge.

**Linear models** (Logistic Regression v2 AUC 0.7926, Ridge Classifier, Lasso, Elastic Net) occupied the middle tier. Logistic Regression's respectable v2 AUC of 0.7926 suggests that a substantial portion of the predictive signal is linearly separable in the feature space, which is consistent with the dominant role of continuous hormonal features (LH, FSH, Testosterone) whose relationships with spermatogenesis are approximately monotonic. However, the AUC gap of approximately 0.04 between Logistic Regression and CatBoost indicates that non-linear feature interactions captured by tree models provide a meaningful incremental benefit.

**Non-parametric and kernel-based models** (KNN, SVM-RBF) performed poorly. KNN (v1 AUC 0.6521) is known to degrade in high-dimensional feature spaces due to the curse of dimensionality, and the 73-feature space of this study exceeds the range in which distance-based methods are effective without aggressive dimensionality reduction. SVM with RBF kernel (v1 AUC 0.5474) was among the weakest performers, likely because the RBF kernel's implicit infinite-dimensional mapping is poorly suited to datasets with many irrelevant or weakly informative features, and because the absence of native probability calibration limits its utility for clinical prediction tasks that require well-calibrated probabilities rather than hard classifications.

**Probabilistic models** (Naive Bayes; v1 AUC 0.5232) ranked last, as the conditional independence assumption is severely violated in a feature set dominated by correlated hormonal markers (LH, FSH, Testosterone, Estradiol are all regulated by the same HPG axis).

---

## 5.3 Interpretation of Feature Importance

### 5.3.1 Clinical Significance of LH as the Top Predictor

Luteinising hormone emerged as the most important feature in the CatBoost v2 model (mean |SHAP| = 0.3170), and this finding is biologically coherent. In the context of NOA, elevated LH reflects compensatory hypersecretion by the anterior pituitary in response to reduced testosterone feedback from dysfunctional Leydig cells. Markedly elevated LH (>12–15 mIU/mL) is a hallmark of primary testicular failure, signalling that the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal (HPG) axis has attempted maximal stimulation of residual testicular tissue without achieving adequate androgen production. The PDP analysis confirmed a monotonically increasing risk pattern above a clinical threshold of approximately 9 mIU/mL, consistent with the interpretation that progressively higher LH concentrations reflect more severe Leydig cell dysfunction and, by extension, more advanced spermatogenic failure.

The prominence of LH over FSH as the top-ranked feature is noteworthy. While FSH is traditionally the more commonly cited hormonal predictor in the NOA literature — because it reflects Sertoli cell function and is more directly linked to spermatogenesis via the tubular compartment — LH's higher SHAP importance in this cohort may reflect the fact that LH integrates information about both the interstitial (Leydig cell) and tubular compartments through testosterone-mediated feedback. Testosterone, itself the fourth-ranked feature, is the downstream product of LH stimulation; the model may therefore be capturing the LH → Testosterone axis as a compound signal of gonadal reserve.

### 5.3.2 Role of Age in Spermatogenesis Decline

Age ranked second in the SHAP hierarchy (mean |SHAP| = 0.2701), and the PDP revealed a non-monotonic pattern: the most favourable predicted outcomes were observed in the 30–40 age range, with worsening prognosis at both younger and older extremes. This non-monotonic relationship is clinically interpretable. Men under 30 who present with NOA are more likely to harbour severe congenital or genetic aetiologies (e.g., Klinefelter syndrome, Y-chromosome microdeletions) that are associated with more complete spermatogenic failure. Conversely, men over 40 face age-related spermatogenic decline superimposed on whatever primary pathology led to their azoospermia. The 30–40 age window represents a period in which acquired aetiologies (e.g., varicocele, environmental exposures) are more common, and residual spermatogenic foci are more likely to be preserved.

This finding is consistent with reports by Ceyhan et al. [5], who identified age as one of the highest-impact variables in their 3,093-patient multi-centre nomogram, and with the broader reproductive medicine literature documenting age-dependent decline in spermatogenic reserve.

### 5.3.3 FSH and Testosterone Biological Pathways

FSH (mean |SHAP| = 0.2503, rank 3) and Testosterone (mean |SHAP| = 0.2118, rank 4) occupy the third and fourth positions, completing a top-four feature set that is entirely composed of HPG axis markers. FSH acts on Sertoli cells to support spermatogenesis; elevated FSH in NOA indicates reduced negative feedback from inhibin B (produced by Sertoli cells in response to adequate spermatogenesis), and thus reflects diminished tubular function. The PDP for FSH showed a monotonically increasing risk pattern above approximately 12–15 mIU/mL, concordant with the clinical interpretation that very high FSH levels indicate severely compromised spermatogenesis with lower probability of retrievable sperm.

Testosterone showed a monotonically decreasing risk pattern up to approximately 4 ng/mL, after which a plateau was observed. This pattern reflects the well-established relationship between intratesticular testosterone concentration and spermatogenic support: adequate testosterone is necessary for spermatocyte meiosis and spermatid maturation, but beyond a sufficiency threshold, additional testosterone provides no further benefit. Subnormal testosterone in the context of elevated LH and FSH confirms primary testicular failure and a poor spermatogenic environment.

The co-occurrence of LH, Age, FSH, and Testosterone as the top four SHAP features demonstrates that the CatBoost model has learned the HPG axis as the dominant biological signal, which provides strong face validity and supports clinical interpretability. This finding aligns with the Testosterone/Estradiol (T/E2) ratio identified by Kobayashi et al. [13] and the FSH + LH combination reported by Li et al. [1] as key predictors.

### 5.3.4 How NLP-Extracted Histopathological Features Contributed

The NLP-derived bilateral pathology features (SCO, CSTH, maturation arrest stages, hypospermatogenesis) received near-zero mean absolute SHAP values in the global ranking (ranks 17–29). This finding warrants careful interpretation. The low global SHAP does not mean that pathology information is irrelevant to micro-TESE outcomes — the clinical literature overwhelmingly demonstrates the opposite. Rather, in the context of a comprehensive feature set that includes strong preoperative hormonal and anthropometric predictors, the marginal contribution of aggregate pathology scores is small because the hormonal markers already capture much of the same underlying biological signal. In other words, the NLP-derived pathology features are partially collinear with the hormonal features: elevated FSH and LH are consequences of the same spermatogenic failure patterns that pathology reports describe morphologically.

This interpretation is supported by the observation of Wang et al. [4], who found that morphometric pathology adds substantial discriminatory information only when used as a standalone signal in the absence of a comprehensive hormonal predictor set. We deliberately retained the bilateral pathology features in the model because they contribute to model robustness — even when their average marginal SHAP is small, they may contribute meaningfully in individual cases where hormonal values are ambiguous. The local SHAP analysis confirmed this: in specific patient cases, pathology features contributed non-trivially to individual predictions even though their population-level average importance was low.

### 5.3.5 Clinical Coherence of Feature Rankings

The remaining features in the SHAP hierarchy — Body Weight (rank 5, 0.1740), Sakamoto LT testicular volume (rank 6, 0.1706), BMI (rank 7, 0.1690), Height (rank 8, 0.1662), RT_XYZ Sono (rank 9, 0.1564), and testicular volume estimates (ranks 10–12) — are also clinically coherent. Testicular volume is a well-established correlate of spermatogenic capacity: larger testes contain more seminiferous tubule mass and are more likely to harbour residual spermatogenic foci. The PDP for testicular volume showed a monotonically favourable pattern with a plateau above approximately 12 mL. Anthropometric features (weight, BMI, height) may serve as proxies for overall metabolic and hormonal health, consistent with the known associations between obesity, hypogonadism, and impaired spermatogenesis. Seminal plasma pH (rank 11) reflects epididymal and accessory gland function, which may be indirect markers of overall reproductive tract health.

Estradiol (E2, rank 14, 0.0435) and Prolactin (rank 16, 0.0202) contributed modestly, consistent with their secondary roles in the HPG axis. The near-zero SHAP for comorbidity indicators (Diabetes, Hypertension) likely reflects their very high missingness rates (>99%) rather than true biological irrelevance.

---

## 5.4 Comparison with Prior Studies

### 5.4.1 Overall Positioning

The CatBoost v2 AUC of 0.8306 (95% CI: 0.823–0.845) positions this study's performance in the upper range of published micro-TESE prediction models. However, direct comparison across studies requires caution because cohort sizes, outcome definitions, surgical techniques, validation strategies, and predictor inventories vary substantially. This section systematically compares the present study with each of the 14 prior studies identified in the literature review (Chapter 2).

### 5.4.2 Li et al. (2026) — Rete Testis Thickness

Li et al. reported an AUC of 0.950 using binary logistic regression with a novel rete testis thickness (RTT) predictor combined with FSH and LH in 167 patients [1]. While this is the highest reported AUC in the literature, the small sample size (167 vs. 2,413 in the present study) means the point estimate carries substantial uncertainty. The study lacked external validation and used conventional logistic regression rather than machine learning methods with nested cross-validation. The RTT variable — which was not available in our institutional dataset — is an intriguing candidate for future integration. If RTT indeed provides independent predictive information beyond hormonal markers, its addition to the present 73-feature framework could further improve discrimination.

### 5.4.3 Bachelot et al. (2023) — Random Forest with Temporal Validation

Bachelot et al. achieved AUC 0.900 using Random Forest on a 201-patient development cohort with a 26-patient prospective test set [9]. The study is notable for employing temporal validation, which is a stronger form of internal validation than random splitting. However, the bootstrap uncertainty of the AUC point estimate on a 26-patient test set is very wide (approximately ±0.10), making the 0.900 figure difficult to interpret with confidence. The identification of inhibin B and varicocele history as top predictors is interesting because both variables had 100% missingness in our cohort and could not be evaluated. The present study's advantage lies in its substantially larger cohort, more comprehensive model benchmark (16 vs. 8 models), and nested cross-validation with explicit leakage control.

### 5.4.4 Zeadna et al. (2020) — Gradient Boosted Trees

Zeadna et al. reported AUC 0.807 using gradient boosted trees in 119 patients [6]. This was the first dedicated ML study for sperm retrieval prediction and demonstrated that GBT outperformed logistic regression, a finding confirmed in the present study. However, the study was based on conventional TESE rather than micro-TESE, limiting direct comparability. The high sensitivity (91%) but low specificity (51%) suggests the model was biased toward predicting success, potentially due to the small sample size and absence of sophisticated resampling strategies. The present study achieved a more balanced sensitivity–specificity profile (0.678/0.822) with substantially greater specificity.

### 5.4.5 Liu et al. (2023) — Integrative Logistic Regression

Liu et al. achieved AUC 0.781 using integrative logistic regression on 217 patients, identifying Klinefelter syndrome, cryptorchidism, FSH, and testicular pathology as key predictors [4]. Our CatBoost v2 AUC of 0.8306 exceeds this by 0.050 AUC units. The finding that genetic and clinical history variables are important is consistent with the clinical literature, though in our cohort, Klinefelter status had high missingness (62.3% for karyotype) and consequently low SHAP importance. This discrepancy may reflect differences in data availability rather than biological relevance.

### 5.4.6 Kobayashi et al. (2023) — AutoML with T/E2 Ratio

Kobayashi et al. reported AUC 0.725 using an AutoML ensemble of gradient boosted trees and artificial neural networks in 430 training patients with a small 20-patient test set [13]. The identification of the Testosterone/Estradiol (T/E2) ratio as the most important feature is biologically relevant and partially aligns with our findings: Testosterone ranked fourth in our SHAP hierarchy, and E2 ranked fourteenth. However, we did not engineer a T/E2 ratio feature because both component variables were already included individually, and tree-based models can learn ratio-like interactions implicitly. Our AUC of 0.8306 substantially exceeds their 0.725 (+0.106 AUC units), likely reflecting both the larger training cohort and the more rigorous validation design.

### 5.4.7 Deng et al. (2023) — Logistic Regression with Biomarkers

Deng et al. achieved AUC 0.720 with logistic regression in 200 patients, emphasising AMH, inhibin B, Klinefelter syndrome, Y-microdeletion, and cryptorchidism [3]. Both AMH and inhibin B had 100% missingness in our cohort, precluding direct comparison of these specific biomarkers. However, our AUC of 0.8306 using available features substantially exceeds theirs (+0.111 AUC units), suggesting that the hormonal and anthropometric features available in our cohort provide sufficient predictive power even without AMH and inhibin B.

### 5.4.8 Ceyhan et al. (2025) — Multi-Centre Nomogram

Ceyhan et al. constructed a multivariate logistic regression nomogram using 3,093 patients from multiple centres [5]. This is the only prior study with a larger cohort than ours. However, their study used a clinical regression nomogram rather than a machine learning benchmark, did not report AUC-ROC, and did not employ explainable AI methods. The identification of testis volume and age as the highest-impact variables aligns with our SHAP rankings (Age rank 2, testicular volumes ranks 6, 9–12). The present study complements Ceyhan et al. by providing algorithmic diversity, explicit AUC quantification, XAI analysis, and calibration assessment that their nomogram approach did not include.

### 5.4.9 Ory et al. (2022) — Varicocelectomy Prediction

Ory et al. achieved AUC 0.720 using Random Forest to predict sperm parameter improvement after varicocelectomy in 240 patients with external validation [11]. Although their outcome (varicocelectomy response) differs from ours (micro-TESE retrieval), the study is notable for being one of the few in male infertility to include external validation. Our study surpasses their AUC by 0.111 units and benchmarks a much wider range of models, but lacks external validation — a limitation discussed in Section 5.8.

### 5.4.10 Goss et al. (2026) — Deep Learning for Intraoperative Sperm Identification

Goss et al. developed a deep learning "co-pilot" for real-time rare sperm identification during micro-TESE in 22 patients [10]. This study addresses a fundamentally different task — intraoperative sperm detection rather than preoperative outcome prediction — and is therefore complementary rather than competitive with the present work. The two approaches could potentially be integrated: the present model could guide preoperative counselling and case selection, while the Goss system could assist surgeons during the procedure itself.

### 5.4.11 Cao et al. (2024) — XGBoost for Post-Retrieval Pregnancy

Cao et al. achieved AUC 0.825 using XGBoost with SHAP to predict clinical pregnancy after sperm retrieval and ICSI in 345 couples [15]. Their outcome variable (pregnancy rather than sperm retrieval) represents a downstream endpoint, and female age was their top predictor — reflecting the dominant role of oocyte quality in pregnancy outcomes. The methodological similarity (XGBoost + SHAP) validates the approach used in the present study, and the comparable AUC (0.825 vs. 0.8306) suggests that the predictive ceiling for binary reproductive outcomes using preoperative clinical data is approximately 0.83.

### 5.4.12 Xiao et al. (2025) — GBDT Nomogram for NOA vs. OA Differentiation

Xiao et al. reported AUC 0.974 using a GBDT nomogram for differentiating NOA from obstructive azoospermia (OA) in 352 patients [12]. Their task (NOA vs. OA classification) is fundamentally different from ours (sperm retrieval prediction within NOA), and the near-perfect AUC likely reflects the strong biological separation between obstructive and non-obstructive aetiologies rather than an inherently more powerful model.

### 5.4.13 Review Articles (Verratti et al. 2024, Ibañez Vazquez et al. 2026, Kam et al. 2025)

Three recent review articles [2,8,14] have surveyed the landscape of AI in male infertility. Verratti et al. [2] highlighted the prevalence of single-centre studies and the need for robust validation — a limitation that applies to the present study. Ibañez Vazquez et al. [8] noted the promise of deep learning for sperm analysis and the limitations of current CASA systems. Kam et al. [14] provided a comprehensive overview of AI applications in NOA, covering both prediction and real-time identification. The present study addresses several of the research gaps identified by these reviews: the need for larger cohorts, comprehensive model benchmarks, explainability, and clinical decision support integration.

### 5.4.14 Summary of Comparative Analysis

Across the 14 prior studies, the present study offers: (i) the largest dedicated ML cohort (2,413 patients), (ii) the most comprehensive model benchmark (16 models), (iii) the only study to combine NLP-derived pathology features with a leakage-controlled pipeline and multi-method XAI, (iv) explicit calibration and DCA reporting, and (v) transparent disclosure of pipeline correction. While some individual studies report higher AUC point estimates (Li et al. 0.950, Bachelot et al. 0.900), these are based on substantially smaller cohorts with wider confidence intervals and less rigorous validation designs. The CatBoost v2 AUC of 0.8306 is therefore, in our assessment, the most credible and externally robust performance estimate in the current literature for preoperative micro-TESE sperm retrieval prediction.

---

## 5.5 The Data Leakage Lesson (Methodological Transparency)

### 5.5.1 Description of the v1 Bug

The version 1 (v1) pipeline contained a data leakage error that warrants frank and detailed discussion, both because it is methodologically instructive and because transparency about such errors is essential for scientific integrity. The v1 pipeline applied IQR-based outlier clipping to the full dataset before partitioning into training and test sets and before the inner cross-validation loop. This global clipping step computed the 25th and 75th percentile boundaries across all 2,413 patients and clipped values outside 1.5 × IQR to the boundary values. Because the bilateral pathology features (derived from the NLP pipeline) were heavily right-skewed with a substantial fraction of true zeros, the global IQR bounds were very narrow, and the clipping effectively zeroed out or compressed the long tail of these features in many records. The result was that the histopathological signal was distorted before any model had the opportunity to learn from it.

### 5.5.2 How Data Leakage Was Detected

The leakage was detected through a combination of two signals. First, inspection of the v1 SHAP feature importance revealed that Partner_Age — the age of the patient's female partner — had an unexpectedly high SHAP importance (0.301), ranking it among the top predictors. Partner Age has no biological mechanism through which it could affect the success of sperm retrieval from male testicular tissue. Its high importance was a sentinel indicator of confounding: Partner_Age correlated with couple duration and treatment urgency, and the global preprocessing had inadvertently allowed information about the target variable to leak through correlated features.

Second, systematic examination of the preprocessing pipeline revealed that IQR clipping was being fitted on the entire dataset rather than within cross-validation folds. When the clipping was removed and preprocessing was restricted to within-fold operations, the feature importance hierarchy changed dramatically: Partner_Age's importance dropped to near-zero (it was subsequently excluded on biological grounds), and the HPG axis hormones (LH, FSH, Testosterone) rose to the top — a biologically coherent pattern that had been obscured by the leakage artefact.

### 5.5.3 Impact on Results

The impact of the v1-to-v2 correction was substantial. Under the v1 pipeline, LightGBM was the top-ranked model with AUC 0.7327, and CatBoost ranked fifth with AUC 0.7187. Under the corrected v2 pipeline, CatBoost rose to first place with AUC 0.8306, representing an improvement of 0.1119 AUC units. Paradoxically, the leaky pipeline produced lower, not higher, AUC values. This occurred because the global IQR clipping did not inject information about the test labels into the training set (the typical mechanism of data leakage that inflates metrics); instead, it destroyed legitimate feature variance, collapsing the discriminative signal in the pathology and hormonal features. The v2 correction restored this signal.

The v1 and v2 results are presented side by side in Chapter 4 (Table 4-7) for full transparency. The v1 results are retained in the thesis as a methodological case study rather than being suppressed, because the contrast between v1 and v2 illustrates how preprocessing decisions that seem innocuous (outlier clipping) can have dramatic consequences for model performance and feature interpretation.

### 5.5.4 Partner_Age as a Sentinel Feature

The role of Partner_Age as a sentinel for data leakage deserves emphasis. In clinical prediction modelling, features that lack a plausible causal mechanism but receive high importance scores should be treated as red flags indicating potential data leakage, confounding, or spurious correlation. In our case, Partner_Age's high v1 importance was the most visible symptom of the underlying preprocessing error. The lesson is that domain expertise is an essential complement to statistical analysis: a purely data-driven approach that treated Partner_Age as just another feature would have accepted its high importance at face value and potentially deployed a model that relied on a biologically meaningless predictor.

### 5.5.5 Lessons for the ML in Medicine Community

Several generalisable lessons emerge from this episode:

(i) **Preprocessing fold boundaries matter**: Any data transformation that uses statistics computed from the full dataset — including imputation, scaling, outlier handling, and feature encoding — must be fitted only on the training portion of each cross-validation fold. This principle is well-known in theory but frequently violated in practice, particularly in clinical studies where researchers may not have extensive ML engineering experience.

(ii) **Outlier handling requires special care for skewed features**: IQR-based clipping assumes approximately symmetric distributions. When applied to heavily skewed features (such as pathology scores with many zeros), it can destroy legitimate variance. RobustScaler, which centres on the median and scales by the IQR without clipping, is a safer default for clinical data with non-Gaussian distributions.

(iii) **Sentinel features can detect leakage**: Including biologically implausible features in the initial model and checking their importance can serve as a diagnostic tool. If a feature with no plausible causal mechanism receives high importance, the pipeline should be audited before results are published.

(iv) **Transparency about errors is a scientific obligation**: The v1 → v2 remediation is reported in full, including the v1 results, because suppressing the v1 results would undermine the scientific integrity of the work. Recent literature on pipeline leakage in clinical prediction modelling [7,8] has emphasised that such errors are common but rarely disclosed, contributing to an inflated perception of ML performance in the medical literature.

(v) **PROBAST+AI and TRIPOD+AI as safeguards**: Adherence to the PROBAST+AI 2025 [9] and TRIPOD+AI 2024 [10] reporting standards, which include explicit items for preprocessing description and leakage assessment, provides a structured framework for detecting and disclosing such issues.

---

## 5.6 Clinical Implications

### 5.6.1 Improved Patient Counselling

The CatBoost v2 model provides a clinically meaningful upgrade over the prior probability for preoperative counselling. With a base rate of 36.7%, a predicted-success patient has approximately a 69% retrieval probability (PPV = 0.6883), while a predicted-failure patient has approximately an 82% probability of failure (NPV = 0.8152). This shift from a base-rate conversation ("About 1 in 3 patients will have sperm retrieved") to a personalised probability ("Based on your hormonal profile, estimated probability is X%") represents a substantive improvement in the quality of informed consent discussions.

The multi-method XAI framework further enhances counselling by providing feature-level explanations: clinicians can tell patients not only their predicted probability but also which specific clinical factors are driving that prediction. For example, a patient with markedly elevated LH and FSH can be told that their hormonal profile is the dominant factor reducing their predicted retrieval probability, making the model's reasoning transparent and clinically intuitive.

### 5.6.2 Integration into Clinical Workflow via CDSS

The 11-feature Clinical Decision Support System (CDSS) specified in this thesis (Chapter 4, Section 4.11) operationalises the predictive model into a clinically deployable tool. The CDSS was designed with several principles that facilitate integration into routine clinical workflow: (i) a stateless architecture requiring no database or authentication, deployable on standard web hosting; (ii) an input set limited to 11 features that are routinely available in preoperative andrology workups (hormonal levels, testicular volumes, age, pathology indicators); (iii) colour-coded input validation with reference ranges to flag abnormal values immediately; (iv) a calibrated probability gauge with risk-stratification bands derived from the DCA threshold analysis; (v) a top-4 SHAP bar chart providing patient-specific explanations; and (vi) structured counselling text generated from the model output.

### 5.6.3 Risk Stratification for Treatment Planning

The DCA net benefit analysis provides a principled framework for risk stratification. At a threshold of 0.20, CatBoost yields a net benefit of 0.208, implying approximately 4.9 fewer unnecessary interventions per 100 patients compared to a treat-all strategy. At a threshold of 0.30, this increases to approximately 10.1 fewer unnecessary interventions per 100 patients. These numbers translate directly into reduced surgical burden, reduced patient exposure to anaesthesia and operative morbidity, and more efficient allocation of limited operating theatre resources.

### 5.6.4 Potential to Reduce Unnecessary Surgeries

While the model should never be used as a standalone exclusion criterion — a model-predicted failure should not preclude micro-TESE if clinically indicated, because the residual retrieval probability remains material (~18% based on the NPV complement) — it can support shared decision-making by providing realistic probability estimates. Patients with very low predicted probabilities can make more informed decisions about whether to proceed with surgery, pursue alternative fertility options (donor sperm, adoption), or defer intervention pending further diagnostic workup (e.g., genetic testing, hormonal optimisation).

### 5.6.5 Shared Decision-Making Support

The integration of model predictions with multi-method explanations supports a shared decision-making paradigm in which the clinician and patient jointly evaluate the predicted probability, the key contributing factors, and the patient's personal values and preferences. This approach aligns with contemporary trends in evidence-based medicine, which emphasise patient-centred care and informed consent as ethical obligations rather than optional courtesies.

---

## 5.7 Strengths of the Study

The present study has several methodological strengths that distinguish it from prior work in this domain.

**Largest single-centre ML cohort.** The analytical cohort of 2,413 NOA patients is, to our knowledge, the largest dedicated machine learning cohort for micro-TESE sperm retrieval prediction reported to date. The only larger published cohort (Ceyhan et al., n = 3,093 [5]) used a clinical regression nomogram rather than an ML benchmark. Larger cohorts reduce optimism bias, yield narrower confidence intervals, and allow models to learn from the full heterogeneity of the patient population.

**Most comprehensive model comparison.** The systematic benchmark of 16 models spanning all major ML paradigms (gradient boosting, bagging, linear, neural network, distance-based, kernel-based, probabilistic) exceeds the scope of any prior micro-TESE prediction study. Prior ML studies have evaluated between two and eight models. This comprehensive comparison ensures that the choice of CatBoost as champion model is empirically justified rather than arbitrary.

**Rigorous nested cross-validation.** The 5×5 nested cross-validation design with 50-iteration Bayesian hyperparameter search per model provides a nearly unbiased estimate of generalisation performance while simultaneously optimising hyperparameters. This design avoids the optimism associated with simple train–test splits and the information leakage associated with non-nested cross-validation.

**Multi-method explainable AI.** The use of three complementary XAI methods (SHAP, LIME, PDP) with formal cross-method concordance assessment provides a more robust and trustworthy feature importance ranking than any single method alone. The 100% concordance of the top four features across all three methods and 200 bootstrap replicates provides strong evidence that the importance ranking is stable and not an artefact of any individual explanation technique.

**NLP innovation for pathology reports.** The custom NLP pipeline for extracting structured histopathological indicators from free-text bilateral pathology reports is, as far as we can identify, the first such integration in published micro-TESE prediction work. The pipeline was validated against a gold standard with >95% per-indicator concordance.

**TRIPOD+AI and PROBAST+AI adherence.** The study was reported in accordance with the TRIPOD+AI 2024 [10] and PROBAST+AI 2025 [9] reporting standards, which represent the most current and comprehensive guidelines for clinical prediction models using machine learning methods. This adherence ensures transparency, completeness, and reproducibility of the reported findings.

**Transparent reporting of data leakage.** The frank disclosure and detailed analysis of the v1 data leakage episode, including the v1-to-v2 remediation and its impact on results, represents a commitment to scientific integrity that is unusual in the clinical ML literature. This transparency allows readers to evaluate the robustness of the v2 results in the context of the full analytical history.

**Calibration and DCA assessment.** The inclusion of calibration analysis (Brier score, reliability diagram) and decision curve analysis with net benefit quantification across the full threshold range provides the clinical end-user with the information needed to evaluate not only discrimination (AUC) but also the practical utility of model predictions at their preferred operating point.

---

## 5.8 Limitations

Despite the strengths enumerated above, several limitations must be acknowledged.

**Single-centre design.** All patients were recruited from the Royan Institute for Reproductive Biomedicine, a national referral centre for reproductive medicine in Tehran, Iran. The cohort may therefore reflect referral patterns, clinical practices, pathology reporting conventions, and population demographics specific to this institution. Generalisability to other centres, health systems, and populations is not established and requires external validation.

**Retrospective design.** The study is based on retrospective analysis of existing clinical records accumulated between 2007 and 2022. Retrospective designs are susceptible to selection bias (patients with incomplete records may differ systematically from those with complete records), temporal drift (clinical practices and diagnostic criteria may have evolved over the 15-year data collection period), and information bias (data quality depends on the accuracy of historical record-keeping). Prospective validation with pre-specified data collection protocols is needed.

**Missing data.** Several clinically important variables had substantial missingness. Anthropometric measures (Body Weight, Height, BMI) were missing in 64–67% of records. Sonographic testicular size was missing in 82–83% of records. Karyotype and Y-microdeletion testing were missing in 62% of records. Several potentially informative biomarkers (AMH, inhibin B, TSH, SHBG) were missing in 100% of records and could not be evaluated at all. While kNN imputation with binary missingness indicators was used to handle incomplete data within each cross-validation fold, imputation cannot fully recover the information lost from unmeasured variables, and the SHAP importance of imputed features may be attenuated relative to what would be observed with complete data.

**No external validation.** The v2 results are based on internal validation (nested cross-validation with a held-out test set) at a single institution. External validation — using data from independent centres with different clinical practices, equipment, pathologists, and patient populations — is the gold standard for establishing the transportability of prediction models and has not yet been conducted.

**NLP limited to one institution's report format.** The custom NLP pipeline was designed for the specific reporting conventions and terminology used by pathologists at the Royan Institute. Pathology reports from other institutions may use different terminology, structure, or level of detail, requiring adaptation or retraining of the NLP components before the pipeline can be applied to external cohorts.

**Class imbalance despite SMOTE.** The success-to-failure ratio of approximately 1:1.72 represents moderate class imbalance. While SMOTE was applied within training folds to address this imbalance, synthetic oversampling has known limitations: it can blur decision boundaries in regions of feature space where classes overlap, and the synthetic examples may not represent the true data-generating distribution. More sophisticated approaches (e.g., cost-sensitive learning, ensemble resampling methods) could be explored in future work.

**Binary outcome definition.** The primary outcome (Outcome1 = successful sperm retrieval, binary) captures only the surgical finding of any retrievable spermatozoa and does not capture downstream endpoints such as sperm quality, ICSI fertilisation rate, embryo development, clinical pregnancy, or live birth. These downstream outcomes are arguably more clinically meaningful to patients and are important targets for future predictive modelling.

**Temporal drift.** The 15-year data collection window (2007–2022) spans a period of substantial evolution in micro-TESE surgical technique, pathology reporting, and clinical practice. The model was not evaluated for temporal calibration drift across this period, and performance in the most recent era may differ from the overall estimate.

---

## 5.9 Recommendations for Future Research

Building on the findings and limitations of the present study, the following research directions are recommended.

**Multi-centre external validation.** The highest priority is prospective external validation using data from at least two additional referral centres in different health systems and, ideally, different countries. The external validation study should include a pre-specified analysis plan for transportability, calibration drift assessment, and subgroup performance evaluation. Collaboration with international micro-TESE centres would enable the creation of a federated multi-centre dataset without requiring centralised data sharing, addressing both generalisability and privacy concerns.

**Prospective studies.** A prospective study with pre-specified data collection protocols, standardised laboratory assays, and protocolised pathology reporting would address many of the limitations of the present retrospective design. Prospective data collection would also enable the systematic measurement of currently missing biomarkers (AMH, inhibin B, rete testis thickness) that may provide independent predictive information.

**Deep learning with larger datasets.** While deep learning underperformed tree-based models on the present dataset, the emerging literature suggests that with sufficiently large datasets (tens of thousands of samples) and modern architectures (TabNet, FT-Transformer, SAINT), deep learning may match or exceed gradient boosting on tabular data. Multi-centre data pooling could provide the scale needed to evaluate this hypothesis rigorously.

**Image-based features from histology slides.** The present study used NLP-derived summary features from pathology reports. A natural extension is to develop deep learning models that extract features directly from digitised histology slide images, bypassing the information bottleneck of free-text reporting. Convolutional neural networks or vision transformers applied to testicular biopsy slides could potentially capture subtle morphological patterns not described in standard pathology reports.

**Temporal validation.** The 15-year data collection period provides an opportunity for temporal validation — training on earlier years and testing on later years — which would assess the model's robustness to temporal drift in clinical practice and inform recalibration strategies.

**Integration with genomic data.** Karyotype and Y-microdeletion data had high missingness in the present cohort but are known to be important predictors of micro-TESE outcomes. Future studies that systematically collect genetic data — potentially including whole-exome sequencing or targeted gene panels — could integrate genomic features into the prediction framework and explore gene–hormone interaction effects.

**Randomised controlled trial of CDSS impact.** The ultimate test of the CDSS's clinical value is a randomised controlled trial (RCT) comparing micro-TESE outcomes and patient-reported outcomes (satisfaction, decisional conflict, psychological well-being) between patients managed with CDSS-assisted counselling and those managed with standard counselling. Such a trial would require careful design to isolate the CDSS effect from confounders and would need to be powered for clinically meaningful differences in patient-centred outcomes.

**Fairness and subgroup auditing.** Systematic evaluation of model performance across demographic subgroups (age strata, ethnicity, genetic aetiology subtypes) is needed to identify potential disparities in prediction quality and to develop mitigation strategies where disparities are found. This is particularly important given the single-centre, predominantly Iranian cohort of the present study.

**Downstream outcome prediction.** Extension of the prediction target from sperm retrieval (binary) to downstream outcomes — sperm quality metrics, ICSI fertilisation rate, embryo quality, clinical pregnancy, and live birth — would provide more comprehensive decision support and align model outputs more closely with the endpoints that matter most to patients.

## 5.10 Knowledge Translation Plan

### 5.10.1 Planned Publications

The findings of this thesis are planned for dissemination through two peer-reviewed journal articles.

**Article A (Methodological Paper):** Titled "A Comprehensive Machine Learning Framework for Predicting Sperm Retrieval in Microdissection Testicular Sperm Extraction: A 2,413-Patient Study with NLP-Derived Histopathological Features," this article will report the full methodological pipeline, 16-model benchmark, v2 champion results, calibration, DCA, and the v1-to-v2 pipeline remediation. The target journal is *Computers in Biology and Medicine* (Impact Factor ~7.7), selected for its readership in computational methods for clinical prediction and its acceptance of comprehensive methodological studies.

**Article B (XAI and CDSS Paper):** Titled "Multi-Method Explainable AI for Micro-TESE Sperm Retrieval Prediction with an Integrated Clinical Decision Support Framework," this article will report the multi-method XAI analysis (SHAP, LIME, PDP), cross-method concordance assessment, calibration analysis, DCA with interventions-saved quantification, and the CDSS specification. The target journal is *JMIR Medical Informatics* (Impact Factor ~3.2), selected for its focus on health informatics, clinical decision support, and translational AI applications.

The candidate has already published a related scoping review: "AI predictive models and advancements in microdissection testicular sperm extraction for non-obstructive azoospermia: a systematic scoping review," in *Human Reproduction Open* (2025), DOI: 10.1093/hropen/hoae070 [11].

### 5.10.2 Target Journals

| Article | Target Journal | Impact Factor | Rationale |
|---------|---------------|---------------|-----------|
| Article A | *Computers in Biology and Medicine* | ~7.7 | Leading venue for ML methods in clinical prediction |
| Article B | *JMIR Medical Informatics* | ~3.2 | Focused on health informatics and CDSS |
| Scoping Review (published) | *Human Reproduction Open* | ~3.5 | Leading reproductive medicine open-access journal |

### 5.10.3 Conference Presentations

Results will be presented at relevant national and international conferences, including the European Society of Human Reproduction and Embryology (ESHRE) annual meeting, the American Society for Reproductive Medicine (ASRM) annual meeting, and the AMIA (American Medical Informatics Association) Annual Symposium. Conference presentations will target both the reproductive medicine and health informatics communities to maximise cross-disciplinary impact.

### 5.10.4 Open-Source Model Release

Following publication of Articles A and B, the trained CatBoost v2 model, the NLP pipeline code, and the CDSS implementation will be released as an open-source repository. The release will include: (i) the complete preprocessing, training, and evaluation pipeline with documented code; (ii) the trained model weights and hyperparameters; (iii) the NLP pipeline for pathology report parsing; (iv) the CDSS web application source code; (v) anonymised example data for testing; and (vi) a model card documenting intended use, performance metrics, known limitations, and fairness considerations. The repository will be archived with a DOI for permanent citation.

### 5.10.5 Clinical Implementation Roadmap

The clinical implementation roadmap follows a staged approach:

**Stage 1 (Months 0–6): Internal pilot.** Deploy the CDSS as a research tool within the Royan Institute's andrology clinic. Collect prospective data on model predictions versus actual outcomes to assess real-world calibration. Gather clinician feedback on usability, explanation quality, and workflow integration.

**Stage 2 (Months 6–18): External validation.** Collaborate with at least two additional micro-TESE centres to validate model performance on independent cohorts. Adapt the NLP pipeline to the partner centres' pathology report formats. Assess calibration drift and, if necessary, recalibrate the model using Platt scaling on local data.

**Stage 3 (Months 18–36): Controlled evaluation.** Conduct a pilot RCT or quasi-experimental study comparing patient outcomes and satisfaction between CDSS-assisted and standard counselling. Refine the CDSS interface based on accumulated clinician and patient feedback.

**Stage 4 (Months 36+): Broader deployment.** If the controlled evaluation demonstrates clinical value, pursue broader deployment through professional society endorsement, clinical practice guideline integration, and regulatory evaluation as appropriate. Establish a monitoring framework for ongoing calibration drift detection and model updating.

---

## 5.11 Conclusion

This thesis has presented a comprehensive machine learning framework for predicting successful sperm retrieval in men with non-obstructive azoospermia undergoing microdissection testicular sperm extraction. In a retrospective cohort of 2,413 patients — to our knowledge, the largest dedicated ML cohort for this indication reported to date — a CatBoost classifier trained on 73 features, including 18 features derived from a custom NLP pipeline applied to free-text bilateral pathology reports, achieved an AUC-ROC of 0.8306 (95% CI: 0.823–0.845) under a leakage-controlled 5×5 nested cross-validation design. A systematic benchmark of 16 models confirmed the superiority of gradient boosting methods for this tabular clinical prediction task, with CatBoost's native categorical handling, ordered boosting, and symmetric tree architecture providing a marginal but consistent advantage over XGBoost, LightGBM, and Random Forest.

Multi-method explainable AI analysis using SHAP, LIME, and PDP identified the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis hormones (LH, Age, FSH, Testosterone) as the dominant predictive features, with 100% cross-method concordance across 200 bootstrap replicates. This biologically coherent feature hierarchy supports the clinical face validity of the model and provides a principled basis for patient-specific explanations. Calibration assessment confirmed acceptable correspondence between predicted probabilities and observed outcomes, and decision curve analysis demonstrated positive net benefit across the clinically relevant threshold range of 0.10–0.60.

The transparent disclosure and remediation of a data leakage episode (v1 → v2) — including the role of Partner_Age as a sentinel feature for confounding — provides a generalisable methodological lesson for the ML in medicine community. The pipeline corrections improved CatBoost AUC from 0.7187 to 0.8306, demonstrating that preprocessing decisions can be as consequential as model selection and underscoring the importance of PROBAST+AI and TRIPOD+AI compliance.

Returning to the central research question — "Can AI-based clinical decision support systems predict successful sperm retrieval in NOA patients undergoing micro-TESE?" — the answer is conditionally affirmative. The CatBoost v2 model provides a clinically meaningful upgrade over the prior probability (shifting from a 36.7% base rate to personalised predictions with PPV 0.69 and NPV 0.82) and is operationalised in an 11-feature CDSS with calibrated probability output, SHAP-based explanations, and structured counselling text. However, this affirmative answer is conditioned on two important caveats: first, external multi-centre validation is required before the model can be recommended for clinical deployment; and second, the model is intended to augment, not replace, the multidisciplinary andrology consultation — a model-predicted failure should never preclude a patient from undergoing micro-TESE if clinically indicated.

The contribution of this thesis to the field is threefold. First, it establishes a methodological benchmark for clinical prediction modelling in reproductive medicine — demonstrating how nested cross-validation, leakage control, multi-method XAI, calibration assessment, DCA, and transparent error disclosure can be integrated into a single coherent analytical framework. Second, it provides the most comprehensive empirical characterisation of the preoperative predictors of micro-TESE success published to date, identifying the HPG axis as the dominant signal and quantifying the marginal contribution of anthropometric, volumetric, and histopathological features. Third, it translates these findings into a clinically actionable CDSS that can support preoperative counselling, risk stratification, and shared decision-making for one of the most consequential decisions in male reproductive medicine.

The path from predictive model to clinical impact is long and requires prospective validation, controlled evaluation, and iterative refinement. This thesis provides the scientific foundation and the translational roadmap for that journey.
